example: A! a paai na kabisa! (Wow! you have arrived early!) A, di gbahi ba! (Oh, don't arrest them!)
A yi chaŋ daa, zaŋmi ligiri na. (If you go to market, bring money.) A bia maa nyɛla ŋun mali haŋkali. (Your child is intelligent.)
Aa! a niŋ la lala? (Ah! you did that?) Aa! yɛlima n-nyɛ li. (Ah! it's true.)
O niŋ la aadalisi o fukumsi ni. (He acted with justice in his ruling.)
From: (Ha.) < (Ar.).  Aai, n bi bɔra. (No, I don't want it.) Aai ka di nyɛla yɛlimaŋli. (No, it's not true.)
O wuhi la aakai o nina zuɣu. (He showed disapproval on his face.)
From: (Twi).  Abaa, chɛli yɛltɔɣa maa! (Oh, stop that talk!)
From: (Ha.) < (Ar.).  O bi yɛn labina hali abada. (He will never return forever.) Lala ku niŋ hali abada. (That will never happen.)
From: (Ha.) < (Ar.). See: alibarika.  Abarika! a tuma viɛla. (Thanks! your work is good.)
From: (Twi). See: abaa.  Abba, chɛli yɛltɔɣa maa! (Oh, stop that talk!)
From: (Twi).  O ko la abe tihi o pɔŋ ni. (He planted palm trees in his garden.) Ti yɛn zaŋ la abe n maai aleji. (We will use palm nuts to make palm oil.)
Abe tia maa wɔla pam yuuni ŋɔ. (The oil palm has produced a lot this year.)
From: (Ha.).  Bihi maa bɔri la abinfuura daa ni. (The children want balloons in the market.)
Bihi maa daa lu abirima ka zɔ n chaŋ yiŋ. (The children panicked and ran home.)
O kɔhi la aboboi pia daa ni. (She sold ten plantains in the market.)
From: (Ha.).  M mini n abuya chaŋ la daa. (My female friend and I went to market.)
See: alichiyama.  Achiama dali, sokam yɛn yiɣisila. (On Judgement Day, everyone will rise.)
From: (Ha.) < (Ar.).  Achiika, ti dirila sheli niŋ sunsuuni. (Truly, we are eating together in the middle.)
From: (Ha.) < (Ar.).  Achiikatim, n bi mi o yɛtɔɣa maa. (Truly, I do not know his story.)
From: (Twi).  Achiri maa nyɛla toondana tiŋa ni. (The Achiri is a leader in the town.)
From: (Ha.) < (Ar.).  Alikali maa niŋla adaalisi ni o fukumsi puuni. (The judge acted with justice in his ruling.)
From: (Ha.) < (Ar.) < (Heb.).  Adaam kam malila chɛhurili ni o zo. (Every human has compassion for their friend.)
From: (Ha.).  Ti yɛn ti laɣimla ti adashi ligiri zuŋɔ. (We are going to collect our pledge money today.)
See: adashi.  Ti yɛn ti laɣim ti adaasi zuŋɔ. (We will collect our pledge fund today.)
From: (Port.) < (Latin).  Niŋmi a jɛma n niŋ adaka maa ni. (Put your tools in the box.)
See: adaka; -bila.  Bɛ zaŋla bɛ nuu n niŋ adakabila maa ni n-ti piibu. (They put their hands in the ballot-box to vote.)
O da kɔhila adata pam daa ni. (She sold a lot of wax print cloth in the market.)
From: (Ha.).  O kpiɣila o adiiku n chaŋ daa. (He carried his bundle and went to market.)
From: (Ha.) < (Ar.) < (Heb.).  Adiini kam malila di zalisi. (Every religion has its own rules.)
From: (Ha.). See: adiiku.  O kpiɣila o adiko n chaŋ daa. (He carried his bundle to market.)
From: (Ha.). See: adiiku.  O zaŋ o adiku n niŋ o zuɣu. (He put his bundle on his head.)
Adu maa nyɛla toondana tiŋa ni. (The Adu is a leader in the town.)
From: (Ha.).  O ko la adua o pɔŋ ni. (She planted pigeon-peas in her garden.)
From: (Ha.) < (Ar.).  O yiɣisi la asiba ni adua ni o ninsala. (He got up in the morning to say his personal prayers.)
From: (Ar.).  Afa maa karimla kuraani n wuhiri bihi. (The scholar reads the Quran teaching children.)
O zaŋla afi n-pigi o zabga. (She used a comb to part her hair.)
From: (Eng.).  Afirika tiŋgbana pam nyɛla ban mali tula. (Many African countries have heat.)
O dirila afirima pɔi ni o kuli. (She eats a snack before lunch.)
O nina ni mali afo ka bi tooi pahiri. (His eye has a swelling and cannot open.)
From: (Ha.).  O tuma maa niŋla afua o zaŋ chaŋ. (His work became easy for him.) O doro maa niŋ afua. (His illness eased off.)
Afulo! di yɛli lala yɛltɔɣa maa. (Heavens! don't say that kind of thing.)
Bɛ boli la Afum maa ni o kana nam ni. (They called the Afum to come to the chieftaincy.)
Di agarigi o ni o bia maa biɛhigu. (It concerns him about his child's behavior.) Di bi agarigi ma. (It doesn't concern me.)
From: (Ha.).  Agazieli yi paai, tula nyɛla din yɛn gbaai niriba. (When the hot season comes, fever will catch people.)
From: (Yoruba).  Bɛ daa ŋmɛ agba tobu ni. (They fired a cannon in the war.)
Ti nyɛla ti yɛn laɣim agbaa saha. (We are going to meet at noon.) Wuntaŋ’ niŋ ya agbaa. (The sun is at its zenith.)
Bɛ zaŋdila agbadudo n pahiri chɛri ni. (They use agbadudo leaves to add to soup.)
See: agba.  Agbahi maa ŋmɛbu wumiya katiŋa. (The firing of cannons was heard far away.)
From: (Eng.).  O malila agɛt mɔhli o nuu ni. (She has an agate stone in her hand.)
From: (Ha.).  O bɔri ago ka o tooi kpari li n-chaŋ salima ni. (He wants velvet cloth to wear to the festival.)
From: (Yoruba) via (Ha.).  Lo a agogo ka a mi saha. (Put on your watch so you know the time.) Agogo maa ŋmɛla buyi. (The clock struck two.)
From: (Ha.).  Agufo maa woligi la yɛltɔɣa maa n-ti ti. (The interpreter explained the matter to us.)
See: agufo.  Agufu maa woligi la yɛltɔɣa maa n-ti ti. (The interpreter explained the matter to us.)
From: (Ha.) < (Ar.).  Ŋɔ nyɛla n ahalali bia. (This is my very own child.)
Akabiya gbaai la o nubihi. (Scab disease has caught his fingers.)
Akai! di nyɛla binshɛɣu n-je. (Hey! that is something I don't like.)
Bɛ daa ŋmahi o akanza o bombili ni. (They carved his tribal marks on his cheek.)
Akarima maa ŋmɛri la tumpani. (The drummer is beating the talking drum.)
From: (Eng.).  Bɛ zaŋ la akasia dɔri mɛ duu maa. (They used acacia wood to build the room.)
From: (Ha.).  O kura la akoko n-kpari. (She prefers to wear akoko cloth.)
Bɛ zaŋ la o n-ti zali akpaaku ni. (They put him in the dock.)
See: kpaɣa.  Akpaɣila gbaagi o dama o yuuni kom. (Ague caught him because he drank cold water.)
Aku! a yi paai na. (Ho, ho! you have arrived.)
From: (Eng.).  Akunta maa karimla laɣ’ kalinli. (The accountant counts the money.)
See: aku.  Akutaku! ti nya li mi. (Ho, ho, ho! we have seen it.)
Ka ala ka a yɛn yo? (How much will you pay?) Ka niriba ala ka kana? (How many people came?)
From: (Ha.) < (Ar.).  O mali alaafee viɛnyɛliŋga. (He has good health.) Alaafee yili maa be la yaɣili. (The health centre is nearby.)
See: -lana.  Alaafeelana diri la bindirigu ka di nyɛri o nyɛma. (A healthy person eats food and it benefits him.)
Alaakali dali, bɛ bi chani gɔrim. (On the last Wednesday, they don't travel.)
O niŋ la alaali o ma kum zuɣu. (He cried out in distress for his mother's death.)
O be la alabalaayi puuni. (He is in distress.)
From: (Ha.).  Alachɛɣu maa yiɣiya n-du zuɣusaa. (The alacheyu bird flew up high.)
Ti ko la alafiɛ ti pɔŋ ni. (We planted pineapple in our garden.)
From: (Ha.) > (Ar.).  Bɛ kɔhiri la alaha daa ni zuŋɔ. (They are selling cheaply in the market today.)
Alaha biɛɣu n-nyɛ li. (It is a bad omen.) Alaha viɛlli n-nyɛ li. (It is a good omen.)
From: (Ha.) < (Ar.).  O zaŋ la alahali n-ŋma o buyu. (She used red yarn to weave her cloth.)
From: (Ha.) < (Ar.).  Ti yɛn chaŋ la salima Alahari dali. (We will go to the festival on Sunday.)
From: (Ha.) < (Ar.).  Bɛ niŋ ma alahaziba ni bɛ baŋsim. (Their knowledge amazed me.)
From: (Ha.) < (Ar.).  O tum alahichi ka bɔri gaafara. (He committed a sin and seeks forgiveness.)
See: -lana.  Alahichilana tu ni o taɣi o biɛhigu. (A sinner must change his behavior.)
See: lahisari.  Alahisari saha paai, ti yɛn puhi la jiŋli. (Prayer time has come, we will pray.)
From: (Ha.).  O nya alaka pam o tuma ni. (He has had much trouble in his work.) Bɛ niŋ o alaka. (They punished him.)
See: ala.  Ka alala ka a yɛn yo n-ti ŋɔ? (How much each will you pay for these?)
From: (Ha.).  Ŋɔ nyɛla anzinfa alali. (This is genuine silver.) O bia alali n-nyɛ ŋɔ. (This is his legitimate child.)
O niŋ alali ka bi tooi tuhi. (He showed cowardice and could not fight.)
See: -tali.  Di nyɛla din wuhiri o yɛda alalitali. (It shows the genuineness of his faith.)
From: (Ha.) < (Ar.).  Sagataa nyɛla alama viɛlli. (Rainbow is a good sign.) Alama biɛɣu n-nyɛ li. (It is a bad omen.)
From: (Ha.) < (Ar.).  Ti yɛn laɣim la Alamihi dali. (We will meet on Thursday.)
Alamyaalam! ti bi mi ni o yɛn kana. (No one knows! we don't know if he will come.)
O ŋmɛrila alangbɔ ka di wula. (He plays the alangbɔ beautifully.)
From: (Ha.) < (Ar.).  Alarabu dali n-nyɛ dabisili din doli Atalata. (Wednesday is the day that follows Tuesday.)
From: (Ha.).  Alasikaro, n ka liɣiri zuŋɔ. (Sorry, I have no money today.)
From: (Ha.) < (Ar.).  O daalɔbo n-nyɛ li. (It is his fate.) Alɔbo n-lu ti zuɣu. (Calamity has fallen on us.)
See: -lana.  O nyɛla alɔbulana dama o diri la buni. (He is a lucky person because he is prosperous.)
Di nyɛla alebo ka o niŋ maa. (It is a disgraceful act that he did.)
From: (Ha.).  O ko la aleefo o pɔŋ ni. (She planted spinach in her garden.)
From: (Eng.).  Aleepile maa yiɣiya n chaŋ zuɣusaa. (The airplane rose up into the sky.)
From: (Eng.).  Ti yɛn chaŋ la aleepile-paachi n-ti deei ti zo. (We will go to the airport to receive our friend.)
From: (Ha.).  Bihi maa bɔri la aleewa daa ni. (The children want candy in the market.)
O niŋ ma aleewa ka zo n chaŋ. (He played a trick on me and ran away.)
O zaŋ la aleji n-niŋ chɛri ni. (She put palm oil in the soup.)
See: haliluya.  O chaŋla Aleluyani jiŋli ni Alaaba dali kam. (He goes to Assemblies of God church every Wednesday.)
Alɛhi suŋ n-nyɛ li. (It is good fortune.) O mali alɛhi biɛɣu. (He has bad intentions.)
O mali la alɛhibiɛri o suhu ni. (He has evil intentions in his heart.)
See: -lana.  Alɛhibiɛrilana maa bɔrila o zo biɛrim. (The evil person seeks his friend's suffering.)
O labu na nyɛla alɛhitama. (His return is doubtful.)
From: (Grk.).  Alfa n-nyɛ tuuli nasara. (Alpha is the first letter.)
See: anfaani.  Tuma ŋɔ mali alfaani pam. (This work is very useful.)
From: (Ha.) < (Ar.).  O karimla Alfaatiha pɔi ni o adua. (He recites the Fatiha before his prayer.)
From: (Ha.) < (Ar.).  Naawuni niŋmi alibarika niŋ a ni. (God bless you.) Alibarika n-nyɛ daa nyaɣisim. (Bargaining brings happiness to the market.)
See: -lana.  O nyɛla alibarikalana dama o sɔŋdila niriba. (He is a blessed person because he helps people.)
From: (Ha.) < (Ar.).  O zaŋ la alibasa n-niŋ chɛri ni. (She put onions in the soup.)
From: (Ha.) < (Ar.).  O kparila alichɛbba n chaŋ jiŋli ni. (He wore a hooded cape to the mosque.)
From: (Ha.) < (Ar.).  O zaŋ la alichiidiri n-ti kom maa. (He used a bucket to fetch water.)
From: (Ha.) < (Ar.).  Alichiyama dali, sokam yɛn yiɣisila. (On Judgement Day, everyone will rise.)
From: (Ar.).  Alifuzaa maa yɛrila yɛltɔɣa ayi. (The hypocrite speaks two different things.)
From: (Ha.) < (Ar.).  Di viɛla aligaya. (It is exceptionally beautiful.)
See: aligaya.  Aligayade, o bi kana. (In spite of everything, he didn't come.)
From: (Ha.).  O niŋ ma aliguso liɣiri polo. (He defrauded me in money matters.)
See: aliguso.  O niŋdi aliguufu o daabilim ni. (He is dishonest in his business.)
From: (Ha.) < (Ar.).  O chaŋ la Makka n-ti leei alihaji. (He went to Mecca and became a pilgrim.)
From: (Ha.).  O niŋ ma aliheeri pam. (He showed me great kindness.)
From: (Ar.).  Bɛ daa ti o alihorima pam. (They gave him great honour.)
From: (Ha.) < (Ar.).  O ŋmɛrila alijeeta ka di wula. (He plays the alijeeta beautifully.)
From: (Ha.).  O zaŋ o naba n-niŋ alikaafi ni. (He put his foot in the stirrup.)
From: (Ha.) < (Ar.).  O zaŋ la alikalami n-sabi gbaŋ. (He used a pen to write a letter.)
From: (Ha.) < (Ar.).  Alikali maa ti la fukumsi. (The judge passed judgment.)
From: (Ha.) < (Ar.).  O ko la alikama o palo ni. (He planted wheat in his field.)
From: (Ha.) < (Ar.).  O lo la alikauli ni o yɛn yo liɣiri maa. (He made a promise to pay the money.)
From: (Eng.).  Alikɔta maa yiɣiya n gili tiŋa maa zuɣu. (The helicopter flew around the town.)
Alikilana maa zu la liɣiri maa. (The rogue stole the money.)
From: (Ha.).  O kparila alikudɔŋ n chaŋ jiŋli ni. (He wore a long cassock to the mosque.)
From: (Ha.) < (Ar.).  O karimla Alikuraan asiba kam. (He reads the Qur'an every morning.)
From: (Ha.).  Alimaʒiri maa bɔhindi la Afa sani. (The Koran student is learning from the scholar.)
From: (Eng.).  O diri la alimɔnd ka di mali alaafee. (He eats almonds and they are healthy.)
From: (Heb.).  Bɛ mɛ la duu ni alimug dɔri. (They built a room with almug wood.)
From: (Ha.).  O barila aliŋgurima viɛlli. (He rides a beautiful large horse.)
From: (Ha.) < (Ar.).  O niŋ la aliwalla pɔi ni o jiŋli. (He performed ablution before his prayer.)
From: (Ha.) < (Ar.).  Anabi Issa aliwumma nyɛla ban doli o soli. (The followers of Prophet Jesus are those who follow his path.)
Aliwuriti maa lahigili la niriba maa. (The comedian entertained the people.)
From: (Ha.) < (Ar.).  Ti diri la alizama viɛnyɛliŋga. (We are having a good conversation.)
From: (Ha.) > (Ar.) > (Heb.).  Alizanda nyɛla zaɣiviɛlli. (Heaven is a beautiful place.)
From: (Ha.) < (Ar.).  O zaŋ la liɣiri maa n-niŋ o aliziifo ni. (He put the money in his pocket.)
From: (Ha.) < (Ar.).  Ti yɛn chaŋ la jiŋli ni Alizimba dali. (We will go to the mosque on Friday.)
From: (Ha.) < (Ar.) + DB.  Alizimbiɛɣu maa muɣisiri o la. (The evil spirit is troubling him.)
From: (Ha.) > (Ar.).  O yɛri ni o nyala alizini. (He says he saw a spirit.) O alizini je o tuma maa. (He absolutely hates that work.)
From: (Eng.) < (Latin) < (Grk.) < (Heb.).  Bɛ zaŋ la aloosi n-ti o bindirigu ni. (They put aloes in his food.)
From: (Ha.).  O zaŋ la aluura n-niŋ o bunu ni. (She put reddish dye in her cloth.)
Bɛ mɛ la duu ni aluura dɔri. (They built a room with teak wood.)
From: (Ha.) < (Ar.).  N bɔra ni n chaŋ, amaa n ka saha. (I want to go, but I have no time.)
Amaachakoo! a tuma viɛla. (Thank you very much! your work is good.)
See: amaa.  Amaada, o bi mi li. (But actually, he didn't know it.)
From: (Ha.) < (Ar.).  Amaada nyɛla tiŋgbani tula. (The Sahara is a hot land.)
Bihi maa wala amaajiro. (The children are dancing amaajiro.)
From: (Ha.) < (Ar.).  O niŋ la liɣiri maa amaana n sani. (He entrusted the money to me.)
From: (Ha.).  Ti tu ni ti sɔŋ amaasachina. (We must help the weak.)
From: (Ha.).  O mini o amachia maa bi laɣindi. (He and his enemy don't associate.)
O zaŋ la amani n-niŋ chɛri ni. (She put dried fish in the soup.)
See: maŋa.  A maŋa n-nyɛ ŋun yɛn tum tuma maa. (You yourself will do the work.)
From: (Eng.).  Bɛ zaŋ la amaŋka n-ʒi bara maa. (They used a stretcher to carry the sick person.)
Amashera! a sɔŋ ya pam. (Thanks! you helped a lot.)
From: (Eng.) < (Grk.).  O malila amɛtisit mɔhli o nuu ni. (She has an amethyst stone in her hand.)
From: (Eng.) or (Ha.) or (Ar.) < (Heb.).  Sokam yɛli, "Ami!" (Everyone said, "Amen!")
Amii o yɛn kana? (I wonder if he will come?) Amii ti yɛn chaŋ daa? (Shall we go to market?)
From: (Ha.).  O lo la amiila o muɣri ni. (He wore a sword belt on his shoulder.)
From: (Ha.) < (Ar.).  O nyɛla n amiini. (He is my trusted friend.)
From: (Ha.) < (Ar.).  Bɛ lo la amiinsi ni bɛ yɛn tum tuma. (They made a secret agreement to work.)
From: (Ha.) < (Ar.).  Amiliya maa viɛla pam. (The bride is very beautiful.) Bɛ lo la amiliya asiba. (They had a wedding in the morning.)
See: amiliya; zo.  O zo n-nyɛ o amiliya zo. (Her friend is her bridesmaid.)
See: -nima.  Amiliyanima maa zaa kana. (All the wedding guests came.)
Paɣaba kparila amoosi. (Women wear a red loin-cloth.)
From: (Twi).  Bihi maa ŋmɛrila ampe. (The children are playing ampe.)
From: (Ha.) < (Eng.).  O ti la amsa viɛlli. (He gave a good answer.)
Amunkai maa nyɛla ŋun viɛli. (The young girl is beautiful.)
Anaago maa yila Lagos na. (The Yoruba person came from Lagos.)
From: (Ha.) < (Ar.) < (Heb).  Anabi maa wuhiri la niriba Naawuni yɛltɔɣa. (The prophet teaches people God's word.)
See: -tali.  O tɔɣisi la anabitali yɛltɔɣa. (He spoke a prophecy.)
O da la nyuya anahi. (She bought four yams.)
Bihi maa ŋmɛrila anamiinimiina. (The children are playing anamiinimiina.)
From: (Eng.).  O zaŋ la anchiti n-nyɛhi o nyɛri kom. (She used a handkerchief to wipe her tears.)
From: (Ha.) < (Ar.).  Tuma ŋɔ mali anfaani pam. (This work is very useful.) O nya anfaani di ni. (He got benefit from it.)
O ŋmahi la anfooni viɛlli. (She took a beautiful picture.) Colloc: ŋmahi anfooni. From: (Ha.) < (Ar.).
From: (Ha.).  Ango maa nyɛla ŋun mali suhupiɛlli. (The bridegroom is happy.)
See: ango; zo.  O zo n-nyɛ o ango zo. (His friend is his best man.)
O mali la anifu o suhu ni. (He has bad intentions in his heart.)
Anifubiɛri m-bali o. (He has evil intentions.)
See: -lana.  Anifubiɛrilana maa bɔrila niriba biɛrim. (The evil person seeks people's suffering.)
O naai tuma yuun' anii ni. (He finished work in eight years.)
From: (Ha.).  Di daa nyɛla aniini yini. (It was one tenth of a penny - obsolete.)
From: (Gonja).  Aniwula! a wula? (Good evening! how are you?)
From: (Eng.).  Bɛ zaŋ la ankufo n-gbahi o. (They put handcuffs on him.)
O mali ma ansarisi. (He is mocking me.) Di niŋdi ma ansarisi. (It makes me a ridicule.)
See: mali.  Ansarisimaana maa lahigiri la niriba. (The mocker entertains people by mocking.)
See: mali.  O ansarisimalibo maa bi viɛla. (His derision is not good.)
Anshituu! ka ti wum yɛltɔɣa maa. (Hush! so we hear the story.)
From: (Gonja).  Antire! a dirila bindirigu? (Good afternoon! are you eating?)
O ko la antirinya tihi pam. (He planted many cashew trees.)
Bɛ kana ni niriba anu. (Five people came.)
Tuma ŋɔ ka anuura. (This work has no value.)
From: (Eng.).  O zaŋ la gbaŋ maa n-niŋ anvuloku ni. (He put the paper in an envelope.)
O ko la anyangbana o palo ni. (She planted maize in her field.)
O di la anyɔ pam. (He did much evil.)
SynD: sangalinjo (W.D.).  O ko la anyɔɣiti o pɔŋ ni. (He planted anyɔɣiti yams in his farm.)
O mali anyibi o zo sani. (He is unfaithful to his friend.)
O niŋ la anyinsi ka bi tum tuma maa. (He showed laziness and didn't do the work.) Anyinsi soli bi viɛla. (The path of injustice is not good.)
From: (Ha.).  O mali anzansi pam. (He has great courage.) O zaŋ la anzansi n-ti tuma maa. (He showed endurance in the work.)
From: (Ha.) < (Ar.).  O malila anzinfa pam. (He has a lot of silver.) Anzinfa dan duu ku kari salima. (Silver entered the room first but won't drive away gold.)
See: anfooni.  O ŋmahi la aŋfooni viɛlli. (She took a beautiful picture.)
From: (Ha.).  O barila aŋgarima viɛlli. (He rides a beautiful large horse.)
See: ango.  Aŋgo maa nyɛla ŋun mali suhupiɛlli. (The bridegroom is happy.)
From: (Ha.).  O mali la aŋkadi ka tooi mɔŋ saɣim. (He has occult powers and can stop rain.)
From: (Eng.) < (Dutch).  Bɛ zaŋ la aŋkɔra n-ʒi kom maa. (They used a barrel to carry water.)
Aŋko viɛlli n-nyɛ li. (It is a good omen.) Aŋko biɛɣu n-nyɛ li. (It is a bad omen.)
From: (Eng.).  O diri la apil dabesi kam. (He eats an apple every day.)
Bɛ zaŋ la apirim n-tuhi tobu ni. (They used a machine-gun in the war.)
From: (Eng.).  O niŋ la apolasa asibiti ni. (He had an operation in the hospital.)
O yo la asadachi n-ti o paɣa. (He paid dowry for his wife.)
O niŋdi la asafi niriba sani. (He jokes with people.) Asafi kpɛma bi viɛla. (Rude joking is not good.)
Ti diri la asafihili viɛnyɛliŋga. (We are having a comical conversation.)
See: -lana.  Asafilana maa lahigili la niriba maa. (The comedian entertained the people.)
From: (Ha.) < (Ar.).  Bɛ puhila asalaati jiŋli ni. (They said salat in the mosque.)
Bɛ puhila asam Ramadan goli ni. (They say night prayers during Ramadan.)
Di daa mali asama. (It was cheap.) Di niŋ asama n-ti o. (It was easy for him.)
See: -lana.  Asansalana maa bi chɛri o tuma. (The steadfast person doesn't give up his work.)
From: (Ha.).  O nyɛla asanza nira. (He is a person of exemplary character.)
O zaŋ la asau n-gbahi zahim. (He used a dragnet to catch fish.)
O pun mi li ashaala. (He already knew it in reality.) Ashaala o bi kana. (In reality, he didn't come.)
From: (Ha.) > (Ar.).  O nya la ashaara pam o daabilim ni. (He had great loss in his business.) Ashaara doli alahichinima. (Misfortune follows sinners.)
From: (Ha.).  Ashee! o kpiya? (Really! has he died?) Ashee o pun mi ni. (In reality, he already knew.)
O kɔhila ashiakomo daa ni. (She sells bean-cake in the market.)
From: (Ha.) > (Ar.).  O sɔɣi la ashili o suhu ni. (He hid a secret in his heart.) Ashili bi veeni. (It's not good to reveal secrets.)
See: ashili.  O yɛli o li ashilɔni. (He told him secretly.) O chaŋ mi ashilɔni. (He went secretly.)
From: (Ha.) > (Ar.).  O yiɣirila asiba kam. (He gets up every morning.) Asiba n-nyɛ saha din viɛli tuma ni. (Morning is a good time for work.)
See: asiba.  O yiɣisi la asibaasi ŋaraŋara. (He got up very early in the morning.)
From: (Ha.) < (Ar.).  Ti vuhirila Asibiri dali. (We rest on Saturday.)
Asibirr! ka ti wum yɛltɔɣa maa. (Silence! so we hear the story.)
From: (Eng.).  O chaŋ la asibiti ni o ti nya alaafee. (He went to hospital to get health.)
O zaŋ la asooma n-niŋ o bunu ni. (She used asooma to dye her cloth yellow.)
From: (Twi).  Ata maa nyɛla ŋun mali haŋkali. (Ata is intelligent.)
Bɛ dɔɣi la bihi ata. (They gave birth to three children.)
Ataa dee, o daa kana yuŋ. (Truly, he came at night.) Ataa, lala n-nyɛ li. (Really, that's how it is.)
Ataade ka o nya ma, o bi puhi ma. (Even when he saw me, he didn't greet me.)
From: (Ha.) < (Ar.).  Ti yɛn laɣim la Atalata dali. (We will meet on Tuesday.)
O kɔhi la o atam daa ni. (He sold his farm produce in the market.) Palo maa mali atam. (The field has rich soil.)
O kparila atampa n chaŋ salima ni. (She wore wax print cloth to the festival.)
From: (Ha.) < (Ar.).  Tuma pilira Atani dali. (Work begins on Monday.)
O zaŋ la ataninmiɛɣu n-neei duu maa. (He used a lamp to light the room.)
From: (Twi).  Atawa maa nyɛla ŋun viɛli. (Atawa is beautiful.)
Atɔpusi maa wurindi la niriba suhuri. (The troublemaker is causing discord among people.)
From: (Twi).  Atuu! a paai na. (Welcome! you have arrived.)
From: (Ha.) < (Ar.) < (Heb.).  Bɛ karimla Atuura jiŋli ni. (They read the Torah in the synagogue.)
See: tutuɣu.  O zaŋ la atuʒiɛɣu n-ti o doro. (He used atuʒiɛɣu for his illness.)
From: (Eng.).  O ʒini mi hawa ata. (He sat for three hours.) Hawa pirigili n-nyɛ li. (It's half an hour.)
Awazakari biɛhigu bi viɛla adiini puuni. (Transvestite lifestyle is not good in religion.)
Bɛ kɔ la puu awɛi. (They farmed nine farms.)
O zaŋ la awule n-gbahi zahim. (He used a net to catch fish.)
From: (Twi).  Awuraba maa tumdi la banki ni. (The educated woman works in a bank.)
See: wurisi.  O kpe la awuriga daa ni. (He entered the confused market.)
From: (Ha.) < (Ar.).  O karim la aya pia Alikuraan ni. (He recited ten verses from the Qur'an.)
From: (Ha.).  O diri la ayaba asiba kam. (He eats plantain every morning.)
Ayayoo! a zu la liɣiri maa. (For shame! you stole the money.)
O mali laɣiri ayɔbu. (He has six cedis.)
Ti diri la Asibiri dabsa ayɔpɔin kam. (We eat every seven days.)
O niŋ la ayei o tuma maa zuɣu. (He expressed regret for his action.)
O mali bihi ayi. (He has two children.)  Niriba ayi n-kana. (Two people came.)
O zaŋ la ayigali n-ti o doro. (She used ayigali seeds for her illness.)
From: (Ha.) < (Ar.).  O niŋ ma ayiinsi. (He was dishonest with me.) Anyinsi soli bi viɛla. (The path of dishonesty is not good.)
See: ayi.  O mali la pihita-ayika. (He has twenty-eight.) Yuun' pishi-ayika m-paai. (Eighteen years have passed.)
Bɛ nɛma din nyɛ ayili: Salima mini anzinfa. (Their goods are as follows: gold and silver.)
Bɛ to la ayirmo salima ni. (They danced and chanted in commotion.) O zo la ayirmo. (He fled.)
Ayuɣuba maa tumdi la pɔŋ ni. (The transient worker is working on the farm.)
From: (Ha.) < (Ar.).  O nyari azaaba pam. (He is suffering much.) Azaaba buɣim nyɛla din yɛn ti niŋ. (Hell-fire will happen.)
From: (Ha.) < (Ar.).  O puhila azafari jiŋli. (He prayed the afternoon prayer.)
From: (Ha.) < (Ar.).  A daazali na bi kana. (Your time has not yet come.) Azali n-nyɛ li. (It is fate.)
From: (Ha.) > (Ar.).  O gbaai la azichi pam. (He has become very rich.) Tiŋgbani azichi mali anfaani. (Natural resources are useful.)
See: -lana.  Azichilana maa mali la liɣiri pam. (The wealthy person has much money.)
See: -tali.  O azichitali nyɛla din galisi. (His wealth is abundant.)
O mali la azua pam o yiŋ. (She has much food in her house.)
From: (Ha.) < (Ar.).  O chaŋ la aʒi Makka. (He went on pilgrimage to Mecca.)
O d'aʒia be n sani. (His belongings are with me.)
From: (Ar.).  Aʒia maa yi la Makka na. (The female pilgrim returned from Mecca.)
M ba nyɛla pukpara. (My father is a farmer.) M ba, nyam' a binyɛrigu. (Sir, here is your garment.) Etym: OV *TE ; OV *BA
N nya ba mi. (I saw them.) O boli ba na. (He called them.) Sim: bɛ 'subject'
Colloc: ba daaŋa 'perform rite to establish legitimacy'.  O paɣa ba la daaŋa. (His wife has done her first cooking for the household.)
O barila wahu n chaŋ daa. (He rode a horse to market.) M baya moto maa zuɣu. (I have mounted the motorcycle.)

english: to shackle, fetter; to peg; to secure
example: Bɛ ba o bandi. (They shackled him with leg irons.) Ti' shɛli din nyaɣa ba tiŋa. (A tree that is firmly rooted.) Serial: ba n-ʒe/zani 'stand firm'.
Baa maa ŋmɛri la vuri. (The dog is barking.) Naa baa gbaai naa kpaŋ, di pa taali. (The chief's dog catches the chief's fowl; it's not bad.)
Baa maa kom be ni. (There is water in the marsh.)
Colloc: tɔɣisi baabaa.  O tɔɣisirila baabaa. (He speaks haltingly.) Cpart: yabiyabi
O nyɛla baabaatu tobu ni. (He is a champion in war.)
From: (Ha.). Syn: fako.  O ka baabi. (He has nothing.)

english: not in the least; none at all
example: See: biɛla.  O daa bi biɛhim Naawuni daalikauli hal baabiɛla. (He didn't doubt God's promise one bit.) O ka bilikɔnsi baabiɛla. (He has no hypocrisy at all.)
Syn: ŋumaŋuma.  Baachali bi viɛla. (Gossipping is not good.)
From: (Ha.).  O nyɛla baadaaikaro, o bi lahi mi o ni tum shɛm. (He is insouciant, he doesn't care what he does.)
From: (Eng.).  O tumdi la baadee tuma. (He works by day.)

english: to lay across; to cross over
example: Serial: baai du.  O baai la mɔɣili maa. (He crossed the river.) Chɛ ka ti baai du n-chaŋ. (Let's cross over and go.)

english: to put up shoots; to take root; to grow
example: Tia maa baaiya. (The tree has put up shoots.) Di ba jila viɛnyɛla. (It is firmly rooted.) O che ka baai m-puhi vari. (It took root and shot leaves.)
Saa baaya. (The rain stopped.) Sarika duu maa bandi baai. (The prison fetters came undone.) Cpart: ba
From: (Eng.).  O zaŋ la baaji n ʒi kom. (He used a bucket to carry water.)
From: (Eng.).  Baaji maa ʒiri la binnyara kom ni. (The barge carries things on water.)
See: balɔɣu.  Baalɔɣu maa gbarigiya. (The male dog is fierce.)
Fom' baalim! (Be still!) O chaŋ baalim baalim. (He walked slowly.) Sim: balim balim
See: -tali.  O mali baalintali pam. (He has great meekness.)
See: bam.  O baam ʒɛya. (His smell is there.)
Bɛ wa la baamaya salima ni. (They danced the baamaya at the festival.)
O ko la baamiɣu o pɔŋ ni. (He planted baamiɣu yams in his farm.)
See: -tali.  Baantali nyɛla o tuma. (Praise-singing is his work.)
See: baŋa.  Baaŋa maa yiini la yila. (The singer is singing songs.)
From: (Eng.).  Sɔja maa be la baarichi ni. (The soldier is in the barracks.)
Colloc: baasali sua 'curved sword'.  O gbibi la baasali sua. (He held a curved sword.)
From: (Eng.). Syn: chɛchɛ.  O barila baasikuri. (He rides a bicycle.)
See: batɔha.  Baatɔha maa gbaai la waɣi. (The hunting dog caught a snake.)
From: (Eng.).  Baatiisinima mali la bɛ jiŋli. (The Baptists have their church.)
From: (Eng.).  Baatiri maa kom biɛla. (The battery acid is low.)
O nyɛla baawaala nira. (He is a gentle person.)
From: (Ha.) 'father'.  Baba maa ŋmɛri la nahu. (The deputy butcher is slaughtering the cow.)
Syn: baabi, fako.  O ka babiɛlifu. (He has absolutely nothing.)
See: baa; -bila.  Babila maa ŋmɛrila diɛma. (The puppy is playing.)
See: ba; bia; to.  O mini o babito laɣim la yiŋ. (He and his half-brother live together.)
From: (Eng.) 'a-b-c'.  Bihi maa bɔhindi la bachi. (The children are learning the alphabet.) Colloc: bachi bia 'letter of alphabet'
Badirikoro maa di la bindirigu pam. (The glutton ate a lot of food.)
Bɛ baɣa ka o ni. (He has no concern for them.) O baɣa ka o tuma ni. (He has no interest in his work.) Colloc: baɣa ka 'negligence'
Baɣa maa buɣirila yɛltɔɣa. (The soothsayer is divining matters.) Baɣa yi buɣi a, a buɣirila a toli. (If a diviner divines for you, you also divine for yourself.) Etym: OV *BO
Baɣabaɣa laɣim kɔbiga ku ŋmani paaŋa. (A hundred sand ants cannot be as big as a cricket.) [Prov. ]
From: (Ha.).  Bɛ zaŋ la bagalua dɔri mɛ gooni. (They used acacia wood to build the fence.)
Baɣayuli yi guhi ʒim je, di nyimsirila baɣayulilana. (If the god becomes angry waiting for blood sacrifice, it will warn its priest.) [Prov. ]
See: -lana.  Baɣayulilana maa mali la sara. (The traditional priest is making a sacrifice.)

english: to be next to, touch, lurk
example: O ʒimi baɣi ma. (He sat next to me.) O baɣila vari. (She is wearing leaves - menstruating.) Sim: miri
Syn: lɔhi.  Nahu maa baɣiya. (The cow is pregnant.)
O baɣi nanzua maa. (He fenced in his peppers.)
O chaŋ hal ka o naba baɣi. (He walked until his feet were tired.) Di baɣi ma. (It wearies me.)
Zahim maa ʒɛla baɣibaɣi. (The fish smells fishy.)
See: baɣa; bia.  O zaŋ la baɣibia n buɣira. (He uses divining beads to divine.)
Bɛ daa zaŋ baɣibuɣibo liɣiri chaŋ o sani. (They took divination fees to him.)
Syn: baɣa.  Baɣibuɣira maa buɣirila niriba taariha. (The soothsayer is divining people's histories.)
See: baɣa.  O zaŋ la baɣigbee n wuhi luɣushɛli. (He used his wand to point somewhere.)
O nya bua baɣili daa ni. (He saw a pregnant goat in the market.) [Prov. ]
Nahu maa mali la baɣili. (The cow is pregnant.) Colloc: mali baɣili.
See: ba.  Baɣilim ma ka ti chaŋ. (Give me a lift so we can go.)

english: to recline, lean, be next to
example: O zaŋ o nyuli baɣili buɣim ni. (He put his yam near the fire.) O ʒini baɣili o. (He sat next to him.)
Sim: yɛli.  O baɣili la chi maa. (She winnowed the millet.)
Baɣimpiŋ maa kuli la tia zuɣu. (The coucal is on top of the tree.) Baɣimpiŋ nini yi zaɣisira, di yɛn doomi. (If the coucal's eye itches, it will break open.) [Prov. ]
See: baɣiŋga; vɔɣu.  O zaŋ la baɣimvɔɣu n pahi chɛri ni. (She added bauhina leaf to the soup.)

From: (Ha.).  Bɛ zaŋdila baɣiŋga m pari bunsi. (They use fibre-plant to tie donkeys.) A yi tɔhi baɣiŋga mihi ni a lo firigima nyin' ka di yɛn wum. (If you strip fibres to tie yams you'll get tired.) [Prov. ]
O firi la baɣiri ni. (He sank in the mud.) O vooi ma yihi baɣiri ni. (He pulled me out of the mud.)
Colloc: buɣi baɣiri.  O buɣiri la baɣiri. (He practices divination.)
O baɣisi gari chaŋ o nyaaŋa. (He by-passed and went behind him.) Yisa daa ti baɣisiri Galili Teeku chana. (Jesus was walking beside the Sea of Galilee.)
O baɣisi la tia wala n ti o. (She ground tree fruits and gave to him.) Baɣisimiya tia wala na nti ʒe o bintɔrigu maa. (Grind tree fruits and put on his ulcer.)
See: baɣa; wulana.  Baɣisiwulana maa sɔŋdila baɣa maa. (The diviner's deputy assists the diviner.)
See: -tali.  Di ti bɔhimya baɣitali. (Do not practice divination.)
See: baɣa; yɛli.  O zaŋ la baɣiyɛlim n ti o ka o yɛli yɛlimaŋli. (He gave him truth-medicine so he would tell the truth.)
Tuma maa bahiya. (The work is finished.) O bahiya. (He has passed away.) Colloc: nyɛvili din ka bahigu 'eternal life'
O bahi la mia maa. (He stretched the rope.)
Bahimi saɣiri maa katiŋa. (Throw the rubbish away.) O bahi o tuma maa. (He lost his job.) O bahi la o saafi. (He lost his key.) Serial: zaŋ bahi 'dispose of', lɔbi bahi 'throw away'

Di bahibamdi bɛn nyɛ saamba la. (Especially those who are strangers.) Gaana tiŋgbani puuni niriba pam zaŋla shigaari nyubo, di bahi bahindi bipola la. (Many people in Ghana smoke, especially the youth.)
Colloc: bahi yɛmbahiga.  A ni ti bahi yɛmbahiga, dama tahima beni. (You will be secure, because there is hope.)
See: bahigu.  Bahiga maa dimi o. (The puff adder bit him.) Bia ŋun ŋmaai bihili ni ti zaŋ o nuu tim bahiga voli ni. (A weaned child will put his hand in an adder's burrow.)
O piligu ni hal ni o bahigu. (From his beginning to his end.) O bahi' suŋ n-nyɛ li. (It is a good ending.) Cpart: piligu
See: bahiga.  Bahigu maa ʒiya voli ni. (The puff adder is in the hole.)
Ti nya o ni yɛn bahindi shɛm. (We will see how it turns out.) Din bahindi nyaaŋa nyɛla... (What finally remains is...)
O zaŋ la bahima m-pili o duu. (He used loose straw to thatch his room.)
Syn: shɛje.  Bɛ booni o la bahiri dama bɛ bi mi o ba. (They call him a bastard because they don't know his father.)
See: bahi; yoli.  Bahiyoli ni paag' a yim libig' a. (Ruin will come on you suddenly.)
See: -lana.  Bahiyolilana maa biɛhigu bi viɛla. (The life of one destined to be lost is not good.)
See: -tali.  Bɛ bo la bahiyotali ti bɛmaŋa. (They brought ruin upon themselves.)
See: baa; kɔha.  Bakɔha maa kɔhiri la bahi daa ni. (The dog-seller sells dogs in the market.)
From: (Ha.).  Bakɔi kam puuni, ti vuhirila Asibiri dali. (Every week, we rest on Saturday.) Colloc: bakɔibakɔi chuɣu 'Festival of Weeks, Pentecost'
See: ba; kpɛma.  M bakpɛma ʒiya yiŋ. (My uncle (father's elder brother) is at home.) Sim: bapira 'father's junior brother'
Ŋuni n-lee bala? (Who is that?) O bahigu yɛn bala. (His end is upon him.) Sim: dini, bɔŋo
See: bali.  Bala! di labina yaha. (Go away and never come back!)

english: to be soft, thin, cheap, easy
example: Di daa bala. (It was cheap.) O tiba bala. (He hears very well.) Tam yi bali shɛli, saa m-mi. (If soil is soft somewhere, it has rained.)
Sim: bee.  O bi mi balante di ti nyɛ o yɛda ni. (He doesn't know, or doesn't believe it.)
O niŋ la balaŋa yuuni ŋɔ. (He reached puberty this year.) Colloc: niŋ balaŋa.
Bɛ ŋmɛri la balaŋkɔn n vɛndi kɔbbila. (They beat the slit-drum to scare birds from the farm.)
From: (Eng.).  O lo la balati o puli ni. (He wore a belt around his waist.)
See: baa; -lɔɣu.  Balɔɣu maa gbarigiya. (The male dog is fierce.)
Juɣu balɔrigu ka o be fɔŋ ni. (The vulture is ugly and lives in town.) Cpart: 'viɛlli
Balɛŋkan suli bi veeni o ka o baŋ ko' duɣira. (It is not good for the lizard to be angry with the one who can swim.) [Prov. ]
Colloc: wɔribali 'horse-riding', buŋbali 'donkey riding'.  Wɔribali mali asama n-gari buŋbali. (Horse riding is easier than donkey riding.)

english: to go away not to return
example: O baliya ka bi lahi labina. (He went away and never returned.) Naa baliya. (The chief has died - euphemism.)
Sim: laɣim.  O zaŋ o maŋ bali ti ni. (He associated himself with us.) M mi bi zaŋ mmaŋ' bali duŋtaariba ni. (I don't associate with rebels.)

english: to poison; to put on cosmetics
example: Colloc: bali chilo 'apply mascara'.  O bali la o nini chilo. (She applied mascara to her eyes.) Bɔzuɣu ka a bali chilo? (Why do you apply mascara?)
O bali la chi maa. (She removed the chaff from the millet.)
Sim: biribbirib.  O zo balibali n chaŋ. (He ran swiftly away.)
Dɔro balibu kam ni tooi gbahi nira. (Every kind of disease can catch a person.) Colloc: balibu balibu 'many kinds'
O nuu baliga. (His hand is slender.) Sim: dani, faai
SynD: bariga (W.D.).  O zaŋ la baliga vari n niŋ chɛri ni. (She used baliga leaves in the soup.)
Baliga maa gbaai la noo. (The weasel caught a fowl.)

english: to become soft, slacken, be tamed
example: Tandi maa baligiya saa nyaaŋa. (The ground became soft after rain.) O baligi o noli. (He spoke softly.) Nini baligi. (He was subdued.)
Sa baligu ni chib' tiŋa. (Little drops of rain will penetrate the ground.) O mal' anzinfa laɣ' baligu pia. (She has ten small silver coins.) Ant: karili
O balim la o sabbu maa. (He copied his writing.)
O balim ti ni ti chaŋ. (He persuaded us to go.) Bɛ balim o je. (They tried in vain to console him.) Colloc: nam balimbu 'paying courtesies to chief'
Sim: baalim, biɛla biɛla.  O chaŋ balim balim. (He walked slowly.) Fom' baalim! (Be still!)
Bɛ zaŋdila balima maa m bali tim. (They use the balima shrub to make medicine.)
Wummi n suhigu mini m balima ŋɔ. (Hear my prayer and entreaties.) A dima ka balima. (You can't negotiate with your enemy.)
Balimbiɛɣu maa ʒɛri la vi. (The stink-bug smells bad.)
Sim: zalinzahi.  Bɛ mali balimgbini m pari yiya. (They use balimgbini grass to thatch houses.)
Balimkpɛɣu maa dimi o yuŋ. (The bed bug bit him at night.)
O kparila balinaɣu n chaŋ salima ni. (He wore a long-sleeved gown to the festival.)
O zaŋ la balinchɔn n niŋ chɛri ni. (She put long pepper in the soup.)
Syn: balle.  Paɣa so kaa ta o bia yɛla, balintee Naawuni ku tam a yɛltɔɣa? (If a woman won't forget her child's affairs, how much more will God not forget your affairs?)
Balinyiri tia maa wɔla pam. (The centaurea tree fruits a lot.)

english: to canter; to mount on riding-beast
example: Yuri balisira. (Horses are cantering.) O zaŋ o balisi o buŋa. (He put him on his donkey.)
O balisi o nina ni. (He stared at him.) Sim: lihi, vihi
Syn: balintee.  N ka bua, balle nahu. (I don't have a goat, how much less a cow.) Bɛ bi mi ma, balle m mi m Ba Naawuni. (They don't know me, let alone know my Father God.)
Buɣim balli ni taai mɔri. (A gentle flame will set the bush afire.) Paɣa balli n-nyɛ o. (She is a slim woman.) Cpart: 'diɣinli, 'kpeeni
Dagbani balli n-nyɛ o ni yɛri shɛli. (Dagbani language is what he speaks.) O balli n-nyɛ Dagbamba. (His tribe is Dagomba.)
See: baa.  Baluli ni baa bi ŋmani ba' darigu. (A puppy from a litter is not like one from the market.) [Prov. ]
Ninkurugu gbini ku kɔŋ bam. (An old person's anus always has a scent.) [Prov. 0]
O nyu la bam ka buɣi. (He drank palm wine and got drunk.)

english: to praise-sing; to moan; to make excuse
example: Luŋa maa bamda. (The drummer chants.) O bamda ka di mali nambɔɣu. (His actions are pitiful.) Di pala yɛlimaŋli, o bamdimi. (It's not true, he's making excuse.)
See: bam.  Bama m-bala. (That's a sham excuse.)
Bambo maa bi baŋ shɛli. (The ignorant person knows nothing.)
Bambua maa zo n chaŋ mɔɣu ni. (The duiker ran into the bush.) O nanim' ŋmanila bambu' dibisi. (Her kings are like young antelopes.)
See: -dibiga.  Bambudibiga maa kuli la mɔɣu ni. (The young male duiker is in the bush.)
Gen: bambua.  Bambulaa maa nyɛla ŋun gbarigi. (The male duiker is fierce.)
Syn: zomkpabisɔŋ.  Bamkparima maa dimi o. (The poisonous snake bit him.)
Ban zaa nyɛla ban kana. (They all are the ones who came.) See: ba, bɛ, bana
Ban kana maa zaa ʒimi. (All those who came sat down.) Sim: bɛ, ba; ŋun (sg.)
Bana n-nyɛ ban tum tuma maa. (They are the ones who did the work.) Sim: bɛ (non-emph.)
SynD: baŋkalansilinli (W.D.).  Banakansilinli maa be la tia gbini. (The skink is under the tree.)
Gen: nɔŋa.  Banandɔɣu maa dimi o. (The large scorpion stung him.)
From: (Eng.).  O zaŋ o liɣiri n niŋ banchi ni. (He put his money in the bank.) Colloc: banchimanija 'bank-manager'
From: (Twi.).  O ko la banchi o pɔŋ ni. (She planted cassava in her garden.)
O gbibi la banchiriga o nuu ni. (He held a dagger in his hand.) Salinima kuriti kalana bandi, ka Naawuni pun kuri banchiriga. (People make fetters for the poor, but God has already made a dagger.)
Bandacheena goli maa puuni, ti yɛn puhi la chuɣu. (During the month of Bandacheena, we will celebrate a festival.)
Yɛlimaŋli ŋmanila bandɔba, tom laɣilaɣi. (Truth is like the bandɔba plant, very bitter.) [Prov. 00]
Bandɔɣu maa be la tia zuɣu. (The agama lizard is on the tree.) Bandɔɣu yi mali kɔbiri o zuli ni, bɛ bi boon' o jɛŋkuno. (Even if a lizard has hair on its tail, they don't call it a cat.) [Prov. ]
Bɛ ba o bandi. (They shackled him.) Sarika duu maa dunoya zaa yooi, ka sokam bandi baai. (The prison doors opened and everyone's fetters came undone.)
From: (Eng.).  Bandiri maa wɔɣila. (The boundary is long.)

example: O burisi li. (He crumbled it.) Yiya bihi mi lu m-burisiburisi. (Small houses fall shattered.)

See: bua; -saa.  Jɛsi daa bo busaa n-zaŋ pa buŋ' zuɣu. (Jesse loaded a donkey with a female kid.)
From: (Twi.).  Burinya chuɣu ni, ti laɣimla ti bihi. (At Christmas, we gather our children.)

See: bu-; shɛm.  Bɛ zaŋ soya balibu bushɛm n-tuhi nyaŋ ayodin pooli dɔriti. (They have many ways of overcoming iodine deficiency.) Siɣimsiɣim bushɛm ka o daa fa ba bahi. (So many times he rescued them.)
O piɛbila buusa. (He is blowing a whistle.)
See: buhili.  O diri la buusa. (He is eating boiled yam.)
See: bua; zɔŋ.  M bi bɔri yi buzɔna ni buhi. (I don't want goats from your folds.) Amaa m mali piɛ' shɛba ka bɛ ka buzɔŋ ŋɔ ni. (I have other sheep not in this fold.)
From: (English).  O be la ɔfis ni. (He is in the office.)
From: (English).  Ɔfisa maa kana tiŋa ŋɔ ni. (The officer came to this town.)
Jɛŋkuno yiɣi zani cha. (The cat jumped and stood straight/upright.)
O dɔro maa niŋ chaa. (His sickness is better.) Zama maa bela chaa. (The crowd is scanty.) Colloc: niŋ chaa.

english: to finish cropping; to exhaust essence; to be exhausted (soil)
example: Jɛrigu yɛliya ni mana chaaya. (The fool says the okro has finished fruiting.) [Prov. ] Tiŋgbaŋ' kuŋ din tam chaai la. (Dry land with exhausted soil.)

english: to improve after sickness, get better
example: Sim: afua, kpaŋ.  O doro maa chaai ya. (His sickness improved.)
From: (Eng.). Sim: chom.  Bɛ zaŋ la chaaji n niŋ o. (They brought a charge against him.)
From: (Eng.). Syn: samli.  Chaaji maa nyɛla din galisi. (The fare is high.)
From: (Eng.).  Bɛ chaaji o la o zo. (They accused his friend.)
From: (Eng.).  O chaaji ma la liɣiri pam. (He charged me a lot of money.)
O zaŋ la chaaka n ʒi bindira. (She used a string bag to carry food.)
From: (Eng.).  O yo la chaaki. (He paid by cheque.)
From: (Eng.).  Malimi chaati ka di wuhiri bihi wɔɣilim. (Make a chart to show the children's heights.)
Kom maa nyɛla chabachaba. (The water is lumpy.)
Yuŋ maa niŋ la chabachaba. (The night became dusky.) Colloc: niŋ chabachaba.
See: chɛfira.  O nyɛla chafira. (He is a pagan.)
Sim: chihi.  O mali chaɣachaɣa o nina ni. (He has a tattoo on his face.)
Sim: bɔbi, gbaai.  O chaɣi o paɣa kukoli. (He gripped his wife's throat.)
M napɔŋ chaɣibi. (I will never go there again.)
See: chaɣisa.  Situri shɛŋa bɛ ni mali wari waa ŋɔ n-nyɛ lɔm, chaɣila. (The costume for this dance includes whiskers and jingles.) Colloc: guŋgombila din mali chaɣisa 'tambourine'

english: to faint, pass out; to be in a coma
example: O chaɣili ya. (He fainted.) O be la chaɣili ni. (He is in a coma.)
See: chaɣisi.  O bipuɣiŋga n-daa gbibi chɛɣisa. (His daughter was holding jingles.) Paɣiba daa ŋmɛri biŋkumda ka chaɣisiri chaɣisanim'. (Women played instruments and shook rattles.)

english: to become weak, poor; to lose strength; to be ruined
example: Sim: chɔɣim, gbarigi.  Tiŋgbaŋ kulim daa chaɣisi. (The soil's fertility was exhausted.) Di bemi kaman tiŋgban' kuŋ din chaɣisi. (It is like dry land which is exhausted.)
O chaɣisi n-lu tiŋa. (He jumped down.)

english: to shake (bottle, rattle); to shake arm in triumph
example: Gaɣili din pali bi chaɣisira, gaɣ' sɔɣu n-chaɣisira. (Empty barrels make the most noise.) [Prov. 00] Bandɔɣu yi yi saazuɣu n-chaɣisi lu tiŋ' na... (When the lizard jumps down and shakes itself...)
See: golichaɣu.  Chaɣu maa lu ya. (The broken part fell.)

english: to cry in shrill voice, screech
example: Colloc: chahibu 'echo, shrill cry'.  O ni daa nya Yisa ka o chahi pam. (As soon as he saw Jesus, he shrieked.) Bɛ chahirimi, ka dimdi bɛ nyina. (They shriek and gnash their teeth.)

english: to tear, be torn; to tear sth.; to dig out
example: Yi niŋgbuna ni situra daa bi chahi. (The clothing of your body was not torn.) Bɛ daa bola bindira niŋ bɔto kura din chahi. (They put food in old sacks that were torn.) Sim: chihi
Chahinchɛɣu maa yiya. (The birth water came out.)
Whole: guŋgɔŋ.  Chahiriga maa chihiya. (The drum snare broke.)
O ko la chamba o pɔŋ ni. (He planted chamba yams in his farm.)
Syn: chichaɣili.  Chami maa yiɣiya. (The flying ant flew up.)
See: chaŋ; -lana.  Chamlana maa yɛli ti la yɛltɔɣa. (The go-between told us the matter.)
From: (Twi.).  Champɔŋ maa nyɛla toondana. (The Champɔŋ is a leader.) Sim: Achiri, Adu, Afum
O chaŋ la Champu ni n da. (He went to Yendi market to buy.)
See: chaŋ.  Bɛ malila chana viɛlli. (They have good formation.)
See: chaŋ.  O chaŋ o chandi. (He went for a walk.) A kari zom' n-kpɛhi kaɣa ni, a ni nya o chandi. (If you drive a blind person into stalks, you'll see how he walks.)

english: to walk; to go; to happen
grammar: Pl: chama, chanya, chanimi, chana, chandi
example: O chaŋ la daa. (He went to market.) Di chaŋ wula? (How come?) To, cham' a chandi! (Right, off you go!) Etym: OV *TO ; OV *CEN
Gen: doro.  Chaŋkpandi gbaagi o. (Smallpox caught him.)
Colloc: zaa chap.  Bɛ zaa chap kana. (They all completely came.)
See: bu-; ayi.  N lɔri nol' la buyi dakulo kam. (I fast twice every week.) A daa pirigi teeku buyi. (You divided the sea in two.) Colloc: buyi zuɣu 'in two groups'

english: to lose flesh; to lose strength, become weak
example: Sim: chaɣisi, gbarigi. Cpart: kpɛma.  O chɔɣim ya. (He lost strength.)
O tum tuma maa chɔɣimata. (He did the work badly.) O ŋme wɔɣirili maa luhi chɔɣimata. (He easily knocked down the giant.)
See: 'chɔɣiŋgu.  O nyɛla chɔɣifu. (He is useless.)
Syn: chɔɣinsi. Cpart: yaa.  Kom yi dir' a, mɔ' chɔɣiŋgu kam timdila a nin' ni. (A drowning man clutches at any straw.) [Prov. ]
Doo ŋɔ simdila kubu, o ni tɔɣisiri yɛtɔɣ' shɛŋa n-chɔɣisiri niriba zaa kɔba ni. (This man deserves death for discouraging everyone.)
O chɔhi la o. (He choked him.)
Sim: chibi, kuhi.  O chɔhi li la voli. (He pierced a hole in it.)
See: chɔriŋmɛra.  O ti o la chɔri. (He gave him a blow.)
Syn: chɔɣiŋgu. Cpart: yaa.  N daa be yi sani mi ni chɔɣinsi. (I was with you in weakness.) Yaa nyaaŋa nyɛla chɔɣinsi. (After strength comes weakness.)
Binyɛra maa be la chɔribɔi. (The things are jumbled.)
See: chɔri; ŋmɛ.  M mi bi be kaman chɔriŋmɛra ŋun ŋmɛri pɔhim. (I am not like a boxer hitting the air.)

english: to cut, chop; to harvest millet; to peck
example: O che la mɔri. (He cut grass.) Ti Duuma, ti cham' ba takɔbiri? (Our Lord, should we cut them with swords?) Noonsi bi birita waamaa n-chɛra. (Birds don't sow or reap.) Etym: OV *CE

english: to leave alone, behind; to stop; to let, allow; to cause
grammar: Pl: chɛla, chɛri, chami, chɛliya, chɛya
example: O chɛ o kochuɣu zali nimaani. (She left her waterpot standing there.) Satahinsi maa daa chɛ. (The thunder stopped.)
O toori taŋkpagu chɛr' o. (He showered him with dirt.)
Ko' chee kani. (There is not a drop of water.) Bɛ daa laɣisi bɔrobɔro chɛhi mini zimburisa. (They collected bread fragments and fish scraps.)
Kpaŋ yi niŋ chee, kpaŋ baŋdi li. (If one guinea fowl shouts "chee", another understands it.) [Prov. ]
Chee maa be la tia zuɣu. (The parrot is on the tree.)
See: napɔnchee.  O napɔnchee biɛri o. (His instep hurts.)
Gen: wahu.  O barila chee. (He rides a roan horse.)
From: (Eng.). Syn: tooji.  N daa sabi gbaŋ ti cheechi na. (I wrote a letter to the church.) Pool daa piligila chɛɛchi nimaani. (Paul founded a church there.)
Sim: che.  Noo cheerila o bihi niŋdi o jɔɣu ni. (The hen pecks at its young to put them under its wings.) [Prov. 0]

english: to release water, break dam; to burst
example: Zaba piligu ŋmanila bɛ ni yi cheei kom bahi la. (Starting a quarrel is like breaching a dam.) Colloc: X baɣiri cheei kom 'X becomes disheartened'

Sim: chɛhi, tɔhi.  O cheei omaŋmaŋ kpalibu. (He tore his own robe.) A yi da ayodin yɛlim na, nyin cheemi di lɔba maa. (When you buy iodised salt, tear its plastic bag.) Chinchin' shɛli din daa yili Naawuni jɛmbu duu sunsuuni la daa cheei. (The temple curtain tore.) Colloc: bɔɣiri chee kom 'to despair'
From: (Eng.).  O cheeni maa chihiya. (His bicycle chain broke.)
See: che; paŋ.  O bo la che-m-paŋ. (He sought forgiveness.)
See: chɛbili.  O zaŋ chɛba n ŋmɛ buɣim. (He used flints to make fire.)
See: che.  A yi nya a chɛba a dirimi. (If you get leftovers, you eat them.) [Prov. ]
See: chɛbili.  Chɛba puhi o niŋgbuna ni. (Pustules swelled on his body.)
O duɣi la chɛballi. (She made a large pancake.)
Syn: chɛribo.  O zaŋ chɛbili n ŋmɛ buɣim. (He used a flint to strike fire.) Colloc: X ka chɛbili 'X has no means of support'
Chɛbili puhi o nina ni. (A pustule swelled on his face.)
Syn: galikuli.  O zaŋ o naba n niŋ chɛbili ni. (He put his foot in the stirrup.)
O lo la chɛbisa o napɔŋ ni. (She wore anklets with bells on her leg.)

english: to take leave, bid farewell; to dismiss; to give parting present
example: Ti chɛbisi taba. (We took leave of each other.) O daa chɛbisi salo maa. (He dismissed the crowd.) O bi chɛbisi ma. (He didn't give me anything.)
From: (Ha.). Syn: baasikuri.  O barila chɛche. (He rides a bicycle.) Dinzuɣu di tuya ka yi bɔ cheche n kpuɣ' o kuna yiŋa. (So you must get a bicycle to take him home.)
Syn: motoo.  O barila chɛchɛbuɣim. (He rides a motorbike.)
See: chɛchɛbuŋ; ba. Syn: chɛchɛbunduhira.  O ni ti zaŋ yi bidibisi niŋ chɛchɛbumbariba. (He will take your sons and make them charioteers.)
See: chɛchɛbuŋ; duhi. Syn: chɛchɛbumbara.  Nyini ka n zaŋ ŋme wurim chɛchɛbuŋa, ka ku chɛchɛbunduhira. (I will use you to destroy the chariot and kill the charioteer.)
O zaŋ sagbana leeg' a chɛchɛbuŋa. (He uses the clouds as his chariot.) Ka bɛ chɛchɛbunsi daa firi bayaɣiti ni. (Their chariots sank in the mud.)
See: chɛrisi.  O noo maa nyɛla chɛchɛrigu. (His fowl is of mixed colours.)
O ko la chɛchito o pɔŋ ni. (He planted chɛchito yams.)
From: (Ha.) < (Ar.).  O nyɛla chɛfira. (He is a pagan.) Nyin' lihim' o kaman chɛfira. (Look on him as a pagan.)
See: -tali.  Ka daa chɛ o chɛfiritali soli. (He left his pagan way.) Chɛfiritali biɛhigu zaɣibiɛtali yɛla. (The evils of the pagan life.)
See: chɛfira.  O nyɛla chɛfura. (He is a pagan.)
O chɛgbɛ ti la. (He saluted us.)
See: chaɣila.  O gbibi la chɛɣisa. (He holds jingles.)
See: chaɣisi.  O chɛɣisi la gaɣili maa. (He shook the gourd.)
Zaɣa chɛɣu n-nyɛ li. (It is a torn one.) A kpee yi ku o noo ŋmaai ti a, a gba noo gɔrila nogbal' chɛɣu. (If your neighbour kills his fowl and gives you some, your fowl walks on one leg.) [Prov. ]
See: chɛhi; chɛrili; chee; cheei.  Chɛɣu maa lu ya. (The broken piece fell.)
See: che.  O chɛhi la pam. (He cried out shrilly.)
Colloc: chɛhi nyu'kuma. Sim: che.  O chɛhi la nyuya. (He harvested yams.)
Di chɛhiya. (It has torn.) Bɛ lansi maa ti pili chɛhibu. (Their nets began to tear.) Bi baŋ shibu mini bi baŋ gbaabu n-chɛ ka gbaŋ chɛhira. (Can't Flay and Can't Hold let the hide get torn.) [Prov. ]
O diri la chɛkparawa. (He is eating thick porridge.)
See: che.  Chɛla! di niŋ lala. (Leave it! don't do that.)
Colloc: chaŋ chɛlachɛla.  O chaŋ chɛlachɛla. (He walked staggeringly.)

english: to scout, observe; to go after
example: O chɛli la o zo maa. (He observed his friend.) O chɛli o nyaaŋa. (He went after him.)
See: chami.  Chɛmi maa yiɣiya. (The flying ants flew up.)
From: (Ha.). Colloc: nyaɣisi chɛmi.  Yɛndan' yɛtɔɣa ŋmanila wahu chɛmi. (The speech of the wise is like horse-spurs.)
See: chaŋ; -lana.  Chɛmlana maa chaŋ ti bɔhi paɣa maa. (The matchmaker went to ask for the woman.)
Tiŋkpaŋ' bandɔɣu bi simdi ka o baŋ chɛmsi. (A village lizard shouldn't know about zinc roofs.) [Prov. ]
See: chaŋ.  O chaŋ o chɛndi. (He went on his way.)
From: (Eng.). Syn: laɣibaligu.  O ti ma la chɛnji. (He gave me change.)
Colloc: gam chɔri.  Di gam la chɔri. (It is more than necessary.)

english: flint; lock of flintlock gun, trigger
example: Syn: chɛbili.  O zaŋ chɛribo n ŋmɛ buɣim. (He used flint to strike fire.) Colloc: chɛribo malifa 'flintlock gun'
O tum tuma maa chɛrichɛri. (He did the work half-heartedly.)
See: cheechi.  O chaŋ la chɛrichi ni. (He went to church.)

english: ladle, small calabash with long handle
example: Chɛriga din dam kurchu bim dini ka bɛ zaŋ dam naɣ' bim. (The ladle that stirs pig's broth also stirs cow's broth.) [Prov. ]
Colloc: beei chɛriga.  Di simdi ka a daa beei chɛriga gili zoli maa. (You should mark a line round the mountain.)
See: che.  Di chɛriguchɛrigu lu ya. (It fell in bits.)
See: che; lana.  Chɛrilana maa kpuɣi o takɔbi. (The executioner took his sword.)
Bɛ zaŋ situr' chɛra. (They got torn clothing.) Mɔɣu ni biŋkɔbiri yi gbaai biŋkɔbigu, o zaŋ o chɛra na ti wuhi shɛhira. (If a wild animal catches a beast, bring the remains as evidence.)
Chɛrili nyɛla bɛn ka yaa, amaa bɛ nyari bindirigu diri siɣili saha. (Ants have little strength, yet they store up food.) [PRO 0:]
O mali chɛrisheeni. (He has a sore in his anus.)
Sim: bomaboma, bɔribɔri.  O noo maa chɛrisiya. (His fowl is spotted.)

english: to chop/break into minute pieces; to tear to pieces
example: Ŋunkam lu kuɣili maa zuɣu ni chɛrisichɛrisi. (Whoever falls on the stone will be broken to pieces.) O ni to n-kpuɣ' a nti chɛris' a. (He will snatch you and tear you to pieces.)
From: (Heb.).  O daa ba chɛrub yiɣi. (He rode upon a cherub and flew.) Naa ni Izraɛl Naawuni ŋun ʒi Chɛrubinim' zuɣusaa. (O King, God of Israel, who sits above the cherubs.)
Salima chɛulɔnim din gbib' Naawuni jɛmbu duu puuni pununim'. (Gold hinges for the doors of God's Temple.) Kaman chɛulɔnim' ni kul gbibi punu ka di viini gindi shɛm. (As a door turns on its hinges.)
See: kahu.  O ko la chi. (He planted millet.)
Bihim chihi garila tab' barilim. (Milk gourds vary in size.) [Prov. ] Colloc: tadabo chia 'inkwell'
O yɛli li chibi. (He said it exactly.)

english: to chip out, bore, pierce; to inject, inoculate
example: Sa baligu ni chib' tiŋa. (Little drops of rain penetrate the ground.) [Prov. 00] Bɛ daa chib' voli Yisa zuɣusaa. (They made a hole above Jesus.) Colloc: chibi X chihiro-chihiro 'to vaccinate'
O mali chibili. (He has magic power for arrows.)
See: chaŋ; kpɛma.  O chaŋ ni chɛnkpɛma. (He walked fast.)
See: chibo; maŋli.  O zaŋ chibimaŋli n paɣi. (She used local soap to wash.)
See: chibo.  O zaŋ chibitarigu n niŋ chibo. (She put soap in the soap container.)

From: (ultimately Port.).  N yi zaŋ chibo paɣi mmaŋa... (If I use soap to wash myself...) Paɣimi a nuhi ni chibo. (Wash your hands with soap.) Paɣaba nyɔri bochaa n-duɣiri chibo. (Women burn lye to make soap.)
O zaŋ chichaɣa n gbahi zahim. (He used a fish trap to catch fish.)
Sim: chami.  Chichaɣili maa diri la tia. (The termite is eating the tree.)
Tiŋa maa nyɛla chichaɣili. (The land is unfertile.)
See: chibo; su.  O zaŋ chibisurili n paɣi. (She used bath soap to wash.)
Kɔchichi zɔri la tiŋli. (Still waters run deep.) [Prov. ] Luɣ' Chichi Shee. (Fair Havens - place name.)
Colloc: fo chichi.  Bɛ kul fomi doya chichi. (They lay there dead silent.) Baa wahi toli, toli ŋuni chichi. (The dog barks at a mortar, the mortar stands silently.) [Prov. ]
From: (Eng.). Sim: karimba.  Chichi maa wuhiri la bihi. (The teacher is teaching the children.)
See: napɔnchichiba.  O napɔn chichiba doli la. (His footsteps are there.)
Sim: yiɣi. Cpart: tɔhi.  O chiɣisi n-lu tiŋa. (He jumped down.)

english: to tattoo, cut marks; to make cuts for medicine, vaccinate
example: Luɣ'shɛŋa bɛ ni chihiri chiha n-nyɛ tapaɣisi. (Places they cut tribal marks are on the cheeks.) Bɔm' subili'viɛlli n chihi o ni dim luɣi shɛli maa. (Get a clean knife and cut where the snake bit.) Colloc: chihi X chihiro-chihiro 'to vaccinate'

Mii maa chihiya. (The rope broke.) O chihi gabiga. (He broke a rope.) Nahu yi chihi o kpaa ni, o ku ŋubi mɔ' chɔɣima. (If a cow breaks loose, it won't eat poor grass.) [Prov. ] Colloc: chihi suhu 'be heart-broken'

english: to deny, refuse, refute; to dislike, taboo; to break law
example: O chihi ni o bi mi o. (He denied knowing him.) O chihiri ma mi. (He hates me.) Di chihira. (It's forbidden.) Alahichi tumbu nyɛla zaligu chihibu. (Sin is breaking the law.) Etym: OV *CIS
See: chihi.  So bindirigu n-nyɛ so chihili. (One man's meat is another man's poison.) [Prov. 0]

See: chihi. Caus: chihi.  Luɣ'shɛŋa bɛ ni chihiri chiha... (Places they cut tribal marks...) Chiha pam m-be Dagbaŋ. (There are many marks in Dagbong.) Chiha chihibu nyɛla din yuui Dagbaŋ. (Tribal marking is an old custom.)
InvNact: chihi. Colloc: Masiachihira 'Antichrist'.  O nyɛla Masiachihira. (He is the Antichrist.)

See: chihi. Colloc: chihi/chibi X chihiro-chihiro.  Tim maa chihi niŋ ninsal' niŋgbuŋ ni maa n-nyɛ chihiro-chihiro maa. (The injected medicine is the vaccination.) Di yi lee ka bɛ chibi bia gbankɔɣu chihiro-chihiro... (When they inoculate a child against measles...)
Sim: chɛɣu.  A mini nir' yi mira mia, mia chihuŋ nyɛ li. (If you make rope with someone, it's a broken rope.) [Prov. ]
O gbi la chilibi. (He dug straight down.)
See: nɔchichɛriga.  O noo maa nyɛla chichɛrigu. (His fowl is mixed colours.)

english: to waste time, toss sb. about
example: Serial: chilim lɛbi.  O chilimdi la o saha. (He is wasting his time.)

From: (Ha.). Syn: farinwuta.  Bɛ zaŋ chilibi yaɣiri bɛ ni be luɣ' shɛŋa. (Sulphur is scattered where they lived.) Buɣim mini nyɔhi ni chilibi yiri bɛ noya ni na. (Fire, smoke and brimstone came from their mouths.)
O mali chilimbihi. (He has magic powers.)
See: -lana.  Chilimbihilana maa niŋ la alahiziba. (The magician performed wonders.)
See: chilim.  Di nyɛla chilima. (It's a waste of time.)
See: chilo.  Jɛrigu m-mali chilimua din mali gbura m-baani o chilo. (A foolish woman uses a crooked eyebrow stick to apply mascara.) [Prov. ]
Syn: chilo, chilitɔŋ.  O zaŋ chilinchihi n bali o nina. (She applied kohl to her eyes.)

english: kohl, antimony, mascara; medicine to see witches
example: Syn: chilo, chilinchihi.  Anabi chilitɔŋ, ban ka laamba m-banda. (The prophet's eye powder is for girls without parents.) [Prov. ]
O zani la chim. (He stood attentively.) Colloc: niŋ chim, fo chim.
Sim: duɣi, she.  O chim la tia wala. (She fried the nuts.) O chim la kawana. (She roasted groundnuts.)
Chima maa yiɣiya. (The beetle flew up.)
Sim: ŋɔrili, shɛrili.  Zaŋmi kawaan' chimda kpalaŋ' ŋɔ. (Take this bag of roast corn.)
Gen: mɔɣu.  Bɛ zaŋ chimli n pili duu. (They used chimli grass to thatch the room.)

Colloc: bali chilo. Syn: chilinchihi, chilitɔŋ.  Bɔzuɣu ka a zaŋ salima nɛma niŋ amaŋa, ka bali chilo? (Why put on gold and apply mascara?) Jɛrigu m-mali chilimua... m-baani o chilo. (A foolish woman... applies eye powder.)
Colloc: Chimsi Gɔli.  Chimsi goli maa puuni, ti puhiri la Chimsi. (During the month of Chimsi, we celebrate Chimsi.)
Ti yɛn puhi la Chimsi chuɣu. (We will celebrate the Chimsi festival.) Sim: lɛha
See: Chimsi; bilaa.  Chimsi Bilaa goli maa nyaaŋa ka Chimsi goli paana. (After Chimsi-Bilaa month, Chimsi month comes.)
See: chinchaŋga; dibiga. Sim: nimdibiga.  O zaŋ chinchandibiga n too chinchaŋga. (He used a skewer to hold the kebab.)
O diri la chinchaŋga. (He is eating kebab.) Chinchaŋga yi pa doo, nyin' zanimi o zaashee maa. (If kebab meat is not brave, take its place and see.) [Prov. ]
Gen: tia. Syn: ʒugulikukui.  O zaŋ chincheeŋa n nɛ o nyina. (He used chincheeŋa twig to clean his teeth.)
Ti bi wumdi o yuri napɔn' chinchiba damli. (We don't hear the beating of his horses' feet.)
O zani la chinchilinchiŋ. (He stood dumbfounded.)

english: small cloth worn over larger one (women)
example: See: chɛmsi.  Chimsa maa viɛla. (The zinc roof is good.)

O daa dɔɣ' o paɣisar' dɔɣim bidibiga n-zaŋ chinchini pil' o. (She gave birth and covered him with cloth.) Suɣulo yi beni chinchini kpɛri gaɣili ni. (With patience, you can put cloth into a narrow gourd.) [Prov. ] Colloc: pakoli chinchini 'widow's mourning clothes', Dagban' chinchini 'country cloth'
See: chinchini; -kɔha.  Paɣ' so ŋun daa yi Taiatira na n-nyɛ ago chinchinkɔha. (A woman from Thyatira was a seller of expensive cloth.)
See: chinchini; bia.  O zaŋ chinchinbia n pili o. (She wore a small cloth over her larger one.)
O zaŋ chinia n niŋ malifa ni. (He put the ramrod in the gun.)
See: chirili.  O ʒini ni chira ata. (He stayed for three months.)
Naawuni pala ŋun tumdi chirachira. (God is not a God of disorder.) Lubu din mali dabiɛm pam mini chirachira be ni. (There is panic and confusion.)
Zuɣusuŋlana mia lɔɣu chirila baɣiŋga gbinni. (The lucky person's quiver breaks under the tree.) [Prov. ]

english: to speak, make a speech; to squawk
example: O chiri o chiri. (He made a speech.) Noo maa chirimi. (The hen squawked.)
See: nintɔchiriga.  O chiri la kom. (He splashed water.)
Yin' bɔhimiya n-nya ninvuɣ' chirichi. (Find a worthy person.) Colloc: tuma chirichi 'professional workmanship'
From: (Twi.).  Chirifo maa nyɛla toondana. (The Chirifo is a leader.) Sim: Achiri, Adu, Afum
Nyin' chirigi' lana gari zab' pamda. (A woman with filed teeth is more beautiful than one with plaited hair.) Colloc: nyin' chiriga.
Bɛ zaŋ chiriga n-chirigi li pa tampiŋ zuɣu. (They use a graver to carve it on rock.) Chiri' biɛ ni saɣim kuriti. (A bad chisel destroys iron.) [Prov. ]
See: chirigi. Colloc: tɔb' chiriga.  Timpani yi bi kum tɔb' chiriga kumsi... (If the drum doesn't give the battle alarm...) Ŋmɛmiya tɔb' chiriga. (Raise the battle cry.)
Colloc: chirigi zabiri 'make parting in hair', chirigi nyina 'file teeth'. Ninst: chiriga.  Bɛ zaŋ chiriga n-chirigi li. (They carve it with a graver.)

english: to meet, cross paths; to guess; to happen to
example: Sa daŋmi yibu n-chaŋ ti chirigi Firawuna. (Go out to meet Pharaoh.) Di daa niŋla daliri ka n chaŋ nti chirigi Sool. (I happened to meet Saul.) Bi niŋ zamba ku chirigi biɛri. (One who doesn't cause downfall won't meet wickedness.) [Prov. ] Serial: chaŋ chirigi 'meet by coincidence'
Sim: bila, fiiŋ.  O nyɛla chirigili. (It is tiny.)
See: chinchini; pili.  O zaŋ chinchinpindigu n pili o. (He used a cover cloth for sleeping.)
Di niŋdi la chirigilichirigili. (It happens occasionally.)
Chirikuli maa yiɣiya. (The bird flew up.)
Sim: goli.  O ʒini ni chirili yini. (He stayed for one month.) Chira ata pɔi ka bɛ dabisili maa naanyi paai. (Three months before their wedding day.)

english: to mistake, make error; to mislead; to interrupt; to confuse
example: Yi chirimya pam. (You are making a serious mistake.) Ninvuɣ' shɛb' beni chirindi ya yi tɛha ni. (Some are misleading you.) Di chirind' o. (Don't confuse him.) Serial: kali chirim 'miscount'
O nyɛla chirinfu. (He is tiny.)
Unit: chirigi. Sim: tuhi.  Bɛ chirisi la taba. (They met each other.)
Bɛ boli o la Chiriwaandana. (They call him Chiriwaandana.)
Colloc: pu chirigili.  O mali chirigili. (He has good luck.)
Bɛ zaa chisimam kana. (They all came, one and all.)
Colloc: tɔɣisi chiriwaani.  O tɔɣisiri la chiriwaani. (He is being cheeky.)

english: place of the dead, judgement place
example: Colloc: lan' chiso.  Poli ka lan'chiso... (Hernia and its truss...) [Prov. ]
Colloc: cho ninni.  Bɛ malaikanim' kul chola m Ba Naawuni ninni. (Their angels always see my Father's face.)
See: choo.  N zaŋ takɔbi maa naŋgbancholi zali bɛ dunoya zaa ni. (I set the sword's point against their gates.) O zaŋ tampin' choya zuɣusaa leeg' o yiŋa. (It makes the high rock pinnacles its home.)
Sim: barinalana, bibiee.  O nyɛla chom. (He is a saboteur.)
Sim: fiɣisi.  O chomdi ma mi. (He is slandering me.) Suhuyini baɣa ni dunia chombu. (A sincere person doesn't worry about slander.) [Prov. ]
See: chom.  A yɛla yi mali a chomda n-kani. (If your problems are solved, your accuser is gone.) [Prov. ]
From: (Twi.).  Chomfo maa nyɛla toondana. (The Chomfo is a leader.) Sim: Adu, Achiri, Afum
See: choli.  Naɣ' choo n-nyɛ li. (It is a small cow.) Molifee nyɛla zuɣupili piɛl' choo. (Molifee is a small pointed white hat.)
See: choli.  Choo maa nyɛla din nyɛɣisi. (The pointed end is sharp.)
Chua ni mali ninvuɣ' so daa ʒi Yisa tooni. (A man with dropsy was before Jesus.) Sim: kpante
Syn: kochuɣu.  Kom yi kpaai bahi ka chɛ chuɣu, di so ni chuɣuŋmaya. (If water pours from a gourd, better a broken gourd.) [Prov. ]

Colloc: di/puhi chuɣu.  Bɛ tɔri li kuyili, chuɣu dali. (They perform it at funerals or festivals.) Nyuli dibu chuɣu puhibu nyɛla binshɛli din yuui Dagbɔŋ. (The yam festival is old in Dagbong.) Colloc: chuɣu bia 'festival gift'
See: binchunchuna.  Bashan Zoli din mali chunchuna pam. (Bashan Mountain with many peaks.)
Sim: dɛde, noo.  Di nyɛla churichi. (It is exact.)
Bɛ wa la chuuka. (They danced the chuuka.)
From: (Ha.) < (Ar.). See: alichiyama.  Kpiimba biɛhigu shee mini chiyaama buɣim kul dola palɔni Yawɛ. (The place of the dead and judgement fire lie open to the Lord.)

english: onyx (a type of jewel, from English < Latin < Greek)
example: O alaama ni mali la ɔniɣisi. (His ring has an onyx.)

english: onycha (a type of incense, from English < Greek)
example: Bɛ zaŋ la ɔnika n-niŋ tulaale maa ni. (They put onycha in the incense.)
N di la bindirigu biɛɣuni. (I ate food earlier today.) O daa kana biɛɣuni ha. (He came earlier today.)
O diri la bindirigu pumpɔŋɔ. (He is eating food now.) Ti chanila daa Asibiri dali kam. (We go to market every Saturday.)
O da la liɣiri palli daa ni. (He bought a new car in the market.) M ma da la chinchini viɛlli n-ti ma. (My mother bought a beautiful cloth for me.)
O da la chi maa n-ti o ma. (He pushed down the millet stalks for his mother to harvest.) Pukpariba maa daari la kaha na. (The farmers are pushing down the millet for harvest.)
Bɛ da o la gbankɔɣu tim asibiti ni. (They vaccinated him against measles at the hospital.) Biɛla kam tu ni bɛ da bihi chihiro-chihiro. (Every child must be vaccinated.)
O daari la mɔɣu ni ka bi lahi mi na. (He is wandering in the bush and can't find his way back.) Biɛla yi da mɔɣu ni, o ni zo dabiɛm. (If a child wanders in the bush, he will be afraid.)
O doli la o ba daadiini soli. (He follows his father's religious path.) Ti ni chɛ ka ti bihi doli ti daalahichi. (We will not let our children follow our sins.)

Ti chaŋ la daa Asibiri kam. (We go to market every Saturday.) Daa maa ni, niriba pam be ni. (In the market, there are many people.) Kawana daa maa booiya. (The price of maize has reduced.) Alibarika n-nyɛ daa nyaɣisim. (Bargaining brings market happiness.)

english: day after tomorrow; day before yesterday; past tense marker
example: Ti ni chaŋ daa. (We will go the day after tomorrow.) Daa la ka o daa kana. (He came the day before yesterday.) O daa be yiŋ daa. (He was at home earlier.) Daadaa ha, niriba daa bi mali motoka. (In ancient times, people didn't have cars.)
Daabia maa kɔhiri la nɛma pam. (The trader sells many goods.) Daabihi pam be daa ni Asibiri kam. (Many traders are in the market every Saturday.)
From: (Ha.).  O nya la chuuta. (He suffered harm.)
O niŋdi la daabiligu, o kɔhiri la chinchini. (He does business, he sells cloth.) Daabiligu nyɛla tuma din mali nyɔri. (Business is a profitable work.)
Bɛ mɛ la daachimbisa Tamale. (They built storey-buildings in Tamale.) Daachimbisa maa du zuɣusaa pam. (The multi-storey buildings are very tall.)

Daadam kam mali o biɛhigu. (Every person has their life.) Daadam ni bɔri shɛli di yɛla m-be o suhu ni. (What a person desires is in the heart.) Daadam pam be daa ni zuŋɔ. (Many people are in the market today.)
O daabilim bi viɛla, o bi mali yɛda. (His dishonesty is not good, he is not trustworthy.) Daabilim ni tooi chɛ ka nira kɔŋ o zo. (Dishonesty can make a person lose friends.)
Tamale daadambiɛligu galisiya pam. (Tamale's population is very large.) Daadambiɛligu maa zaa laɣim ya salima ni. (All the crowds gathered at the festival.)
Ti tu ni ti sɔŋdi ti daadamtaba. (We must help our fellow-men.) Di niŋ viɛlli ni ti be ni ti daadamtaba. (It is good to live with our fellow-people.)
Daadamtali nyɛla din mali anfaani. (Humanity is useful.) O daadamtali zuɣu, o sɔŋdi la niriba. (Because of his humanity, he helps people.)
O daai la yama maa. (She reground the coarse flour.) Kaŋkpaɣu maa tu ni bɛ daai li. (The millet needs to be partially ground.)

english: to push, push away/down/over; to blow (wind); to surge
example: O daai o n chaŋ tooni. (He pushed him forward.) Pɔhim maa daai la tihi maa. (The wind blew the trees.) O daai o maŋa n chaŋ salo maa ni. (He pushed himself into the crowd.) Kom maa daai n kana. (The water surged forward.)
O zaŋ daakpɛliŋa n zali duɣu maa. (She used a pot-wedge for the pot.) Daakpɛliŋa maa gbibi duɣu maa. (The pot-wedge holds the pot.)
Daalana maa laɣim la liɣiri daa ni. (The market supervisor collects money in the market.) O nyɛla daalana ŋun tumdi viɛnyɛla. (He is a market supervisor who works well.)
O kparila daafumaata n chaŋ jiŋli ni. (She wore print cloth to the mosque.) Daafumaata maa viɛla pam. (The print cloth is very beautiful.)
O niŋ la daamachia tuma maa ni. (He committed sabotage in the work.) Daamachia bi viɛla dama di saɣinda tuma. (Sabotage is not good because it spoils work.)
Daamachialana maa wurindi la tuma. (The saboteur is spoiling the work.) Bɛ gbaai daamachialana maa. (They caught the saboteur.)
See: dam.  O nyu la daam ka buɣi. (He drank pito and got drunk.) Ti duɣiri daam Chimsi ni. (We brew pito at Chimsi.)
Bɛ zaŋ daamati n gbi kobiliga. (They used dynamite to dig the well.) Daamati maa ŋmɛbu daa wumya katiŋa. (The dynamite blast was heard far away.)
Daanaa maa kpaŋsiri la wahi wabu. (The dance leader encourages the dancing.) O nyɛla daanaa ŋun mi baamaya. (He is a baamaya dance leader who knows it well.)
Daanbaliya maa yɛli ti la yɛltɔɣa. (The spokesman told us the matter.) O nyɛla daanbaliya ŋun yɛri viɛnyɛla. (He is a spokesman who speaks well.)
See: daimɔnd.  O lo la daamɔn o nuu ni. (She wore a diamond on her finger.) Daamɔn maa mali jilima pam. (The diamond has great value.)
See: dandiɛɣu.  Daanbiɛɣu maa ŋubi la ʒim. (The leech sucks blood.) O nyɛla daanbiɛɣu, o bɔri binshɛɣu ka bi tumda. (He is a parasite, he wants things without working.)
O mali daani viɛlli. (He has good intentions.) Di mali daani biɛɣu n-ti o. (Don't have evil intentions towards him.) O daani zuɣu, o tum la lala. (Because of his intention, he did that.)
O laɣim daankpali n guli buɣim. (He made a wood-pile to keep fire.) Daankpala maa zaa ʒiya dundɔŋ ni. (All the wood-piles are in the courtyard.)
O zaŋ daankuɣa n zali duɣu. (She used hearth stones for the pot.) Daankuɣa maa yi lu, di nyɛɣira. (If the hearth stones fall, they break.)
Daankaluŋ be daaŋa ni. (Soot is in the kitchen.) Daankaluŋ maa sabiya. (The soot is black.)
Daantaliga maa gbibi duu maa. (The roof-supporter holds the room.) Daantalisi anahi bi duhiri nam. (Four supporting poles don't make a chief great.) [Prov. ]
See: dantali.  Ti ni chaŋ daantali. (We will go in three days.) Daantali la ka o daa kana. (He came three days ago.)

english: cooking-place, kitchen; hearth; tongs; fire-pot, brazier
example: O ʒiya daaŋa ni n duɣira. (She is in the kitchen cooking.) Daaŋa maa buɣim bira. (The hearth fire is burning.) O zaŋ daaŋa n tuui buɣim. (He used tongs to start the fire.)
Bɛ zaŋ daanya n mɛ duu maa. (They used bronze to build the room.) Daanya maa neera. (The copper is shiny.)
See: daankaluŋ.  Daaŋkaluŋ be daaŋa ni. (Soot is in the kitchen.) Daaŋkaluŋ maa sabiya. (The soot is black.)
O nyɛla daarɛkita suŋ. (He is a good director.) Daarɛkita maa gbibi la tuma maa zaa. (The director controls all the work.)
O zaŋ daarisani n duɣi bindirigu. (She used a metal pot to cook food.) Daarisani maa mali viɛlim. (The metal pot is beautiful.)
Daasu maa nyɛla toondana Kambɔn-waa ni. (The Daasu is a leader among the gunmen.) Bɛ boli Daasu maa ni o kana. (They called the Daasu to come.)
O laɣim la dabaligu n-tuui buɣim maa. (He gathered small sticks to start the fire.) Biɛla maa ŋmɛri la dabala ni diɛma. (The small child is playing with twigs.)
See: daankpali.  O laɣim daaŋkpali. (He made a wood-pile.) Daaŋkpali maa galisiya. (The wood-pile is large.)
Dabaralana maa tibi o la tihi vari. (The herbalist treated him with leaves.) Niriba pam chanila dabaralana sani bɛ dɔriti ni. (Many people go to the herbalist for their illnesses.)
Ti chaŋ ti nya tiŋ' kurili maa dabari. (We went to see the ruins of the old town.) Dabari maa puuni mɔri zoya pam. (In the ruins, grass has grown tall.)
Dabarizaana maa zu la buni ka zo. (The plunderer stole the goods and ran.) Bɛ gbaai la dabarizaana maa ni o nayiɣa. (They caught the plunderer with the stolen goods.)
O kpiɣi la dabɔbili n kana yiŋa. (He carried a bundle of firewood home.) Dabɔba maa galisiya, ti ni tooi nyo buɣim daba pam. (The firewood bundles are many, we can make fire for many days.)
Liɣiri ŋɔ nyɛla dabɔdabo, pa yɛlimaŋli. (This money is fake, not real.) O tuma maa nyɛla dabɔdabo, di bi viɛla. (His work is a botch-up, it's not good.)
O ba dabɔɣu maa puuni, mɔri zoya pam. (In his father's ruined compound, grass has grown tall.) Dabɔɣu maa bi lahi mali nira. (The deserted compound no longer has anyone.)
O nyɛla dabɔɣulana, o yidana kpi la yuuni gari. (She is a widow, her husband died last year.) Dabɔɣulana maa kum daa ti yɛn niŋ. (The widow's late husband's funeral will be held.)

Dabeenzɔra ku tooi zani tobu tooni. (A coward cannot stand at the front of battle.) Bɛ mi ni ŋuna nyɛla dabeenzɔra, dinzuɣu bɛ bi bɔri o tuma ni. (They know he is a coward, so they don't want him at work.)

From: (Ha.) < (Ar.).  O niŋ la dabara ka bɔ nya o ni tooi tum shɛm. (He did an experiment to see how it works.) O chaŋ ti nya dabara timalana sani. (He went to get medicine from the herbalist.) Dabara bi viɛla dama di tooi niŋ barina. (Magic is not good because it can cause harm.)

english: to be attached, cling; to barely penetrate; to refit; to implicate
example: Bia maa dabi la o ma sani ka bi bɔri chaŋ. (The child clings to his mother and doesn't want to go.) Kpahi maa dabi la tia maa ni. (The nail barely penetrated the wood.) Bɛ dabi o la yɛltɔɣa maa ni. (They implicated him in the matter.)
Tim maa nyɛla dabidabi. (The medicine is sticky.) Bindirigu ŋɔ nyɛla dabidabi, di bi viɛla ŋubu. (This food is sticky, it's not good to eat.)
O mali dabidoo ŋun tumdi o puu ni. (He has a male slave who works on his farm.) Dabidɔbba maa daa mali la yaa pam. (The male slaves were very strong.)
Dabeenzobo gbaagi o ka o bi tooi yɛli yɛltɔɣa. (Fearfulness seized him and he couldn't speak.) O dabeenzobo zuɣu, o bi chaŋ ti yɛli o ba. (Because of his fear, he didn't go to tell his father.)
O nyu la dabiɛɣu ka doro gbaagi o. (He drank bad liquor and got sick.) Dam dabiɛɣu ku tooi kɔhi dama niriba bi yura. (Bad liquor can't be sold because people don't like it.)
Dabiɛmlana bi tooi chaŋ yuŋ o ko. (A coward cannot go alone at night.) Tɔbu ni, bɛ bɔri la dabba pa dabiɛmnima. (In war, they want brave men, not cowards.)
O zaŋ dabiga n di o wahu maa. (He used horse grass to feed his horse.) Dabiga maa zooi viɛnyɛla yuŋɔ. (The horse grass has grown well this year.)

english: to remove, break off; to break, come apart
example: O dabigi la baŋkaŋa maa o nu ni. (He removed the tick from his hand.) Mia maa dabigiya dama di kurigi. (The rope snapped because it was old.)
Kurimbuni, niriba daa kɔhiri daba. (In the past, people used to sell slaves.) O nyɛla dabili, ka o duuma bi saɣiri o viɛnyɛla. (He is a slave, and his master doesn't treat him well.)

english: slight attachment; slight penetration; insecure repair; implication
example: Dabiɛm gbaagi o ni o yiŋa ni yuŋ. (Fear seized him alone at night.) Biɛla maa zo dabiɛm ni wahu. (The child is afraid of snakes.) O ka dabiɛm, o ni tooi chaŋ yuŋ mi. (He has no fear, he can go at night.)
Bɛ zaŋ dabili n-ti o ka o bi mi shɛli. (They wrongly implicated him and he knew nothing.) Dabili maa bi kpɛma, dinzuɣu di yi luri na. (The attachment is not strong, so it keeps falling.)
O mali dabipaɣa ŋun sɔŋdi o yiŋ tuma ni. (He has a female slave who helps with housework.) Dabipaɣiba maa daa duɣiri la bindirigu. (The female slaves were cooking food.)
Dabiriga maa waɣilim bi paai o mayɛli. (The dwarf's height didn't reach his age-mates.) O nyɛla dabiriga tuma ni, so bi wumdi o yɛltɔɣa. (He is unimportant at work, no one listens to him.)
Mia maa nyɛla dabisa ka ku tooi chihi. (The rope is strong and won't break.) Wahu ŋɔ mali dabisa zuɣu. (This horse has a strong back.)
Di dabisa maa ti daa be Tamale. (Those days we were in Tamale.) Dabisa maa puuni, o daa bi yi yiŋ. (During those days, he didn't go out.)
Tim maa dabisa pam. (The medicine is very sticky.) Kum maa ni, bindirigu ŋɔ dabisa. (In the dry season, this food becomes sticky.)
O zaŋ dabisi n pahi dam maa ni ka di ŋɔri. (He added pito-mash to the brew to ferment.) Dabisi maa nyɛla din mali viɛlim dam maa ni. (The pito-mash gives good quality to the brew.)
Bin' dabisili ŋɔ bi viɛla nuu ni. (This sticky thing is not good in the hand.) Bindirigu maa leei la dabisili. (The food became sticky.)

Dabisili kam, o yiɣirila asiba zaŋ piligu. (Every day, he wakes up early.) Ti yɛn laɣim la dabisili din pahi ata. (We will meet on the third day.) O dɔɣim dabisili daa paai, ti zaŋ kpini ti o. (His birthday arrived, we gave him a gift.)
O zaŋ dabindi n pahi bɔrɔbɔro maa ni. (She added yeast to the bread.) Dabindi maa chɛ mi ka bindirigu maa piri. (The yeast makes the food rise.)

Dabodabolana maa niŋ la alahiziba niriba maa tooni. (The magician performed wonders before the people.) Niriba pam daa chaŋ ti nya dabodabolana maa tuma. (Many people went to see the magician's work.)
O zaŋ dabolaari n di o wahu maa. (He used dabolaari grass to feed his horse.) Dabolaari maa zooi viɛnyɛla mɔɣu ni. (The dabolaari grass grows well in the bush.)

english: hillock; site of deserted house; grave-mound
example: Bɛ daa du daboli maa zuɣu n lihi tiŋa maa zaa. (They climbed the hillock to see the whole town.) O daa ʒini daboli maa zuɣu n vuhira. (He sat on the hillock resting.) Bɛ sɔɣi o la daboli maa gbini. (They buried him under the grave-mound.)
O ŋma dɔɣu maa ka daburisi lu tiŋa. (He cut the wood and wood chips fell.) Daburiga maa kpe o nina ni. (A wood chip entered his eye.)
O nyɛla n dachee, n tuzopaɣa yidana. (He is my brother-in-law, my sister's husband.) Dachee maa kana ti nya ti. (Our relative came to visit us.)
Bɛ daa yihi ba dabitim ni yuun' kɔbigu nyaaŋa. (They freed them from slavery after a hundred years.) Dabitim bi viɛla dama niriba ka yɛda. (Slavery is not good because people have no freedom.)
Dachɛni kpe o nubili ni ka nyɛli o. (A splinter entered his finger and is painful.) O yihi la dachɛni maa ni pɛnti. (He removed the splinter with a needle.)
Tuma maa nyɛla dachɛri, n ku tooi naai li zuŋɔ. (The work is very difficult, I can't finish it today.) O biɛhigu leei la dachɛri o ma kum nyaaŋa. (His life became hopeless after his mother's death.)
O nyɛla n dachia, m mabia yidana. (He is my brother-in-law, my sister's husband.) Dachia maa kana yiŋ ti ni. (Our in-law came to our house.)
M mini o mali dachɛhili, dinzuɣu ti diɛmdi taba. (He and I have a joking relationship, so we joke with each other.) Dagbamba mini Mohi mali dachɛhili. (Dagomba and Mossi have a joking relationship.)
Dadira maa kɔhiri la bindira Asibiri dali kam. (The market-goer sells food every Saturday.) Dadiriba pam be daa ni Asibiri kam. (Many market-goers are in the market every Saturday.)
Daduɣira maa duɣiri la dam viɛnyɛla. (The brewer brews pito well.) Ti ni tooi da dam daduɣira maa sani. (We can buy pito from the brewer.)
O zaŋ daduɣu n niŋ dam maa ni. (She used the pito pot for the brew.) Daduɣiri ayi m-beni, zaɣ' yini mali dam, zaɣ' yini mali kom. (There are two pots, one has pito, one has water.)
O duɣi la dafaduka n-ti saamba maa. (She cooked jolof rice for the guests.) Dafaduka nyɛla bindirigu din nyɛɣisa pam. (Jolof rice is a very tasty food.)
Dafaradafara! Naawuni ni ti suhiri biɛri. (Greetings! May God comfort the sad hearts.) O daa yɛli dafaradafara n-ti o zo maa kum ni. (He offered condolences to his friend at the funeral.)

Ti mini ba mali dachihili, dinzuɣu ti bi loori taba. (We have an in-law relationship with them, so we don't marry each other.) Dachihili maa zuɣu, ti diɛmdi taba saha kam. (Because of the joking relationship, we always joke with each other.)
O kparila dafumaata n chaŋ salima ni. (She wore print cloth to the festival.) Dafumaatanima pam be daa ni zuŋɔ. (Many print cloths are in the market today.)
O zaŋ dagaɣili n niŋ dam n ti o zo. (He used a pito-gourd to give pito to his friend.) Dagaɣa maa yi lu tiŋa, di nyɛɣira. (If the pito-gourd falls, it breaks.)
Dagaɣu maa yila Ɔpɔ West na n ti ʒini Tamale. (The Dagarti person came from Upper West to live in Tamale.) Dagaɣu mini Dagbana tooi laɣinda viɛnyɛla. (Dagarti and Dagomba people get along well.)
O lo la dagalia o nuu ni n chaŋ salima ni. (She wore an arm ring to the festival.) Dagalia maa mali viɛlim pam. (The arm ring is very beautiful.)

english: piece of stick, firewood; wasps' nest
