example: O laɣisi la dagansi na n-tuui buɣim. (He gathered sticks to start a fire.) Tabilakpihi dagansi be tia maa zuɣu, miri ka a chaŋ m-miri li. (There are wasps' nests on the tree, don't go near it.)
O nyɛla Dagbambia, o yɛla Dagbani. (He is a Dagomba, he speaks Dagbani.) Dagbambia maa yɛri la salima. (The tale-teller is telling stories.)
Dagbambila kam tu ni o baŋ o kali. (Every Dagomba should know their culture.) O nyɛla Dagbambila amaa o be la Kumasi. (He is a Dagomba but he lives in Kumasi.)
Dagbambila maa bɔhindi la Dagbani karimba sani. (The young Dagomba is learning Dagbani from a teacher.) Dagbambihi pam be karimzɔŋ ni zuŋɔ. (Many young Dagomba are at school today.)
O nyɛla Dagbampaɣa ŋun mali baŋsim pam. (She is a knowledgeable Dagomba woman.) O mali Dagbampaɣa amaa o na bi kpuɣi o. (He has a girlfriend but hasn't married her yet.)

Dagbana kam nyɛla ŋun mali jilima n-ti o kpamba. (Every Dagomba respects their elders.) Dagbamba pam nyɛla pukpariba. (Many Dagomba are farmers.) Dagbankpamba yɛliya ni asiba nari narila zaawuni. (Dagomba elders say that morning preparations are done in the evening.)

O nyɛla Dagbandoo ŋun doli o kali viɛnyɛla. (He is a Dagomba man who follows his tradition well.) Dagbandaba maa zaa laɣim ya n ti puhi chuɣu. (All the Dagomba traditionalists gathered to celebrate the festival.)

O yɛri la Dagbani viɛnyɛla. (He speaks Dagbani well.) Bihi maa bɔhindi la Dagbani karimzɔŋ ni. (The children are learning Dagbani at school.) Dagbani kali wuhiya ni ti tu ni ti sɔŋdi taba. (Dagbani tradition says we should help each other.)
O yɛri la Dagbanli ni o ma. (He speaks Dagbani with his mother.) Dagbanli paɣa bɔbu nyɛla din mali soya pam. (Dagomba-style courtship has many customs.)

Dagbɔŋ nyɛla tiŋgbani din galisi. (Dagbong is a large area.) N chaŋ la Dagbɔŋ ni n ti nya n dɔɣiriba. (I went to Dagbong to see my relatives.) Dagbɔŋ puuni, ti puhiri chuɣu pam. (In Dagbong, we celebrate many festivals.)
Dagɔɣu maa nyɛla zahim din mali kɔbiri. (The dagɔɣu is a fish that has spines.) O gbaai la dagɔɣu mɔɣili ni. (He caught a spiny fish in the river.)
O ti ma la dagɔnia n tum tuma maa. (He gave me help to do the work.) Ti bɔri la dagɔnia n-ti ti puu ni tuma. (We need help for our farm work.)
See: daguli.  O kɔhiri la dagɔrili daa ni Asibiri kam. (He sells merchandise in the market every Saturday.) Dagɔrili maa galisiya o butiki ni. (The merchandise is plenty in his shop.)
Dagɔrili maa bi mali yɛda o paɣa sani. (The adulterous man is not faithful to his wife.) Niriba bi jɛri dagɔra biɛhigu. (People don't like the lifestyle of promiscuous men.)
O zaŋ dafua n-niŋ o bunu maa ni. (She used light blue in her cloth.) Chinchini dafua maa viɛla pam. (The light blue cloth is very beautiful.)
Dagɔrilim chɛ ka o bɔri paɣa kam o ni nya. (Male desire makes him want every woman he sees.) Dagɔrilim ni tooi chɛ ka nira tum tuumbiɛri. (Male desire can make a person do bad things.)

english: to be dirty; to make dirty
example: O daɣiya pam, o tu ni o paɣi o maŋa. (He is very dirty, he should wash himself.) Di daɣi duu maa ni binchɛra. (Don't dirty the room with rags.) Saa daɣira, ti ku tooi chaŋ daa. (Rain is threatening, we can't go to market.)

Daɣila, a ni kana biɛɣuni bee? (And so, will you come tomorrow or not?) Ka daɣila yi na bi baŋ ni lala bi viɛla? (So you still don't know that's not good?) A ni buɣisi shɛm ŋɔ ka daɣila a bɔri ni a baŋ? (So you want to examine and understand?)
O yɛla binyɛr' daɣiri. (He is wearing dirty clothes.) Kom maa nyɛla daɣiri, di bi simdi nyubu. (The water is dirty, it's not fit to drink.)
Dagɔrisigu bi viɛla Naawuni sani. (Adultery is not good in God's sight.) Bɛ ni ŋmaai dagɔrisigu maa bahi. (They should stop committing adultery.)
O nyɛla daɣirilana, o bi bɔri paɣibu. (He is a dirty person, he doesn't like washing.) Daɣirinima tooi gbahiri dɔriti. (Dirty people often catch diseases.)

Dagudara maa kɔhiri la nɛma pam ka diri nyɔri. (The merchant sells many goods and makes profit.) Dagudariba maa zaa laɣim ya ni bɛ tɔɣisi daa yɛla. (All the merchants gathered to discuss market matters.)
O daguli maa nyɛla din mali nyɔri. (His merchandise is profitable.) Daguya maa zaa kɔhiya zuŋɔ daa ni. (All the articles sold in the market today.)

Shinshɛɣu maa puuni mali daɣiri pam. (The bathroom has a lot of dirt.) So yi shihi kpiimba, o kpe la daɣiri ni. (If someone touches a dead body, he incurs ritual impurity.) Ti tu ni ti paɣiri ti nuhi ka chɛ daɣiri. (We must wash our hands to remove dirt.)
Daɣumahililana maa ʒiya mɔɣuni n guri saamba. (The prostitute is at the lorry station waiting for strangers.) Bɛ gbaai la daɣumahililanima maa zaa. (They arrested all the prostitutes.)
O daha pam o tuma ni. (He is very active in his work.) Biɛla maa dahiya ka tooi kpiɣi dɔɣu maa. (The child is strong and can carry the wood.)
Dagumahili nyɛla tuumbiɛɣu. (Prostitution is a sin.) Paɣa maa yi chaŋ dagumahili ni, o ni tooi gbaai dɔriti. (If a woman goes into prostitution, she can catch diseases.)

english: to level; to straighten; to test strength; to tune
example: Bɛ daa dahim tiŋa maa n mɛ. (They leveled the ground for building.) O dahim la luŋa maa ka di wum viɛnyɛla. (He tuned the drum and it sounded good.) A yi bi dahim a maŋa, a ku baŋ a yaa. (If you don't test yourself, you won't know your strength.)

O mali dahima pam o biɛhigu ni. (He has many opportunities in his life.) Bɛ zaŋ dahima n laɣisi lahabaya maa. (They took time to gather the information.) Ti ka dahima ni ti chaŋ pumpɔŋɔ. (We have no time to go now.)
O nyɛla dahimalana, o mali liɣiri pam. (He is a rich person, he has much money.) Dahimalana maa sɔŋdi la nandaamba. (The rich person helps the poor.)

english: day; moment, period; opportunity, chance; spare time; means, riches
example: Dahinli kam, o yiɣirila asiba zaŋ piligu. (Each day, he wakes up early.) O kɔŋ la dahima ni o chaŋ daa. (He missed the chance to go to market.) N ka dahima pumpɔŋɔ, ti ni tɔɣisi biɛɣuni. (I have no time now, we'll talk tomorrow.) O dahima galisiya, o ni tooi sɔŋ ti. (His means are plenty, he can help us.)

Dahinshɛli, ti ni laɣim ti yila yɛla. (Someday, we will discuss our issues.) O mali dahinshɛli tɛha viɛnyɛla. (He has good thoughts about the future.) Dahinshɛli ha, o ni leei bundana. (In the future, he will become rich.)
O lo la daimɔnd o nuu ni. (She wore a diamond on her finger.) Daimɔnd nyɛla kuɣ' kara din mali jilima. (Diamond is a precious stone that has value.)
O di bindirigu maa zaa dain. (He ate all the food completely.) Bɛ daa saɣim tiŋ' maa zaa dain. (They destroyed the whole town completely.)

english: person who starts journey before market day
example: Dajɔha maa daŋ o chaŋ daa ni o ti nya bindira. (The early traveler went to market early to get food.) Dajɔhanima tooi nyari bindira din viɛla. (Early travelers often get good food.)
Bɛ zaŋ dajitɔŋli n mɛ duu maa zuɣu. (They used crossed sticks to build the room's roof.) Dajitɔŋli maa kpuɣi la dakuɣiri pam. (The crane lifted many logs.)
O zaŋ dajitɔriga n pili o duu. (He used crossed sticks to thatch his room.) Dajitɔriga maa kuui, ti tu ni ti taɣi li. (The roofing stick is old, we need to replace it.)

english: wood gatherer; boy caring for old woman
example: Dakabira maa chaŋ mɔɣu ni n ti kabi dari. (The wood gatherer went to the bush to collect firewood.) O ti o ma dakabira n sɔŋdi o. (She gave her mother a boy to help her.)
Ŋɔ nyɛla n dahalali bia. (This is my very own child.) O mali o dahalali duu o yiŋa. (He has his own room in the house.) Ti zaa mali ti dahalali biɛhigu. (We all have our own lives.)
Bihi maa ŋubi la dakanda ka di nyɛɣisa. (The children are chewing sugar cane and it's sweet.) O ko la dakanda o pɔŋ ni. (He planted sugar cane in his farm.)
O nyu la dakahili ka di bi buɣi o. (He drank unfermented pito and it didn't make him drunk.) Dakahili nyɛla dam din na bi mili. (Unfermented pito is brew that hasn't fermented.)
O pili la dakɔɣili Asibiri dali. (She started the first stage of pito brewing on Saturday.) Dakɔɣili maa nyaaŋa, o ni ti pili dakumsili. (After the first stage, she will start the fermented stage.)
O chaŋ dakɔhigu ni n ti da dɔɣu. (He went to the wood market to buy firewood.) Dakɔhigu nyɛla tuma din mali nyɔri. (The firewood trade is a profitable business.)
O nyɛla dakoli, o na bi kpuɣi paɣa. (He is a bachelor, he hasn't married yet.) Dakoli maa paɣa kpi la yuuni gari. (The widower's wife died last year.)
O zaŋ dam n niŋ dakoligu ni. (He put pito in the wineskin.) Dakoligu kurili maa chɛhiya. (The old wineskin tore.)
O be la dakɔtali ni dama o bi bɔri paɣa. (He is in bachelorhood because he doesn't want a wife.) Dakɔtali ni tooi nyɛ wahala. (Bachelorhood can be difficult.)
Dakolisaɣabɔrili wala nyɛla din nyɛɣisa. (The wild berry plant's fruits are sweet.) Biɛla maa chaŋ ti tɔhi dakolisaɣabɔrili. (The children went to pick wild berries.)
O zaŋ dakolisakpuɣili n niŋ bindirigu maa ni. (She used a round bowl for the food.) Dakolisakpuɣili maa pali la saɣim. (The round bowl is full of t.z.)
Dakolizini puhi o nina ni. (A pimple swelled on his face.) O mali dakolizina pam o ningbuna ni. (He has many pimples on his body.)
O ʒini la dakpaɣili zuɣu n vuhira. (He sat on a dry log resting.) Dakpaɣili maa kuui, di ni tooi taai buɣim. (The dry log is old, it can catch fire easily.)
O ko la dakpam o pɔŋ ni yuuni ŋɔ. (He planted dakpam yams in his farm this year.) Dakpam maa wɔla pam. (The dakpam yams have produced well.)
Dakolim bi viɛla o, o bɔri paɣa. (Bachelorhood doesn't suit him, he wants a wife.) O kpalim la dakolim ni yuun' pam. (He remained in bachelorhood for many years.)
Dakpɛma maa mali la sara n-ti tiŋa maa. (The earthpriest makes sacrifices for the land.) Ti chaŋ ti nya dakpɛma maa o yiŋa. (We went to see the earthpriest at his house.)
Dakpɛra maa kɔhiri la bindira Asibiri kam. (The market-person sells food every Saturday.) Dakpɛriba pam be daa ni zuŋɔ. (Many market-people are at the market today.)
Dakpɛra maa mɛri la dapaɣa n-ti ti. (The carpenter is making a plank for us.) O boli dakpɛra na ni o ti mali o dɔɣu. (He called a carpenter to make his chair.)
O deei la dakpamli o ba kum nyaaŋa. (He received the earthpriesthood after his father's death.) Dakpamli nyɛla jilima din galisi. (Earthpriesthood is a great honour.)
O nyu la dakua ka dam maa yi o ninni. (He took a pick-me-up and sobered up.) Dakua nyɛla tim din tiri niriba dam ni. (Pick-me-up is medicine given to drunk people.)

O kpiɣi la dakuɣu n kana yiŋa. (He carried a large log home.) Bɛ ŋma dakuɣiri mɔɣu ni n ti mɛ duu. (They cut timber in the bush to build a room.) Dakuɣiri maa zaa ʒiya taa. (All the logs are stacked together.)
O chaŋ daa la dakulo kam. (He goes to market every market-week.) Ti yɛn laɣim la dakulo pirigili puuni. (We will meet within half a market-week.)
O zaŋ dakpiri n bahi. (He threw away the pito sediment.) Dakpiri maa be duɣu maa gbinni. (The lees are at the bottom of the pot.)
O nyu la dakumsili ka buɣi. (He drank fermented pito and got drunk.) Dakumsili nyɛla dam din mali yaa. (Fermented pito is strong brew.)

Ti yɛn laɣim la dali din pahi ata. (We will meet on the third day.) O dɔɣim dali paai, ti zaŋ pini ti o. (His birthday arrived, we gave him a gift.) Dali kam, o yiɣirila asiba zaŋ piligu. (Each day, he wakes up early.)
O ti o la dalama ka o lam dam maa. (She gave him a pito sample to taste.) Dalama maa nyaɣisa, o da la dam maa. (The sample was good, he bought the pito.)
Bɛ zaŋ dalibo n du mɔɣili maa. (They used a footbridge to cross the river.) Dalibo maa gbaai viɛnyɛla. (The footbridge is firmly in place.)
O ŋmɛri la daliga salima ni. (He is playing the long drum at the festival.) Daliga maa kum viɛla pam. (The long drum sounds very good.)
O zaŋ dalige n mɔni saɣim. (She used meal paste to make t.z.) Dalige maa pahiri bindirigu ni. (The meal paste is added to the food.)
Daligu maa kumsi wumiya katiŋa. (The long drum's sound is heard far away.) O ʒiri la daligu o zuɣu ka chani. (He carries the long drum on his head as he walks.)
Dɔɣu dalim maa bi viɛla mɛbu ni. (The bent wood is not good for building.) O zaŋ dɔɣu dalim n mɛ duu, ka di ŋmanila di yɛn lu. (He used bent wood to build the room, and it looks like it will fall.)
O dalim la kurugu maa ka di leei gɔɣu. (He bent the iron and it became curved.) Pɔhim kpiɛŋ maa dalim la tihi maa. (The strong wind bent the trees.)

english: to lean (self) against; to depend, rely
example: O dalim la dɔɣu maa zuɣu n vuhira. (He leaned against the tree resting.) A ku tooi dalim a maŋa, a tu ni a bɔhi niriba. (You can't rely on yourself, you must ask people.) Bia maa dalim la o ma sani. (The child clings to its mother.)
Bɛ dalim la biŋkɔbiri maa ni bɛ baŋ ba. (They marked the animals to identify them.) O dalim o gbaŋ maa ni o nuu. (He signed the paper with his hand.)
O dalim n chaŋ o duu ni naɣila so bi nya o. (He slipped away to his room without anyone seeing him.) Saha shɛli, o dalim yi tuma maa ni. (At times, he slips away from work.)
Bɛ zaŋ dalima n tɔ di nyɔri. (They used lead to seal the holes.) Dalima nyɛla binshɛli din mali anfaani pam. (Lead is a very useful thing.)
Dalimbundili maa be la mɔɣu ni. (The shrew is in the bush.) Dalimbundili nyɛla binnɛma din pɔra. (The shrew is a small animal.)
Di dalindi a zo ka di ka daliri. (Don't criticize your friend without reason.) O dalindi o maa ni o bi tum tuma maa. (He accused him of not doing the work.)

english: identifying mark, logo, seal; mark, blemish; slang, pig-Latin
example: Di nyɛla dalinli din wuhiri ni ŋuna n-nyɛ li. (It is a mark showing it's his.) O mali dalinli o nina ni. (He has a mark on his face.) Bihi maa yɛri la dalinli ka kpamba bi baŋdi li. (The children speak slang that elders don't understand.)
Daliŋ bi viɛla dama di saɣinda niriba yuya. (Defamation is not good because it spoils people's names.) O niŋ o la daliŋ ka o je o. (He defamed him and made him angry.)

english: reason, meaning; excuse; fortune, good luck
example: Daliri din chɛ ka o kana maa nyɛla bɔ? (What is the reason he came?) O niŋ la daliri ka bi chaŋ tuma ni. (He made an excuse not to go to work.) N chandi ŋɔ dɔɣi ma daliri pam. (This journey brought me good luck.)
O nyɛla n dalirilana, o sɔŋ ma pam. (He is my benefactor, he helped me a lot.) Dalirilana maa ka ti, ti bi mi ni ti yɛn niŋ bɔ. (Our mentor is not here, we don't know what to do.)
O kparila daliya palli n chaŋ salima ni. (He wore a new smock to the festival.) Daliya maa viɛla pam, ŋun n-ŋma li? (The smock is very beautiful, who wove it?)

english: two shillings coin (obsolete); counter in games
example: Kurimbuni, dalli daa nyɛla liɣiri. (In the past, the two-shilling coin was money.) Bɛ ŋmɛri la dara ni dala. (They play draughts with counters.)

O nyu la dam ka buɣi. (He drank pito and got drunk.) Ti duɣiri la dam Chimsi chuɣu ni. (We brew pito for the Chimsi festival.) Dam bi viɛla dama di saɣinda niriba. (Alcohol is not good because it spoils people.)

english: to stir, shake; to excite, agitate
example: O dam la saɣim maa ni chɛriga. (She stirred the t.z. with a ladle.) Tiŋgbani dam ya yuŋ, ka niriba zɔ dabiɛm. (The earth shook last night, and people were afraid.) O dam o suhu ka o je o. (He agitated him and made him angry.)

O bi kana dama o biɛra. (He didn't come because he is sick.) Ti ni chaŋ daa dama ti bɔri bindira. (We will go to market because we need food.) O nyɛla suhupiɛlli dama o nya o laamba. (He is happy because he saw his parents.)
Damakɔɣu maa dimi o ka o chaŋ asibiti. (The small snake bit him and he went to hospital.) Damakɔri be mɔɣu ni pam. (There are many small poisonous snakes in the bush.)
Damankuŋ maa nyɛla toondana Kambɔn-waa ni. (The Damankuŋ is a leader among the gunmen.) Bɛ boli Damankuŋ maa ni o kana nam ni. (They called the Damankuŋ to come to the chieftaincy.)

Ti yɛn wa la Damba Chimsi goli ni. (We will dance Damba in the month of Chimsi.) Damba dali, sokam chaŋ nayili ni. (On Damba day, everyone goes to the palace.) Damba nahu ŋɔ nyɛla din mali jilima. (This Damba cow is honourable.)
Damba Goli puuni ka ti puhiri Damba chuɣu. (During Damba month we celebrate the Damba festival.) Damba Goli nyɛla goli din mali jilima. (Damba month is an honourable month.)
Damba-Bilaa goli maa puuni, ti mali shili n-ti Damba. (During Damba-Bilaa month, we prepare for Damba.) Damba-Bilaa nyaaŋa ka Damba goli paana. (After Damba-Bilaa, Damba month comes.)
Damba-biŋli-kulisi dali, bɛ naai la Damba. (On the send-off day, they finish Damba.) Ti yɛn wa la Damba kulisi yuŋ. (We will dance the Damba send-off tonight.)
O damdi tiŋa n-ti o kpɛma. (He squatted down to his elder.) Di simdi ka ti damdi yɔɣuni, ka pa yiriŋ. (We should squat in the bush to defecate, not carelessly.)

O wum la o damli ka baŋ ni o kana. (He heard his footsteps and knew he was coming.) Ti wum la damli ni o kpiya. (We heard news that he died.) Damli maa wuhiya ni bɛ be ni. (The sound shows they are there.)
Tiŋgbani damli daa dam tiŋa maa yuŋ. (An earthquake shook the town last night.) Damli be tiŋa maa ni, niriba zɔra. (There is chaos in the town, people are running.)

Ti ni chaŋ dali, pa zuŋɔ bee biɛɣuni. (We will go the day after tomorrow, not today or tomorrow.) Dali la ka o daa kana. (He came the day before yesterday.) Zuŋɔ ni biɛɣuni zaŋ chaŋ dali. (Today, tomorrow, and the day after.)
O tumda o dan' sani yuun' pam. (He has worked for his master many years.) Daba tu ni bɛ ti bɛ dana jilima. (Slaves must give their master respect.) N dana, a bɔri bɔ? (Sir, what do you want?)
Bɛ boli o la Danaa dama o nyɛla tuzɔ. (They call him Danaa because he is the second twin.) Danaa mini Dawuni nyɛla bihi ayi. (Danaa and Dawuni are two children.)

english: horse with white legs and forehead
example: O barila danda viɛlli salima ni. (He rode a beautiful danda horse at the festival.) Danda maa nyɛla wahu din mali viɛlim. (The danda is a beautiful horse.)
Yɛltɔɣa maa leei la dandani, so bi mi di bahigu. (The matter became problematic, no one knows its end.) O niŋ ti la dandani ni o yɛltɔɣa. (He gave us a difficult matter with his talk.)
Dandiɛɣu maa ŋubi la ʒim. (The leech sucks blood.) O nyɛla dandiɛɣu, o bɔri la binshɛɣu ka bi tumda. (He is a parasite, he wants things without working.)
Dangbɛhinima maa zaa kana kuli maa ni. (All the grandchildren came to the funeral.) O dangbɛhinima sɔŋ o la pam o kurigim ni. (His grandchildren helped him much in his old age.)
Naa maa gbibi la damli o nuu ni. (The chief holds the sceptre in his hand.) Damli maa nyɛla nam dalinli. (The sceptre is a symbol of chieftaincy.)

english: to collectively move; to carry with difficulty; to be heavily pregnant
example: Bɛ zaɣi yino kam dani la o bɔɣu. (Each one of them carried their own load.) Paɣa maa dani la baɣili pam. (The woman is heavily pregnant.) O dani la dakuɣu maa ni wahala. (He carried the log with difficulty.)
O daniya dama o bi diri viɛnyɛla. (He has lost weight because he doesn't eat well.) Biɛla maa daniya ka o ma yihi o chaŋ dɔɣite. (The child lost weight and his mother took him to the doctor.)
Bɛ yiya maa danigi taba pam. (Their houses are far apart.) Ti mini ba danigiya, ti bi tooi nya taba. (We are far from them, we can't see each other.)
O duɣi la dankale n-ti bihi maa. (She made wafers for the children.) Dankale maa nyɛɣisa pam. (The wafers are very sweet.)
Bihi maa yɛri la dani ka ti bi baŋdi li. (The children speak slang that we don't understand.) O tɔɣisi la dani ni o zo. (He spoke slang with his friend.)

english: person who divides family through treachery
example: Danŋmara maa wurindi la daŋ maa ni o yɛltɔɣa. (The traitor is dividing the clan with his talk.) Bɛ yihi danŋmara maa daŋ maa ni. (They expelled the traitor from the clan.)
Bɛ gbaai la dansarika maa n niŋ sarika duu ni. (They caught the prisoner and put him in prison.) Dansarika duu maa puuni, niriba pam be ni. (In the prison, there are many people.)
O kparila dansiki o binŋmaa gbinni. (He wears a sleeveless smock under his gown.) Dansiki maa nyɛla din viɛli. (The sleeveless smock is beautiful.)
O gbibi la danta o nuu ni. (He holds a short gun in his hand.) Danta maa tooi ŋmɛra katiŋa. (The short gun shoots far.)

english: three days away; three days ago
example: Ti ni chaŋ dantali, pa zuŋɔ. (We will go in three days, not today.) Dantali la ka o daa kana ti nya ti. (He came three days ago to see us.) Daantal' la kurig' ata saha, n daa be yiŋ. (Three days ago at three o'clock, I was at home.)
Bihi maa yɛri la danli ka bɛ kpamba bi wumdi li. (The children speak slang that their elders don't understand.) Danli nyɛla yɛltɔɣa biɛhi. (Slang is children's language.)
Dantini maa chaŋ tobu ni n ti tuhi. (The platoon went to war to fight.) O nyɛla toondana dantini maa ni. (He is a leader in the platoon.)
Danyɔŋ maa diri la sima. (The large sugar ant is eating groundnuts.) Danyɔna pam be pɔŋ ni. (Many sugar ants are in the farm.)
O nyɛla danyura, o nyurila dam dabisili kam. (He is a drunkard, he drinks pito every day.) Danyuriba tooi bahiri bɛ liɣiri saɣi. (Drunkards often waste their money foolishly.)

O nyɛla ti daŋ ni yino. (He is one of our clan.) Daŋ maa niriba zaa laɣim ya n ti puhi chuɣu. (All the clan people gathered to celebrate the festival.) Ti daŋ mali la ti kali. (Our clan has its customs.)

english: to anticipate, do first; to do early; to be early
example: O daŋ ma tooni n chaŋ daa. (He went to market before me.) Sa daŋmi yibu biɛɣ' asiba ni a ti nya bindira. (Go out early in the morning to get food.) A yi daŋ tuma, a ni naai yomyom. (If you start work early, you'll finish quickly.)
O mali la daŋa n wuɣiri chinchini. (She uses the heddle to weave cloth.) Daŋa maa chihiya, o tu ni o taɣi li. (The heddle broke, she needs to replace it.)
O lu tiŋa ka nya daŋa o ninni. (He fell down and got a wound on his face.) O zaŋ tani n pɔbi o daŋa. (He used a bandage to wrap his wound.) Dansi maa nyɛli o pam. (The wounds are very painful.)
Daŋdaŋe Abudu n-daa di nam. (Abudulai the First was chief.) Ŋuna n-daa nyɛ daŋdaŋa ŋun kana. (He was the very first to come.)
Di ŋmanila fiig tiwal' daŋda. (They are like first-ripe figs.) O ko la daŋdili nyuya. (He planted early yams.) Daŋdili bindirigu maa nyɛɣisa pam. (The early food is very tasty.)
O niŋdi la dantɔŋ, o kɔhiri la nɛma pam. (He does wholesale, he sells many goods.) Dantɔŋ nyɛla din mali nyɔri pam. (Wholesale is very profitable.)
Ti doli la ti daŋkali ni ti yaanima. (We follow our ancestral customs from our grandparents.) Daŋkaya maa wuhiri ti ni ti tu ni ti be shɛm. (Ancestral customs teach us how we should live.)
O niŋ la daŋgana o suhu ni o ma kum nyaaŋa. (He had faith in God's goodness after his mother's death.) Daŋgana sɔŋdi nira wahala ni. (Faith helps a person in trouble.)
Bɛ zaŋ daŋko n mali chɛchɛ kuriti. (They use rubber to make bicycle tyres.) Daŋko maa tooi teera. (The rubber stretches easily.)
Daŋkuŋa tia maa be la mɔɣu ni. (The daŋkuŋa tree is in the bush.) Bɛ zaŋdi daŋkuŋa dɔri n mɛri yiya. (They use daŋkuŋa wood to build houses.)
O laɣim dapaa n guli buɣim. (He made a woodpile to keep fire.) Dapaa maa galisiya, ti ni tooi nyo buɣim yuŋ kam. (The woodpile is big, we can make fire every night.)
O zaŋ dapaɣa n mɛ o duu. (He used a plank to build his room.) Dapaɣa maa waɣila. (The plank is long.)
O zaŋ dapaɣili n pili o duu. (He used a plank to roof his room.) Dapaɣisi maa zaa ʒiya dundɔŋ ni. (All the planks are in the courtyard.)
O ti o la daŋkani ka o lam bindirigu maa. (She gave him a sample to taste the food.) Daŋkani maa nyɛɣisa, o da la bindirigu maa. (The sample was tasty, he bought the food.)

O nyɛla dapala ŋun tumdi viɛnyɛla. (He is a servant who works well.) Jɛsi dapala maa kana ti nya ti. (Jesse's son came to see us.) Sool daa bɔh' o, "Ŋun' dapala n-lee nyɛ a?" (Saul asked him, "Whose son are you?")
Bɛ zaŋ dapuhi n mɛ duu maa. (They used beams to build the room.) O ga la naɣikɔra dapua. (He yoked the oxen with a yoke bar.)

Bɛ zaŋ dapulli n pili duu maa. (They used crossed sticks to thatch the room.) Bɛ daa kpahi Yisa tabili dapulli zuɣu. (They crucified Jesus on the cross.) Dapulli maa gbibi duu maa. (The tie-beam holds the room.)
O zaŋ dapaɣiri n niŋ tim maa ni. (He used tree-bark in the medicine.) Dapaɣiri maa tɔ, amaa di tibirila dɔriti. (The tree-bark is bitter, but it cures diseases.)
O zaŋ dapuri n bahi. (He threw away the pito dregs.) Dapuri maa be duɣu maa gbinni. (The dregs are at the bottom of the pot.)
Bɛ ŋmɛri la dara yuŋ kam. (They play draughts every night.) O tooi ŋmɛri dara, o ni tooi di. (He plays draughts well, he can win.)
O laɣisi la dari n tuui buɣim. (He gathered sticks to start a fire.) Dari maa zaa kuui, ti tu ni ti kabi palla. (All the sticks are old, we need to collect new ones.)
O dari la mia maa ka di tiɣi. (He pulled the rope and it tightened.) O dari tɔhi o binyɛrigu. (He tore his garment.) Biɛla maa dari la o ma nuu. (The child pulls his mother's hand.)
Daridari gbaagi o dama o daŋa daɣi ya. (Tetanus caught him because his wound got dirty.) O chaŋ asibiti ni daridari. (He went to hospital with tetanus.)
O di la dari o dima zuɣu. (He gloated over his enemies.) Di diri dari dama di bi viɛla. (Don't gloat because it's not good.) Bɛ chaŋ la dari dibu chandi ni. (They went in a triumphal procession.)
Darigalana maa zu la liɣiri pam. (The fraudulent person stole a lot of money.) Bɛ gbaai dariganima maa zaa. (They caught all the fraudulent people.)

english: to pull, snatch, drag; to punish; to stretch, prolong
example: O darigi la o nuu ka o yi. (He snatched his hand and left.) Naawuni ni darigi alahichinima tibili. (God will punish sinners.) A yi so kpalaŋa ti darigi tana, di wuhirimi ni di pooya. (If trousers stretch when worn, it shows they are short.)

english: to punish him (lit. pull his ear)
example: Ba maa darigi o bia tibili. (The father punished his child.)
Ba' darigu bi ŋmani baluli ni baa. (A bought dog is not like one from a litter.) Bindirigu darigu nyɛla din nyɛɣisa. (Bought food is tasty.)
O zaŋ darigu n du duu maa zuɣu. (He used a ladder to climb onto the roof.) Darigu maa ŋmani din yɛn chihi. (The ladder looks like it will break.)
O darim ya dama o bi diri viɛnyɛla. (He has lost weight because he doesn't eat well.) Biɛla maa darimda, o ma yihi o chaŋ dɔɣite. (The child is losing weight, his mother took him to the doctor.)
Daŋko maa nyɛla darimata. (The rubber is elastic.) Bindirigu ŋɔ leei la darimata. (This food became rubbery.)
O kpamba mali darimihi, di bi chɛri ba chandi. (His elders have rheumatism, it stops them from walking.) Darimihi nyɛla dɔro din nyɛdi ninkura. (Rheumatism is a disease that pains old people.)
O nyɛla zom' darita. (He is a blind person's guide.) Darita maa sɔŋdi la zom' maa chandi. (The guide helps the blind person walk.)
O niŋ la dariga ka zɔ n chaŋ. (He committed fraud and ran away.) Dariga bi viɛla dama di niŋdi niriba wahala. (Fraud is not good because it causes people trouble.)
O kɔhiri la dasaɣim Asibiri kam daa ni. (She sells prepared food every Saturday at the market.) Dasaɣim maa nyɛɣisa, niriba pam da li. (The prepared food is tasty, many people bought it.)
Dasahira maa laɣim la liɣiri daa ni Asibiri kam. (The market fee collector collects money every Saturday at the market.) Dasahiriba tu ni bɛ niŋ adaalisi. (Market fee collectors must be fair.)
Dasampɔɣu maa wumya di yi ti ʒɛ. (The empty dawadawa pod makes sound when it moves.) Biɛla maa ŋmɛri la dasampɔɣu ni diɛma. (The child plays with an empty dawadawa pod.)
Dasanpukariga maa kuhi la yuŋ. (The hyena howled at night.) O nyɛla dasanpukariga, o je niriba. (He is like a hyena, he hates people.)
O tɔhi la dasaŋa n yihi dozim. (She picked a dawadawa pod and removed the powder.) Dasandi maa zaa ʒiya tia zuɣu. (All the dawadawa pods are on the tree.)
Bɛ zaŋ dasara n mɛ duu maa. (They used beams to build the room.) Dasara maa gbibi suɣili maa. (The pegs hold the tent.)
Dapaɣili kam mali dashɛbira ayi. (Each plank has two projections.) Dashɛbira maa tuɣi la dapaɣa maa taba. (The projections join the planks together.)
Dasiba! a wula? (Good morning! how are you?) O yɛli dasiba n-ti o zo. (He said good morning to his friend.)
Dasibiya! yi wula? (Good morning everyone! how are you?) O yɛli dasibiya n-ti bihi maa. (He said good morning to the children.)
O zaŋ dasiŋa n niŋ dam maa ni. (She used a pito pot for the pito.) Dasiŋa maa yi lu tiŋa, di nyɛɣira. (If the pito pot falls, it breaks.)
O zo n-nyɛ o dataa. (His friend is his rival.) Bɛ mini datahi maa bi laɣinda. (They and the rivals don't associate.) N dataa bɔri ni o ti ma barina. (My enemy wants to harm me.)
O nam mali dariza pam. (His reign has great splendour.) Liɣiri maa dariza booiya. (The money's value has reduced.) Naawuni dariza be o zuɣu. (God's grace is upon him.)
Datɔɣira maa tɔɣiri la dam mɔɣu ni. (The tapper is tapping wine in the bush.) O zaŋ dam na n-ti datɔɣira maa. (He brought pito for the tapper.)
O zaŋ datee n tuui buɣim palli. (He used a burning stick to start a new fire.) Datɛhi maa yi ti she, di ni tooi taai buɣim. (If the burning stick falls, it can start a fire.)
O ti o la datoluŋ n chaŋ daa. (He gave him errand money to go to market.) Datoluŋ maa bi saɣi n da binshɛɣu. (The errand money wasn't enough to buy anything.)
Datoluŋga kpe o nubili ni ka nyɛli o. (A splinter entered his finger and is painful.) O yihi datoluŋga maa ni pɛnti. (He removed the splinter with a needle.)
O ti ma la dawɔnia n tum tuma maa. (He gave me help to do the work.) Ti bɔri la dawɔnia n-ti ti puu ni. (We need help for our farm.)
O ŋma la dawɔrili n ti o ma. (He split firewood for his mother.) Dawɔra maa ʒiya dapaa ni. (The split firewood is in the woodpile.)
Dawɔrita maa ŋmari la dari viɛnyɛla. (The firewood-splitter splits wood well.) O nyɛla dawɔrita ŋun mali yaa. (He is a strong firewood-splitter.)
Dawɔrita maa kuui, ti tu ni ti ŋma palla. (The split firewood is old, we need to split new ones.) O zaŋ dawɔrita n tuui buɣim. (He used split firewood to start the fire.)
Bɛ ŋmɛri la dawule n boli niriba na. (They beat the gong-gong to call people.) Dawule maa kumsi wumiya katiŋa. (The gong-gong's sound is heard far away.)
Bɛ boli o la Dawuni dama ŋuna n-nyɛ kpɛma. (They call him Dawuni because he is the elder.) Dawuni mini Danaa nyɛla bihi ayi. (Dawuni and Danaa are two children.)
O zaŋ dayaɣili n mɛ o duu. (He used forked sticks to build his room.) Dayaɣa maa gbibi dapulli maa. (The forked sticks hold the cross-stick.)
Dayuɣu maa be la pɔŋ ni n diri nyuya. (The giant rat is in the farm eating yams.) Dayuri pam be mɔɣu ni. (Many giant rats are in the bush.)
Dazɔha maa gindi la tinsi ni n kɔhiri chinchini. (The itinerant trader goes round villages selling cloth.) Dazɔhinima tooi nyari liɣiri pam. (Itinerant traders often make a lot of money.)
Damba dazɔhigu dali, ti mali shili n-ti chuɣu. (On the eve of Damba, we prepare for the festival.) Tamale dazɔhigu dali, daa maa pali la niriba. (On Tamale market eve, the market is full of people.)
Bɛ mini o mali datahili pam. (They have great enmity with him.) Datahili bi viɛla yiŋ puuni. (Rivalry is not good in a household.)
Dazuli tia maa wala nyɛɣisa. (The dazuli tree's fruits are sweet.) Dazuya pam be mɔɣu ni. (Many dazuli shrubs are in the bush.)
Dazuli tikpurili maa be la mɔɣu ni. (The 'deaf dazuli' tree is in the bush.) Di nyɛla tia din mali anfaani. (It is a useful tree.)
O niŋdi la dazɔhigu, o chaŋla tinsi pam. (He does itinerant trading, he goes to many villages.) Dazɔhigu nyɛla tuma din mali anfaani. (Itinerant trading is a useful job.)
InvNact: dɔbee.  O nyɛla dɔbiɛlana, o gbihiri biɛla. (He is a restless sleeper, he sleeps little.) Dɔbiɛlana maa gbihiri ni wahala. (The restless sleeper sleeps with difficulty.)
See: do; biɛɣu. Nact: dɔbiɛlana.  O dɔbee bi chɛri o gbihibu. (His tossing stops him from sleeping.) Dɔbee nyɛla wahala n-ti dɔbiɛlana. (Tossing is trouble for a restless sleeper.)
From: (Ha.).  O ko la dɔbino o pɔŋ ni. (He planted date palms in his farm.) Dɔbino wala nyɛɣisa pam. (Dates are very sweet.)
Ti ʒiya dɔdo. (We are sitting comfortably.) Ti baŋ taba zuɣu dɔdo. (We know each other's secrets and are at ease.) [Prov. ]
See: do; dɔni; kpee.  O dɔdoonikpee maa nyɛla o paɣa. (His bed-fellow is his wife.) Dɔdoonikpee maa bi gbihi viɛnyɛla. (The bed-fellow didn't sleep well.)

See: taɣi.  O nyɛla dɔgbulitaɣirigu, o sɔŋdi la dirɔba. (He is a driver's mate, he helps the driver.) Dɔgbulitaɣirigu maa zaŋ la taɣirigu n zali loori maa gbini. (The mate put a chock under the lorry.)

english: to bear, give birth; to beget; to produce
example: O dɔɣi la bia asibiti ni. (She gave birth to a child at the hospital.) Wula ka Zara ni tooi lan dɔɣi bia? (How can Sarah have a child?) O dɔɣi la daliri pam. (It produced good fortune.) Di dɔɣi niŋ biɛlim. (It yielded a crop.)
See: -lim.  Doo yi chɛ ka o dɔbilim yina, o sum' o niŋgbuŋ. (If a man has an emission, he should wash.) O mali dɔbilim pam. (He has great courage.)
See: dɔɣi.  Di nyɛla o dɔɣiluŋ. (It is his inbred characteristic.) Dɔɣiluŋ nyɛla din yi dɔɣim ni na. (Inbred characteristics come from birth.)

Colloc: dɔɣim dali.  O dɔɣim dali paai, ti zaŋ pini ti o. (His birthday arrived, we gave him a gift.) Dɔɣim dali ka vi. (There's no shame on delivery day.) Paɣisar' dɔɣim n-nyɛ o. (She is a firstborn.)
See: dɔɣim; to. Syn: biɛri.  Bɛ sɔɣi la dɔɣinto maa. (They buried the placenta.) Dɔɣinto maa yi bahiya. (The afterbirth has been delivered.)
See: dɔɣim; to.  O nyɛla n dɔɣinto. (He is my maternal relative.) Dɔɣintonima maa zaa kana. (All the maternal relatives came.)
O nyɛla ti dɔɣira. (He is our relative.) Yisa dɔɣiriba daa wum lala. (Jesus' family heard that.) O dɔɣiriba zaa kana. (All his relatives came.)
Bɛ nyɛla dɔɣim yini. (They are one family.) O mini o nyɛla dɔɣim. (He and him are related.) Bɛ tuhi gbaag' o dɔɣim maa. (They captured his relative.)
Dɔɣirikpamba maa laɣim ya n ti tɔɣisi yɛltɔɣa. (The clan elders gathered to discuss matters.) O nyɛla ti dɔɣirikpɛma. (He is our senior brother.)

english: first surviving child after several deaths
example: O nyɛla dɔɣirikpihuŋ, o mabihi kpi. (He is the first surviving child, his younger siblings died.) Dɔɣirikpihuŋ maa mali dalinli. (The first surviving child has a mark.)
O nyɛla gbaridɔɣirili. (He is lame from birth.) O nyɛla jɛridɔɣirili. (He is a born fool.)

english: new mother; wife related by blood
example: Dɔɣiripaɣa maa nyɛla ŋun mali suhupiɛlli. (The new mother is happy.) Bɛ ti la dɔɣiripaɣa maa pini. (They gave the new mother gifts.)

O sani ka dɔɣiri yini kam yuli maŋli yina. (From him comes the true name of every family.) A duŋ yila a dɔɣiri yili ni. (Your hatred comes from your relatives' house.) Dɔɣiri dabisili maa paai. (The birth day arrived.)
See: dɔɣiri; tiŋa.  O labi la o dɔɣiritiŋa. (He returned to his motherland.) Dɔɣiritiŋa nyɛla din mali jilima. (The land of birth is honourable.)
See: dɔɣisa; dɔɣisi.  O nyɛla dɔɣisa, o yɛltɔɣa pam. (She is a nagger, she talks too much.) Dɔɣisa maa je niriba. (The nagger annoys people.)
Paɣa maa dɔɣisi la o tuzɔ. (The woman acted as midwife for her sister.) Bɛ boli paɣidɔɣisotɔɣinda na n ti dɔɣisi o. (They called a midwife to deliver her.)
O dɔɣisiri la o yidana dabisili kam. (She nags her husband every day.) Di dɔɣisiri niriba. (Don't nag people.)
O dɔɣisa pam chɛ ka niriba je o. (His many complaints make people dislike him.) Kpibiga ŋun zooi dɔɣisa ŋuna kɔŋdi maana. (An orphan who nags too much lacks a caretaker.) [Prov. 0]
Syn: paɣidɔɣisotɔɣinda.  Dɔɣisira maa sɔŋ o la dɔɣibu ni. (The midwife helped her in childbirth.) Bɛ boli dɔɣisira na. (They called the midwife.)
From: (Eng.).  O chaŋ dɔɣite sani dama o biɛra. (He went to the doctor because he is sick.) Dɔɣite maa tibi o la tim. (The doctor treated him with medicine.)
Dɔɣu gbaagi bia maa. (Convulsion caught the child.) O chaŋ asibiti ni dɔɣu. (He went to hospital with convulsions.)
O tɔhi la dɔɣu n yihi dozim. (She picked a dawadawa pod and removed the powder.) Dɔri maa zaa ʒiya tia zuɣu. (All the dawadawa pods are on the tree.)

english: stick, piece of wood, firewood, log
example: O ŋma la dɔɣu n ti o ma. (He cut a piece of wood for his mother.) Pool daa laɣisi dagansi na. (Paul collected firewood.) Kulim dari buɣim ni! (Feed the fire with wood!) O chaŋ ti kabi dari na. (He went to collect firewood.)

english: to filter, strain; to squeeze; to drip; to scoop last drops
example: O dɔhi la kom maa. (She filtered the water.) O dɔhi o la tim o nina ni. (He put drops in his eyes.) O dɔhi la dam maa bahigu. (He scooped the last drops of pito.)
Sim: ko.  O dɔhi la o puu. (He weeded his farm.) Bɛ dɔhi la mɔri maa zaa. (They weeded all the grass.)

english: to lie down; to sleep with; to die (euphemism)
example: O dɔni la o garo zuɣu. (He lay down on his bed.) A bi nya paɣa ka yɛrigi sɔŋ; a mini ŋuni n-yɛn doni? (Don't count your chickens before they're hatched - lit: you haven't got a woman and you're spreading your mat, who will you lie with?) [Prov. 0] N dɔni kabiri puuni. (I lie in the grave - I die.) O suhu dɔni luɣ' yini. (His heart is at peace.)
See: doo; -nyaŋ.  O nyɛla dɔnyaŋ, o zo dabiɛm pam. (He is a weak man, he is very afraid.) Dɔnyaŋ maa bi tooi tuhi. (The weak man cannot fight.)
See: dirɔba.  Dɔrɔba maa kari la loori. (The driver is driving the lorry.) O nyɛla dɔrɔba suŋ. (He is a good driver.)

O mali dɔro, o chaŋ asibiti. (He has an illness, he went to hospital.) Dɔriti pam gbahiri niriba. (Many diseases catch people.) Dɔr' baŋda nyɛ dɔr' tibira. (One who knows the disease can cure it.) [Prov. ]
See: do; shee.  O dooshee maa viɛla. (His sleeping place is good.) A dooshee? (How was your sleeping-place? - morning greeting)
See: -tali. Sim: dɔbilim.  O nyɛ n dɔtali tuuli bia. (He is the first child of my manhood.) Dɔtali nyɛla din mali jilima. (Manhood is honourable.)
See: do; tɔɣu.  O nyɛla dɔtoo, so bi ŋmɛri o diɛma. (He is a no-nonsense man, no one jokes with him.) Dɔtoo maa bi saɣiri yɛltɔɣa. (The no-nonsense man doesn't tolerate nonsense.)
See: doo.  Bɛ zaŋ dɔvihi n fiɛbiri kpini. (They use dawadawa twigs to whip guineafowl.) Dɔvihi maa be mɔɣu ni. (The dawadawa tree is in the bush.)

See: doo. Syn: dɔɣu, dasaŋa.  Nira yi kuli ʒi o dɔzaba, di yɛn fiɛbila o tiba. (If someone carries dawadawa pods on his head, they'll whip his ears.) [Prov. ] Dɔzaba maa zaa ʒiya. (All the dawadawa pods are there.)
See: dazɔhigu.  Damba dɔzɔhigu dali, ti mali shili. (On Damba eve, we prepare.) Tamale dɔzɔhigu. (Tamale market eve.)

english: yellow powder from dawadawa; yellow colour
example: See: doo; zim.  O zaŋ dɔzim n niŋ chɛri ni. (She put dawadawa powder in the soup.) Dɔzim maa nyɛla din mali viɛlim. (The yellow powder is good.) Di kɔtomsi nyɛ zaɣ' dɔzim. (Its appearance is yellow.)
A ba beni, de? (Is your father there at all?) A ni chaŋ, de? (Will you go, at all?) A bi mi li, de? (You don't know it, at all?)
A nya o? - Dee, dee, n bi nya o. (Did you see him? - Er, er, I didn't see him.) Dee, n bi mi ni n yɛn yɛli bɔ. (Umm, I don't know what to say.)
Dee maa be la mɔɣu ni. (The warthog is in the bush.) Dɛhi pam be tiŋa ŋɔ ni. (Many warthogs are in this area.)
Ŋun dee kpaŋ o maŋa, o bi di nasara. (Although he tried hard, he didn't succeed.) O dee bi bɔra, o kana mi. (Although he didn't want to, he came.)
From: (Eng.) < (Indian).  Bɛ niŋ la deeba nayili ni. (They held a durbar at the palace.) Deeba maa ni, niriba pam daa be ni. (At the durbar, many people were there.)

english: to receive, accept, take; to succeed, replace
example: O deei la pini o ba sani. (He received a gift from his father.) Bɛ deei la soli n chaŋ Tamale. (They set out on the journey to Tamale.) Ŋun lee yɛn deeg' o tuma maa? (Who will replace him at work?) O bi deei o noli. (He didn't obey him.)
See: diɛli.  O deeli la o chinchini. (She spread her cloth to dry.) Bɛ deeli la chi maa. (They spread the millet.)

O deemba maa kana ti nya o. (His father-in-law came to see him.) O nyɛla naa deemba. (He is the chief's son-in-law.) Musa daa gulila o deemba Jɛtro biŋkɔbiri. (Moses tended his father-in-law Jethro's animals.)
O deempaɣa maa sɔŋdi o yiŋ tuma ni. (His mother-in-law helps him with housework.) Pita deempaɣa daa biɛra. (Peter's mother-in-law was sick.)
Deendiɛmda maa wa la waa viɛnyɛla. (The dancer danced well.) Deendiɛmdiba maa zaa mali baŋsim. (All the entertainers have skill.)
Deeni maa ni, niriba zaa diɛm ya. (At the entertainment, everyone enjoyed.) Bɛ mali deeni pam salima ni. (They have much entertainment at the festival.)
See: diɛli.  Bɛ zaŋ chi maa n niŋ deeni ni. (They put the millet out to dry.) Deeni maa puuni, chi maa kuui ya. (In the drying, the millet has dried.)
O tum tuma maa dɛde. (He did the work exactly right.) Bɛ ʒiya dɛde bɛ taba nyaaŋa. (They sat exactly behind each other.) Yurilim din niŋ dɛde nyɛla din mali yaa. (Perfect love is strong.)
Di chaŋ! (Don't go!) Di diri dari! (Don't gloat!) Di chɛ ka ti doli lala soli. (Don't let us follow that path.)
O di kana biɛɣuni. (He came earlier today.) Bɛ di diri saɣim ka lara. (They were eating t.z. and laughing earlier.) N di chaŋ daa. (I went to market earlier today.)

A yi di chaŋ, n naan chaŋ. (If you had gone, I would have gone.) Kum n-yi di mali ma, n naan ku yɛli. (If I were hungry, I wouldn't tell.) Di biɛrim yi di be ka bo, n naan mali suhupiɛlli. (No matter the pain, I would be happy.)
Di nyɛla binshɛɣu din viɛla. (It is something good.) O tɛhiri la di yɛla. (He is thinking about it.) Di maa puuni, kom be ni. (Inside it, there is water.)

english: to eat, consume; to be sharp; to win; to enjoy; to experience; to marry; to burn; etc.
example: O diri la bindirigu. (He is eating food.) Sua maa diri pam. (The knife is very sharp.) Bɔl ŋmɛbu ni, ti di la nasara. (In the football match, we won.) Ti di la chuɣu ni suhupiɛlli. (We celebrated the festival with joy.) O di la wahala pam. (He experienced much suffering.) O di paɣa yuuni gari. (He married a wife last year.) Buɣim maa di la tia maa. (The fire burned the tree.)

english: it is difficult for me (lit. it doesn't know me)
example: Lala karim maa bi mi ma. (That reading is difficult for me.)

english: it is easy (lit. it knows you)
example: Lala tuma maa di mii ya. (That work is easy for you.)

english: it's a challenge/difficulty for him (lit. it's a bone to him)
example: Lala sabbu maa nyɛla kɔbili n-ti o. (That writing is a challenge for him.)

english: if it happens that; in case
example: Di yi niŋ ka o bi kana, ti ni chaŋ. (If it happens that he doesn't come, we will go.)

english: it failed/refused to work for him
example: Engine maa zaɣisi n-ti o. (The engine failed to start for him.)
Bɛ zaa diba ayi ni kana. (Two of them will come.) O timsim daa nyɛla kilogram diba ayi. (His weight was two kilograms.) Dib' anahi maa zaa yino kam. (Each of the four of them.)
O barila wɔridibiga. (He rides a young male horse.) O nanim' ŋmanila bambu' dibisi. (Her kings are like young male antelopes.)

english: small stick, twig; chewing-stick; disease from poisoning
example: O zaŋ dibiga n nɛ o nyina. (He used a chewing-stick to clean his teeth.) Dibisi maa ʒiya tiŋa. (The small sticks are on the ground.) O mali dibiga o niŋgbuna ni. (He has a disease from poisoning.)
O dibigi n zo n chaŋ. (He ran away and escaped.) Biɛla maa dibigi ka chɛ o ma. (The child ran away from his mother.)
O diɛhi o maa n chaŋ. (He assisted his mother to walk.) Biɛla maa diɛhi la o tuzɔ. (The child helped his younger sibling walk.)
O diɛhi la pini pam o dɔɣim dali. (He received many gifts on his birthday.) Bɛ diɛhi la liɣiri maa zaa. (They collected all the money.)
O diɛli la o chinchini wuntaŋa ni. (She spread her cloth in the sun to dry.) Bɛ diɛli la chi maa pɔŋ ni. (They spread the millet in the farm to dry.) O diɛli o maŋa wuntaŋa ni. (He sunbathed.)

english: to play, joke; to entertain; to enjoy; to be in joking relationship
example: Bihi maa diɛmdi la bɔl ŋɔbɔ. (The children are playing football.) O diɛmdi la o zo maa dachɛhili. (He jokes with his friend in their joking relationship.) Ti diɛm diɛma pam salima ni. (We enjoyed many entertainments at the festival.)

Bihi maa ŋmɛri la diɛma pam. (The children play many games.) Diɛma maa ni, niriba zaa laɣim ya. (At the entertainment, everyone gathered.) O diɛma maa lahiɡili la niriba. (His jokes entertained the people.)
Diɛmalana maa ŋmɛri la bolli viɛnyɛla. (The player plays football well.) Diɛmanima maa zaa mali baŋsim. (All the players have skill.)
O nyɛla n diɛmba, n paɣa ba. (He is my father-in-law, my wife's father.) O chaŋ ti nya o diɛmba. (He went to see his son-in-law.)

english: bridewealth, dowry; funeral gifts to wife's family
example: O yo la diɛmbuni n-ti o paɣa ba. (He paid bridewealth to his wife's father.) Diɛmbuni maa galisiya. (The bridewealth is large.) Bɛ ti la diɛmbuni kuli maa ni. (They gave funeral gifts at the funeral.)
O diɛmdoo maa kana ti nya o. (His father-in-law came to see him.) O ti o diɛmdoo la pini. (He gave his father-in-law a gift.)
See: deemba; paɣa.  O diɛmpaɣa maa sɔŋdi o pam. (His mother-in-law helps him a lot.) O chaŋ ti nya o diɛmpaɣa. (He went to see his mother-in-law.)

english: gifts to wife's family; polite address; in-law relations
example: O ti la diɛndi o paɣa ba kuli ni. (He gave gifts to his wife's father at the funeral.) Diɛndi, a wula? (Greetings, sir, how are you?) Bɛ mini o mali diɛndi viɛlli. (They have good in-law relations with him.)
O chaŋ diɛnkuli ni n ti sɔŋ o paɣa. (He went to his wife's parent's funeral to help his wife.) Diɛnkuli maa ni, niriba pam kana. (At the funeral, many people came.)
O do la difiɛli zuɣu. (He sleeps on a pillow.) O zaŋ difiɛli n ʒini di zuɣu. (He sat on a cushion.)
See: difiɛli; meeŋa.  O ʒini la difiɛmeeŋa zuɣu. (He sat on a pouffe.) Difeɛmeeŋa maa nyɛla din bala. (The pouffe is soft.)
O zaŋ difiɛsurigu n pili o difiɛli. (She used a pillowcase to cover her pillow.) Difeɛsurigu maa nyɛla din viɛli. (The pillowcase is beautiful.)
O zaŋ difu n di o bunsi. (He used chaff to feed his donkeys.) Difu maa be kpaŋ maa ni. (The chaff is in the granary.)
O tum la lala digaŋgan. (He did that intentionally.) Di bi niŋ yoli, o niŋ li digaŋgan. (It wasn't by accident, he did it on purpose.)
O lihi la diɣi ni n nya o ninni. (He looked in the mirror to see his face.) Zom' yi ti da diɣi, ninbihilana n-lihiri li. (If a blind person buys a mirror, the sighted person looks in it.) [Prov. ]
O ko la diɣi o pɔŋ ni. (He planted diɣi yams in his farm.) Diɣi maa wɔla pam. (The diɣi yams have produced well.)
O ʒiya diɣidiɣi. (He sat motionlessly.) Yuŋ maa nyɛla diɣidiɣi. (The night is dark and gloomy.) Kom maa leei la diɣidiɣi. (The water became thick.)
Diɣili maa waɣila pam. (The anthill is very tall.) A zo yi nya diɣili, a bi kaar' o. (If your friend is an anthill, you don't visit him.) [Prov. ]
O nyɛla diɣili, o waɣilim bi paai o mayɛli. (He is a dwarf, his height doesn't reach his age-mates.) Diɣa maa zaa be laɣingu maa ni. (All the dwarfs are at the gathering.)
Bɛ ko la diɣili maa zuɣu. (They farm on the raised hard land.) Diɣili maa tiŋa kpeya. (The raised land's ground is hard.)

english: to raise part of land and make it hard
example: Bɛ diɣili la tiŋa maa n mɛ. (They raised and hardened the land for building.) O diɣili o pɔŋ maa. (He raised and hardened his farm plot.)

english: to be blunted; to be hardened
example: Sua maa diɣilim ya. (The knife has become blunt.) O suhu diɣilim ya. (His heart has become hardened.)
Teeku kom maa kpaŋ diɣinli. (The sea water became hard.) Bɛ leela ninvuɣ' diɣilima. (They became hardened people.)
O zaŋ diɣiri n tɔ di nyɔri. (He used resin to seal the holes.) Diɣiri maa nyɛla din mali anfaani. (Resin is useful.)
Sua maa nyɛla diɣu. (The knife is blunt.) Diɣu bi tooi ŋmaai binshɛɣu. (A blunt thing cannot cut anything.)

english: to feed, provide food for; to poison
example: O dihi la o bia bindirigu. (She fed her child food.) O dihi o la tim ka o kpi. (He poisoned him and he died.) Di dihi a maŋa pam. (Don't overfeed yourself.)

english: to press down, press upon; to typewrite, print; to oppress
example: O dihi la gbaŋ maa n sabi. (He pressed the paper and wrote.) Bɛ dihi la litaafi maa. (They printed the book.) Dihinli dihi o la yuŋ. (A nightmare oppressed him at night.)
O mali dihili pam o yiŋa. (He has much food in his house.) Di nyɛla o dihili, o ni diri li. (It's his portion, he will eat it.) Saɣidihili maa saɣi ti zaa. (The food portion is enough for all.)
Dihinli dihi o la yuŋ, ka o zo dabiɛm. (A nightmare troubled him at night, and he was afraid.) O bi tooi gbihi dihinli zuɣu. (He couldn't sleep because of the nightmare.)
See: dilindihi.  Dihiŋdihi gbahi o naba. (Numbness caught his foot.) O ʒini tooi, ka dihiŋdihi gbaagi o. (He sat too long, and got pins and needles.)
O zaŋ dihirintoli n sabi gbaŋ. (He used a typewriter to write a letter.) Dihirintoli maa sabiri viɛnyɛla. (The typewriter writes well.)
O dii kana o maŋmaŋa. (He really came himself.) N suhu dii bi bɔri lala. (I really don't want that.) O dii yiɣisi n-chaŋ. (He immediately arose and went.) Dii kamna! (Come at once!)
Di nyɛla dii. (It is sufficient.) Di dii ya, m bi lahi bɔri. (It's enough, I don't want more.)
From: (Eng.).  Diisi maa kana ti nya ti. (The District Commissioner came to see us.) O nyɛla diisi ŋun tumdi viɛnyɛla. (He is a D.C. who works well.)
See: di; che. Syn: di-ka-guui.  O di la di-ka-che maa zaa. (He ate all the leftovers.) Di-ka-che maa be buɣili ni. (The leftovers are in the pot.)
See: di; ka; guui. Syn: di-ka-che.  Di-ka-guui maa bahi ya. (The leftovers have been thrown away.) O zaŋ di-ka-guui n ti o. (He gave him the leftovers.)
Bɛ wa la dikala kuli maa ni. (They danced the dikala at the funeral.) Dikala waa nyɛla din mali viɛlim. (The dikala dance is beautiful.)
Dikili maa zani la jɛ. (The remaining wall is still standing.) Dikiya maa gbinni, mɔri zoya. (At the base of the walls, grass grows.)
Dikilijina maa puuni, bɛ nya la binshɛŋa. (In the debris, they found things.) Dikilijini maa zaa lu ya. (All the broken wall fell.)
See: dukpini.  Dikpuni maa waɣila. (The wall is tall.) Bɛ pahi la dikpuni maa zuɣu. (They added to the top of the wall.)
See: dukpini; sabinli.  Karimba maa sabi la dikpunsabinli zuɣu. (The teacher wrote on the blackboard.) Dikpunsabinli maa sabiya. (The blackboard is black.)
See: dukunu.  O diri la dikunu ni zahim. (He eats kenkey with fish.) Dikunu nyɛla bindirigu din nyɛɣisa. (Kenkey is tasty food.)
See: Alikuraan.  O karimla dikuraan Asibiri kam. (He reads the Qur'an every Saturday.) Dikuraan wuhiri la muslinnima soli. (The Qur'an shows Muslims the way.)
O deei la dilaali. (He took the larger part.) Dilaali lana maa bi bɔri ni o ti shɛli. (The one who wants the larger part doesn't want to share.)
Dilali maa gindi la tinsi ni n kɔhiri chinchini. (The hawker goes round villages selling cloth.) Dilalinima tooi nyari liɣiri pam. (Hawkers often make a lot of money.)

english: that person, the latter, that fellow
example: See: di; -lana.  Dilana maa tuma bi viɛla. (That person's work is not good.) Dilana maa ni kpi, di yi ti biigi yaɣi. (That person will die if it gets too high.) Bɛ yi shihi dilana, doro ni tooi loogi o. (If they touch that person, disease can infect him.)
Diligu maa yiɣiya n du zuɣusaa. (The black bird flew up high.) O mali diligu, o kukoli bi viɛla. (He has tonsilitis, his voice is not good.)
Dilindihi gbahi n naba. (Numbness caught my foot.) O ʒini tooi, ka dilindihi gbaagi o. (He sat too long, and got pins and needles.)
Colloc: kpaŋ dilinli.  O nuu daa kpaŋ dilinli. (His arm went rigid.) O ni daa teeg' o nu' shɛli, dii kpaŋ dilinli. (The arm he stretched out became paralysed.)
Saɣim maa leei la dill. (The t.z. became thick.) Kom maa niŋ dill. (The water became thick.)
Yuŋ maa nyɛla dim. (The night is dark and gloomy.) Di dim ya, ti bi tooi nya binshɛɣu. (It's dark, we can't see anything.)
See: dini.  Dim bɔ n-lee bɔŋɔ? (Which one is this?) A bɔri dim? (Which one do you want?)
Wahu maa dimi o. (The snake bit him.) O dim o nyina ni niŋ ma. (He gnashed his teeth at me.) Tɔlim maa dimi o. (The scorpion stung him.) Bɛ dim o tiba ni ka o chaŋ. (They persuaded him to go.)
O dimdi la naɣibindi luɣ' yini. (She gathers cow dung in one place.) Bɛ dim la bɛ maŋa luɣ' yini. (They gathered themselves together.)
Nimdi maa dim ya. (The meat stinks.) Di dim ya, yihimi li bahi. (It stinks, throw it away.)
O dima bɔri ni o ti o barina. (His enemies want to harm him.) Di leei a zo dima. (Don't become your friend's enemy.) N dimnim' suhiri biɛri. (My enemies have bad hearts.)
See: di; maŋa.  Di maŋa n-nyɛ li. (It is itself.) Di maŋa wuhiya. (It showed itself.)
Dimbala maa nyɛla din viɛli. (That one is good.) Di niŋ kamaata ni n chɛ dimbala maa yɛla. (It's necessary that I leave the former matters.) Dimbala maa gari dimbɔŋɔ. (That one is better than this.)
Dimbɔŋɔ nyɛla din viɛli. (This is good.) Dimbɔŋɔ tuya ni ti niŋ li yomyom. (This needs to be done quickly.) Dimbɔŋɔnima zaa pala yɛlimaŋli. (All these things are not true.)
See: dim.  Wahu dimbu nyɛla din nyɛda. (Snake bite is painful.) Dimbu maa shee, o chaŋ asibiti. (At the bite place, he went to hospital.)
Bɛ wa la dimbu Gushiegu ni. (They danced the dimbu in Gushiegu.) Dimbu waa nyɛla din mali viɛlim. (The dimbu dance is beautiful.)
See: dim.  Wahu dimdi nyɛla barina. (Snake biting is dangerous.) Dimdi maa shee nyɛli o. (The bite place hurts him.)
Syn: dimli.  Dimdi be bɛ sunsuuni. (There is enmity between them.) O daa kpihim dimdi din be ti sunsuuni. (He removed the enmity between us.)
Dimgbaai maa be la bunzɔŋ ni. (The goat is in the pen.) O kɔhi la dimgbahi pam. (He sold many goats.)
See: duni.  O duna biɛri o. (His knees hurt.) O kurim la o dima. (He bent his knees.)
N diminima, wumiya! (My countrymen, listen!) Ti diminima zaa kana ti nya ti. (All our friends came to see us.)
Dimkabishee maa be la tia zuɣu. (The bachelor bee is on the tree.) Dimkabishɛhi pam be mɔɣu ni. (Many bachelor bees are in the bush.)
See: dim.  Wahu dimli nyɛla din nyɛda. (Snake bite is painful.) Di bahigu ŋmanila wɔɣ' dimili. (In the end it is like a snake-bite.)
Syn: dimdi.  Dimli be bɛ sunsuuni. (There is enmity between them.) Maana n-gari dimli. (Reconciliation is better than enmity.) [Prov. ]

See: di.  O mali dimma pam. (He has a big appetite.) Dina ka Yawɛ zali ya ni sokam pihim' o dimma tarig' la. (Thus the Lord commanded each to collect as much as he needs to eat.) Dimma ni nyubu ni be yil' shɛli. (A house of feasting.)
See: di; pooi.  Di-m-pooi gbaagi tiŋa maa. (Food-shortage caught the land.) A yi kɔŋ kabira di-m-pooi n-tahi li. (If you lack seeds, food-shortage has brought it.) [Prov. ]

english: to carry on head without holding
example: O dimsiri li mi n chana. (He is carrying it on his head without holding.) Nyɛvili bi ʒir' ka dimsira. (Life is not carried without holding.) [JOB :]

english: which; it, as for it (inanimate relative/emphatic)
example: Soli din wɔɣa ku du tia. (A path that is long won't climb a tree.) [Prov. ] Litaafi din pah' ayi. (The second book.) Ti bi mi din niŋ o. (We don't know what happened to him.) Din nyɛla din viɛli. (It is something good.)

english: this, that, these, those; as for that, also
example: Dina ka o niŋda. (That is what he does.) Dinnim' zaa zuɣu ka n nyɛvili be. (Because of all these things my life exists.) Din' la nyɛla din viɛli. (The other one is good.) Dina n-chɛ ka n sɔɣi maa. (That is why I hid.) Ti deemi din viɛli, ka ku deei din bie dina. (We receive good, and not receive bad also.)
Bɛ ŋmɛ la dindaara salima ni. (They played tug-of-war at the festival.) Dindaara nyɛla diɛm din nyɛɣisa. (Tug-of-war is an enjoyable game.)
Dindali maa, ti daa chaŋ daa. (That day, we went to market.) Buɣim tɔbu biɛɣu yi neei, dindali maa sokam chani la nayili. (On Fire-throwing day, then everyone goes to the palace.)
Dindaluŋ maa be la mɔɣu ni. (The long-legged grasshopper is in the bush.) Dindalima pam be puu ni. (Many grasshoppers are in the farm.)
O dindana bɔri ni o ti o barina. (His enemy wants to harm him.) Dindaamba maa zaa laɣim ya. (All the enemies gathered.)

Dindina a wumsim maa ni baligi. (Then your exhaustion will reduce.) A yi ku doli ma chaŋ, dindina n gba ku chaŋ. (If you won't go with me, then I won't go either.) Dindina a ni baŋ. (Then you will know.)
O tum la dingalingam. (He committed a wrongful act.) Dingalingam bi viɛla Naawuni sani. (Wrongful acts are not good in God's sight.)
O nyɛla dingalingamlana. (He is a wrongdoer.) Dingalingamlanima ni tu ni bɛ taɣi. (Wrongdoers must change.)

Niŋ dimbɔŋɔ bee dini ŋɔ. (Do this or that.) Dini ŋɔ nyɛla din viɛli. (This one is good.) Chɛriga din dam kurchu bim dini ka bɛ zaŋ dam naɣ' bim. (The ladle that stirs pig's broth is the same one that stirs cow's broth.) [Prov. ]
Yɛla ayi ŋɔ sunsuuni, dini lee be asama ni? (Between these two things, which is easy?) Dini ka n yɛn zaŋ? (Which one should I take?) Dabsi' dini n-daa galisi? (Which day was biggest?)
See: duni.  O dina biɛri o. (His knees hurt.) O kurim la o dina. (He bent his knees.)
Ŋɔ nyɛla n dini. (This is mine.) A yi mali a dini, a ni tooi zaŋ li. (If you have your own, you can take it.) O zaŋ o dini n ti o. (He gave him his own.)
See: dina.  Dinnima zaa nyɛla ban kana. (All those came.) Dinnima maa bi viɛla. (Those are not good.)
See: diŋwuna.  Dinŋuna di simdi ni a nyuri kom. (Therefore you should drink water.) Dinŋuna ti ni chaŋ. (Then we will go.)
See: dini.  Tuuli dino daa ŋmanila gbuɣinli. (The first one was like a lion.) Ŋɔ nyɛla n dino. (This is my own.)
See: di; saha.  Dinsaha, bɛ yɛliya ni bɛ zalimi waa. (At that time, they said they should start a dance.) Do' so daa be Naya dinsaha. (There was a man at Yendi at that time.)
O da la bindira dintiliga ni. (She bought food at the roadside market.) Dintiliga maa ʒiya soli yaɣili. (The roadside market is by the road.)
See: toli.  O mɛri la o dintoli duu. (He is building his own room.) Ŋɔ nyɛla n dintoli. (This is my very own.) O dintoli Naa n-nyɛ o. (He is an independent chief.)

See: di; zuɣu.  Dinzuɣu, ti ni chaŋ. (Therefore, we will go.) Dinzuɣu ka Yawɛ daa wurim ba. (Therefore the Lord scattered them.) Di zuɣu daa chɛ ka Ijiptinim' gbarigi. (Because of that, the Egyptians weakened.)

english: to balance a load on the head
example: See: dimsi.  O diŋsiri li mi n chana. (He is balancing it on his head as he walks.) O diŋsi la dakuɣu maa o zuɣu. (He balanced the log on his head.)
Diŋwuna di simdi ni a nyuri kom pam. (Therefore you should drink plenty of water.) Diŋwuna ti ni chaŋ daa. (Then we will go to market.)
O nyɛla ʒiri shɛhira dira. (He is a false witness.) Shɛhira dira maa yɛli la ʒiri. (The witness told lies.) Naawuni nyɛla nasara dira. (God is the victor.)
From: (Eng.).  Dirɔba maa kaari la loori viɛnyɛla. (The driver drives the lorry well.) O nyɛla dirɔba suŋ. (He is a good driver.)
Diribarilim bi viɛla. (Selfishness is not good.) Yi yi mali diribarilim tɛha yi suhuri ni... (If you have selfish thoughts in your hearts...)
Diribarilimnima bi bɔri ni bɛ sɔŋ niriba. (Selfish people don't want to help others.) Ti pala diribarilimnim'. (We are not selfish people.)
Sim: chibi.  O diribi voli dapaɣili maa ni. (He drilled a hole in the plank.) Bɛ diribi voli n niŋ. (They drilled a hole in it.)
Bindirigu ŋɔ nyɛla din diri. (This food is eatable.) Liɣiri ŋɔ nyɛla laɣidira. (This money is for spending.)
See: 'dirili.  Sua dirigu ku tooi ŋmaai yurilim. (A sharp knife cannot cut love.) [Prov. 0]
See: diligu.  Dirigu maa be la tia zuɣu. (The black bird is on the tree.) O mali dirigu, o kukoli bi viɛla. (He has tonsilitis, his voice is not good.)
O zaŋ dirigu n di saɣim. (He used a spoon to eat t.z.) Dirigu maa lu ya. (The spoon fell.)
O mali dirigu o nyɔɣu ni. (He has sores in his nose.) Dirigu nyɛla dɔro din nyɛda. (Syphilitic sores are a painful disease.)
See: dirigu; jaba. Syn: dirinyina.  O zaŋ dirijaba n di nimdi. (He used a fork to eat meat.) Dirijaba maa ʒiya teebuli zuɣu. (The fork is on the table.)
See: di.  Bindirigu ŋɔ nyɛla dirili. (This food is edible.) Dirili maa zaa di ya. (All the edible things have been eaten.)
See: di.  O gbibi la sua dirili. (He holds a sharp knife.) Sua dirigu ku tooi ŋmaai yurilim. (A sharp knife cannot cut love.) [Prov. 0]
See: dira.  O nyɛla shɛhira dirili. (He is a witness.) Shɛhira dirili maa yɛli la yɛlimaŋli. (The witness told the truth.)
See: dirigu; nyini. Syn: dirijaba.  O zaŋ dirinyina n di nimdi. (He used a fork to eat meat.) Dirinyina maa ʒiya. (The fork is there.)
O biɛhigu nyɛla diti. (His lifestyle is unchanging.) Di nyɛla diti, di bi taɣira. (It is invariable, it doesn't change.)
O ʒini ni diyoyo. (He stayed for a considerable time.) N chaŋ ni diyoyo, ka na bi labina. (I've been gone a long time, and haven't returned.)

english: to be lying down; to be in a place (flat/horizontal)
example: O do la garo zuɣu n gbihira. (He is lying on the bed sleeping.) Wahu bi doya ka bɛ lan buɣisiri li. (A snake doesn't lie there while you measure it.) [Prov. ] Yaakubu kobiliga daa do nimaani. (Jacob's well was there.) O suhu bi doya. (His heart is not at peace.)
O do la o puu Asibiri kam. (He weeds his farm every Saturday.) Di simdi ka ti dɔri ti sambani. (It's necessary that we weed our compounds.) O do la mɔri maa zaa. (He weeded all the grass.)
O nyɛla dodihira, o booni la kpiimba shia. (He is a medium, he calls the spirits of the dead.) Yi ni so miri ka o nyɛ dodihira. (None of you should be a medium.)
See: dɔɣite.  O chaŋ dokta sani. (He went to the doctor.) Dokta maa tibi o. (The doctor treated him.)
See: dakuɣu.  O kpiɣi la dokuɣu n kana. (He carried a log.) Dokuɣiri maa ʒiya. (The logs are there.)
See: doo.  Dokuliʒiɛm maa viɛla pam. (The dawadawa flower is very beautiful.) Dokuliʒiɛm maa ʒɛya tia zuɣu. (The dawadawa flower is on the tree.)

english: old man; name for boy named after grandfather
example: Dokurigu maa ʒiya yiŋ. (The old man is at home.) Dokur' ayi yi doli soli, so ni boli o kpee o biɛli. (If two old men walk together, one will call the other elder brother.) [Prov. ]

A zo yi nyɛ jaŋa a doli ku kpalim tia zuɣu. (If your friend is a monkey, your throwing-stick won't stay in the tree.) [Prov. ] Doli din she sima bi lan zɔri sabilim. (The stick used for roasting groundnuts no longer fears being black.) [Prov. ]

english: to follow, accompany; to pass via; to follow a road; to depend on; to be applied to
example: O doli la o ma n chaŋ daa. (He accompanied his mother to market.) A yi ku doli ma chaŋ, n gba ku chaŋ. (If you won't go with me, I won't go.) Ti doli la soli ŋɔ n chaŋ Tamale. (We follow this road to Tamale.) O samli doli o. (The debt is applied to him.) Di doli taba. (They go together.)

english: to lay down; to preserve, restore
example: O doli la o bia garo zuɣu. (She laid her child on the bed.) Dolimi n nyɛvili. (Preserve my life.) Bɛ doli la suɣili. (They pitched a tent.)

english: to stretch oneself; to stretch sth.; to straighten
example: O doli o maŋa. (He stretched himself.) Dolimi naa nyɛvili. (Grant the king a long life.) O doli la dapulli maa. (He straightened the cross-stick.)
O lo la dolibi o nyiŋgɔya ni. (She wore a chain on her neck.) Karimbaani n-nyɛ bɛ nyiŋgɔya ni dolibi. (Pride is their necklace.) [PSA :]
See: doli; kpee. Syn: dolintaba.  O nyɛla n dolinkpee. (He is my companion.) Fawɔɣili dolinkpee n-nyɛ sarati. (Carelessness is the companion of accident.) [Prov. 00]
See: doli; taba. Syn: dolinkpee.  O nyɛla n dolintaba. (He is my companion.) N dolintab' kaɣiri ma mi. (My companions avoid me.)
See: -tali.  Ti mini ba mali dolintabitali. (We have fellowship with them.) Dolintabitali nyɛla din viɛli. (Fellowship is good.)

english: to trace, follow; to make sb. follow
example: O dolisi la mɔɣili maa. (He traced along the river.) O dolisi o soli. (He followed the path.) Wula ka n ni tooi baŋ di gbinni, ka pa ni so n-dolisi ma? (How can I understand unless someone leads me?) Di dolisi a nuu. (Don't stretch out your hand.)
O nyɛla dolodolo, o chaŋla cheechi ni. (He is a Christian, he goes to church.) Dolodolo pam be tiŋa ŋɔ ni. (Many Christians are in this town.)
From: (Ha.). Syn: dama.  O bi kana domi o biɛra. (He didn't come because he is sick.) Ti ni chaŋ domi ti bɔri li. (We will go because we need it.)
Syn: vinyaɣilana.  O nyɛla dondira, o bi bɔri tuma. (He is a lazy person, he doesn't want work.) Dondira maa ʒiya ka bi tumda. (The lazy person sits without working.)
Syn: luŋa.  Dondo maa ŋmɛri la luŋa viɛnyɛla. (The drummer plays the talking drum well.) Dondonima mali jilima Dagbaŋ. (Drummers have honour in Dagbong.)

Doo nyɛla ti' gahindili. (Dawadawa is a special tree.) Dɔhi pam be tiŋa ŋɔ ni. (Many dawadawa trees are in this area.) Yiŋ' doo ni kaɣili ŋmaaŋa. (A dawadawa near a house is a problem for monkeys.) [Prov. ]

O nyɛla doo suŋ. (He is a good man.) O yidana n-nyɛ doo maa. (Her husband is the man.) Bɛ dɔɣi la doo, pa paɣa. (They gave birth to a male, not female.) Dɔbba ayi ni ti be puu ni. (Two men will be at the farm.)
O zaŋ doodo n pili o nina. (He wore a mask to cover his eyes.) O nyɛla doodo tiŋa ŋɔ ni. (He is a great man in this town.)
From: (Ha.).  Doogali maa gbaai la nayiɣa maa. (The native police caught the thief.) Doogalinima be daa ni. (The native police are in the market.)

english: to extract water/liquid; to mould; to plate; to skim
example: O dooi la kom kobiliga ni. (He extracted water from the well.) Luɣuluɣu yɛli ni o ku dooi baŋa. (The bottle says it won't be used to mould bracelets.) [Prov. ] Bɛ dooi la salima pa li. (They plated it with gold.)

english: to mould, cast; to plate, overlay
example: O dooi la daanya pa li. (He plated it with bronze.) Niriba dooi anzinfa pa di zuɣu. (People plate it with silver.)
O zaŋ li dooli. (He took it by force.) Di niŋ dooli ka o chaŋ. (He went compulsorily.)
O ma daa ti anzinfa doora. (His mother gave to the silversmith.) Salindoora maa tumdi viɛnyɛla. (The goldsmith works well.)
O dooshee maa viɛla. (His sleeping-place is good.) A dooshee? (How was your sleeping-place? - morning greeting) Yɛda ni tooi vuui zoya di dooshɛhi. (Faith can move mountains from their places.)
See: doo; yili.  Paɣ' so ŋun ka dooyili nyɛla pakoli. (A woman without a marital home is a widow.) O kuli la dooyili. (She married - lit: went to husband's house.)
O mali la dozim chinchini. (He has a yellow smock.) Dozim maa viɛla pam. (The yellow color is very beautiful.)

english: look for dir- or with other vowel

example: Nimdi maa duya. (The meat has gone bad.) O bɔrila da' shɛli din bi dura. (He looks for wood that doesn't rot.)
Bɛ du la mɔɣili maa. (They crossed the river.) Ti du la teeku. (We crossed the sea.) Du n-chaŋ yaɣili maa. (Cross over to the other side.)

english: to filter, strain; to squeeze out, drip
example: Syn: dɔhi.  O du la kom maa. (She filtered the water.) O du o la tim o nina ni. (He put drops in his eyes.)

english: to go up, climb, be exalted; to set in; to mount (animal)
example: O du la zoli maa zuɣu. (He climbed to the top of the mountain.) Wuntaŋa duya. (The sun has risen.) O yuli duya pam. (His name is very famous.)
Yɛltɔɣa maa du ʒini o suhu. (The matter depressed his heart.) O du ʒini la o maŋa. (He depressed himself.)
See: du.  Gooni maa dubu nyɛla mita anu. (The wall's height is five metres.) Di wɔɣilim mini di yɛliŋ ni di dubu zaa nyɛ yim. (Its length, breadth and height are the same.)
See: du.  Naɣilahi dubu nyɛla din niŋda. (The bulls' mounting is happening.)
See: duu; dɔni.  O kpe o dudoonigu ni n ti gbihi. (He entered his bedroom to sleep.) Dudoonigu maa viɛla. (The bedroom is nice.)
O ʒiya duduhugu ni n duɣira. (She is in the kitchen cooking.) Duduhugu maa puuni, ti mali la buɣim. (In the unfloored room, we have a fire.)
See: duu.  Bɛ du la dufahirili zuɣu n ti nya tiŋa maa zaa. (They climbed onto the flat roof to see the whole town.) O ʒiya dufahirili zuɣu. (He is on the flat roof.)
See: difiɛli.  O do la dufiɛli zuɣu. (He sleeps on a pillow.) Dufiɛli maa bala. (The pillow is soft.)
See: difiɛsurigu.  O zaŋ dufiɛsirigu n pili o dufiɛli. (She put a pillowcase on her pillow.) Dufiɛsirigu maa viɛla. (The pillowcase is beautiful.)
See: difu.  O zaŋ dufu n di o bunsi. (He used chaff to feed his donkeys.) Dufu maa be kpaŋ maa ni. (The chaff is in the granary.)
See: duu; gbe.  O kpe o dugbɛrili ni n ti gbihi. (He entered his bedroom to sleep.) Dugbɛrili maa viɛla. (The bedroom is nice.)

english: to cook, boil; to be hot (temperature)
example: O duɣi la bindirigu n-ti o bihi. (She cooked food for her children.) Kom maa duɣiya. (The water has boiled.) O niŋgbuŋ duɣiya, o mali fiiba. (His body is hot, he has fever.) O duɣi la dam. (She brewed pito.)

Bihi maa duɣiri la kom mɔɣili ni. (The children are swimming in the river.) O baŋ la ko' duɣili. (He knows how to swim.) A yibala duhiri ŋarɔŋ, lɔn ania m-bɔhim ko'duɣili. (If your enemy is paddling the canoe, try to learn to swim.) [Prov. ]
Duɣiduɣi yiɣiya soli zuɣu. (Dust is rising on the road.) Duɣiduɣi maa kpe o nina ni. (The dust entered his eyes.)
Kom duɣili maa tula pam. (The boiling water is very hot.) O mali niŋgbun' duɣili. (He has a fever - lit: boiling body.)
See: duɣi.  Pumpɔŋɔ nyɛla o duɣili. (Now it's her cooking turn.) Duɣili saha paai. (Cooking time has come.)
See: duɣi.  O chaŋ ko' duɣili ni. (He went swimming.) Duɣili nyɛla din mali alaafee. (Swimming is healthy.)
See: duɣu; me.  O niŋdi la duɣimɛbo. (She does pottery-making.) Duɣimɛbo nyɛla tuma kurili. (Pottery-making is an old craft.)
Syn: yaɣimɛra.  Duɣimɛra maa mɛri la duɣiri. (The potter makes pots.) O nyɛla duɣimɛra suŋ. (She is a good potter.)
See: duɣi.  O nyɛla bindiriduɣira. (She is a food-cook.) Duɣiriba maa duɣiri bindirigu. (The cooks are cooking food.)
See: duɣi. Cpart: 'kahili.  Nimdi duɣirili maa nyɛɣisa. (The cooked meat is tasty.) O ku deei nin' duɣirili, naɣila nim' kahili. (He won't accept cooked meat, only raw.)

english: pot (general term); hub of wheel; title of Chief Linguist
example: O zaŋ duɣu n duɣi bindirigu. (She used a pot to cook food.) Chɛchɛbuŋ' duɣuri daa nyɛla daanya. (The chariot's hubs were bronze.) M Ba Duɣu n-nyɛ o. (He is the Chief Linguist.)
See: duɣu.  O lo la duɣubɛli o napɔŋ ni. (She wore a pot-shaped anklet on her leg.) Duɣubɛla maa viɛla. (The pot-shaped anklets are beautiful.)
See: duu; gula.  Duguliba maa guli la sarika duu maa. (The warders guard the prison.) O nyɛla dugula. (He is a warder.)
InvCaus: du.  O duhi la o binyɛra mɔɣili maa duli. (He brought his things across the river.) Bɛ duhi la naa malibu na. (They brought the chief's possessions across.)

english: to lay out meat for sale
example: O duhi la nimdi daa ni. (She laid out meat for sale in the market.) Bɛ duhiri la nimdi Asibiri kam. (They lay out meat every Saturday.)

english: to lift up, raise, exalt; to pile up; to mount (animal)
example: O duhi o zuɣu n lihi. (He lifted his head and looked.) Bɛ duhila bɛ yee. (They raised their voices.) O duhi o yuli. (He exalted his name.) Bɛ duhi la kuɣa. (They piled up stones.)

english: to paddle a canoe; to drive/pilot; to convey; to pull a cart
example: O duhiri la ŋarɔŋ. (He is paddling the canoe.) O mi loori duhibu. (He knows how to drive.) O duhiri la waɣa. (He is pulling a cart.)
Syn: duli.  Bɛ laɣim la kuɣa n niŋ duhili. (They gathered stones into a pile.) Duhili maa waɣila. (The heap is tall.)
See: duhi; maŋa.  O duhimaŋtali chɛ ka niriba je o. (His pride makes people dislike him.) Duhimaŋtali bi viɛla. (Pride is not good.)
See: dukpini; baɣili.  Dukpimbaɣinda maa be la mɔɣu ni. (The large grasshopper is in the bush.) Dukpimbaɣindisi pam be puu ni. (Many large grasshoppers are in the farm.)
See: dukpini; baɣisi. Syn: kunduŋ.  Dukpinbaɣisigu maa kuhi la yuŋ. (The hyena howled at night.) O nyɛla dukpinbaɣisigu. (He is like a hyena.)
See: duu. Sim: gooni.  Dukpini maa waɣila. (The wall is tall.) DBɛ dihi ba ti tabili dukpini. (They pressed them against the wall.)
See: dukpini; sabinli.  Karimba maa sabi la dukpinsabinli zuɣu. (The teacher wrote on the blackboard.) Dukpinsabinli maa sabiya. (The blackboard is black.)
See: duu. Sim: duduhugu.  Dukulijini maa zani la jɛ. (The ruined room wall is still standing.) Dukulijina maa puuni, mɔri zoya. (Inside the ruined walls, grass grows.)
From: (Twi.).  O diri la dukunu ni zahim. (He eats kenkey with fish.) Dukunu nyɛla bindirigu din nyɛɣisa. (Kenkey is tasty food.)
Sim: jɛŋkuno.  Dulaamihi maa gbaai la waɣi. (The cat caught a snake.) Dulaamihi nyɛla biŋkɔbigu din nyaɣisa. (The cat is a nice animal.)

english: shore, bank; across, other side; heap, pile
example: Bɛ ʒiya duli maa zuɣu. (They are sitting on the bank.) O duhi la binyɛra duli. (He brought things across.) Ti be la teeku duli. (We are on the other side of the sea.) Bɛ laɣim la kuɣa n niŋ duli. (They gathered stones into a heap.)
Duligu maa be la tia zuɣu. (The ground-hornbill is on the tree.) Dulisi pam be mɔɣu ni. (Many ground-hornbills are in the bush.)
O mali duligu o nyɔɣu ni. (He has a nasal disease.) Duligu nyɛla dɔro din nyɛda. (The nasal disease is painful.)

O nyɛri la dulim. (He is urinating.) Bɛ dulim, nintɔri, bindi bee tiri pala din ni tooi looi so. (Urine, spit, faeces or vomit cannot infect anyone.) Dulim chanila katiŋa amaa di naarila a tooni. (Urine goes far but stops in front of you.) [Prov. ]
See: dulim; su; surigu.  O dulimsurigu maa biɛri o. (His bladder hurts.) Dulimsurigu surigu ni tooi dam paɣa yi walisiri yuura. (The bladder can be affected if a woman is in labour too long.)
See: -nima. Colloc: teeku dulinima.  Teeku dulinim' nyaya ka dabiɛm kpe ba. (The coastal peoples saw and were afraid.) Dulinima maa ʒiya teeku yaɣili. (The coastal people live by the sea.)
See: dulim; laa.  O zaŋ dulinlaa n ti bia maa. (She gave the child a potty.) Dulinlaa maa be duu ni. (The chamberpot is in the room.)
See: dulim.  Bɛ zaŋ duliŋkua n tibiri dɔriti. (They use the wild bulb to treat diseases.) O mali duliŋkua o duliŋ ni. (He has pain when urinating.) Duliŋkua nyɛla dɔro. (Bilharzia is a disease.)
See: duni; bia.  O dumbia biɛri o. (His kneecap hurts.) O dumbia maa luya. (His kneecap dislocated.)
See: dulim; biliga.  O gbi la dumbiliga o dugbɛrili ni. (He dug a urine sump in his bedroom.) Dumbiliga maa puuni, kom be ni. (In the bathroom place, there is water.)
See: dunoli; bia.  O ʒiya dunɔbia zuɣu. (He is sitting on the doorstep.) Dunɔbihi maa zuɣu, bɛ ʒiya. (They are sitting on the doorsteps.)
See: dunoli.  O ʒiya dunɔdali maa ni. (He is standing in the doorway.) Dunɔdaya maa zaa yooi ya. (All the gateways are open.)
See: dunoli; du.  O du la dunɔdura maa. (He climbed the stairs.) Dunɔdura maa kalinli nyɛla pia. (The number of steps is ten.)
See: dunoli; guli.  Dunɔgula maa yooi la dunoli. (The gatekeeper opened the gate.) Dunoguliba guli la dunoya. (The gatekeepers guard the gates.)
O zaŋ dunɔkparigu n kpari dunoli. (He used a door peg to close the door.) Dunɔkparigu maa chihiya. (The door peg broke.)
See: dunoli; kpari.  Dunɔkparita maa kpari la dunoli yuŋ kam. (The door-closer shuts the door every night.) O nyɛla dunɔkparita. (He is the one who closes the door.)
O sabi la yɛltɔɣa maa dunɔpaɣirli zuɣu. (He wrote the words on the doorpost.) Dunopaɣira maa zaa mali viɛlim. (All the doorposts are beautiful.)
See: dunoli; bia.  O ʒiya dundɔbia zuɣu. (He is sitting on the doorstep.) Dundɔbihi maa zuɣu, bɛ ʒiya. (They are sitting on the doorsteps.)

Bɛ ʒiya dundɔŋ ni n diɛmda. (They are in the courtyard playing.) Dundɔŋ maa puuni, ti mali buɣim. (In the courtyard, we have a fire.) Karimbaa maa ʒiya karim dundɔŋ ni. (The teacher is in the schoolyard.)
Dunduɣu maa dimi o. (The cobra bit him.) Dunduri pam be mɔɣu ni. (Many venomous snakes are in the bush.)
Dundum diri la dɔɣu maa. (Borer-beetles are eating the wood.) Dundum maa be dapaɣili ni. (Wood dust is in the plank.) Syn: kufo
O duɣi la dundunkolo n diri. (She fried yam chips and is eating.) Dundunkolo nyɛɣisa pam. (Yam chips are very tasty.)
O duna biɛri o. (His knees hurt.) O kurim la o duna. (He bent his knees.) Miri ka a dahindi a duna yi ti biɛra. (Don't strain your knees if they hurt.)
O duni la gbinni. (He urinated.) Bia maa dunimi. (The child urinated.) O chaŋ ti duni. (He went to urinate.)

From: (Ha.) < (Ar.).  Dunia ŋɔ puuni, ti be la biɛhigu ni. (In this world, we are in life.) O be dunia yili ni. (He is alive.) Dunia salinim' zaa, yilimiya suhupiɛlli yila. (All people of the world, sing joyful songs.) Di po dunia ŋɔ. (Don't swear by this world.)
See: -nima.  Dunianim' zaa ni ti nya. (All the people of the world will see.) Dunianima be la dunia yili ni. (The people of the world are alive.)
See: dulinlaa.  O zaŋ dunlaa n ti bia maa. (She gave the child a chamberpot.) Dunlaa maa be duu ni. (The chamberpot is in the room.)
O ʒiya dunokɔra zuɣu. (He is sitting on the doorstep.) Dunokɔra maa zuɣu, bɛ ʒiya. (They are sitting on the doorsteps.)
Syn: dunɔbia.  O ʒiya dunokoli zuɣu. (He is sitting on the doorstep.) Dunokɔya maa zuɣu, bɛ ʒiya. (They are sitting on the doorsteps.)

O ʒiya dunoli maa ni. (He is standing in the doorway.) Kum nyɛla dunoli ka sokam yɛn doli kpe. (Death is an entrance everyone will pass through.) [Prov. ] Niriba maa zaa dunoya dɔɣirikpamba laɣim. (The clan elders of all the clans gathered.)
See: dunoli; yo.  O zaŋ dunoyɔriga n yo dunoli. (He used a door-closer to shut the door.) Dunoyɔrisi maa zaa yooi ya. (All the gates are open.)
Colloc: paɣidɔɣiso duntam.  O kparila duntam pala. (She wore new white sandals.) Paɣidɔɣiso duntam nyɛla din viɛli. (A new mother's white sandals are beautiful.)

O mini o mali duŋ. (He and him have enmity.) A duŋ yila a dɔɣiri yili ni. (Your hatred comes from your relatives' house.) [Prov. ] Dinzuɣu n-chɛ ka Izraɛlnim' na kul mali duŋ ni Dauda yilnim'. (Because of this, the Israelites still have enmity with David's dynasty.)
Sim: duŋtaabo.  Duŋkalibu be tiŋa maa ni. (Rioting is in the town.) Bɛ gbaai la ban niŋ duŋkalibu maa. (They caught those who rioted.)
Sim: duŋkalibu.  Bɛ niŋ la duŋtaabo tiŋa maa ni. (They made rebellion in the town.) Duŋtaabo bi viɛla. (Rebellion is not good.)
O nyɛla duŋtaara. (He is a rebel.) Duŋtaariba maa zaa gbaai ya. (All the rebels were caught.)
See: du.  Bɛ zaŋ dura n mɛ duu maa. (They used trestles to build the room.) Dura maa zuɣu, bɛ ʒiya. (They are sitting on the scaffold.)
See: dunɔdurili.  O du la durili maa. (He climbed the step.) Durili maa kalinli nyɛla pia. (The number of steps is ten.)

english: room, hut; prison cell; mud nest
example: O mali o duu o ba yiŋa. (He has his room in his father's house.) Bɛ zaŋ o ti kpɛhi duu. (They imprisoned him.) Polɔya kpe la duu maa ni. (The toads entered the room.) Duu maa puuni, bɛ ʒiya. (Inside the room, they are sitting.)

english: to worsen, get worse; to arouse emotion
example: O dɔro maa duui ya. (His sickness worsened.) Yɛltɔɣa maa duui o suhu. (The matter aroused his emotions.)
O zaŋ duula n niŋ o. (He used a syringe to give him an enema.) Duula niŋbu nyɛla din mali anfaani. (Administering an enema is useful.) Colloc: niŋ duula.
See: duu; -lana.  O nyɛla duulana. (She is a wife.) Duulana maa ʒiya o duu ni. (The woman is in her room.) O nyɛla duulana n-ti nachimbila maa. (She is the boy's guardian.)

O duuma maa ti o la tuma. (His master gave him work.) Nyin' Jɛrɔbɔam ŋun nyɛ Sulemaana dabili ŋɔ ti zaɣis' a ba duuma. (You, Jeroboam who were Solomon's servant, rejected your father's master.) Yaa n Duuma Yawɛ. (O my Lord God.) Tomas yɛli, "N Duuma ni n Naawuni!" (Thomas said, "My Lord and my God!")
Duuŋa maa dimi o. (The mosquito bit him.) Duunsi pam be yuŋ. (Many mosquitoes are at night.)
See: duuŋa.  Bɛ nyo la duuŋmam n kariti duunsi. (They burn mosquito weed to repel mosquitoes.) Duuŋmam maa nyɔbu sɔŋdi. (Burning the mosquito weed helps.)
See: duu; ʒini.  Bɛ ʒiya duʒiinigu ni n diɛmda. (They are in the sitting room playing.) Duʒiinigu maa viɛla. (The sitting room is nice.)
From: (Eng.). Sim: nakpaa.  O mali la pɔŋ eka anu. (He has a farm of five acres.) Eka maa galisiya. (The acre is large.)
See: alimug.  Bɛ zaŋ ɛligum dɔri n mɛ duu. (They used algum wood to build the room.) Ɛligum tia maa waɣila. (The algum tree is tall.)
