O zaŋla kambu n-wuɣi chinchini maa. (She used fine-spun yarn to weave the cloth.)
O daa zaŋ kamli n-niŋ ʒɛri maa ni. (She put garden eggs in the soup.)
Bɛ zaŋla kampaŋa n-lo ningbuni maa. (They used pieces of rope tied together to tie the animal.)
O tulaale maa mali kamsi din nyaɣisa. (Her perfume has a sweet scent.)

english: which (third person singular inanimate relative pronoun, rare/archaic)
example: Soli kan viɛla, dina n-nyɛ so shɛli ti ni bɔra. (The path which is good, that is the way we want.)
O sa kana ti sani. (He came to us yesterday.)
O zaŋla kanaafiri n-niŋ tii maa ni ka di nyaɣisi. (She put cloves in the tea to make it tasty.)
Bihi maa diɛmdi la kanakana. (The children are playing on a seesaw.)

english: condiment made from the seed of 'rosella' (Hibiscus sabdariffa)
example: Bɛ zaŋla kananjuŋ n-niŋ ʒɛri maa ni. (They put rosella seed condiment in the soup.)

english: food made by a woman for a man on credit
example: O daa ti o kanchima ka o naan yi yo li. (She gave him food on credit which he later paid for.)

english: a type of pimple; a wart
example: Kandabili yi piri a nia, di bi viɛla. (If a wart grows on your hand, it's not good.)
From: (English).  O daa zaŋ kanɛlian n-niŋ o alaama ni. (He put a carnelian in his ring.)

english: what?; which?; who? (interrogative, placed after the word qualified)
example: A yuli n-nyɛ kani? (What is your name?)

english: an amulet for the arm or leg (to protect against weapons)
example: O zaŋla kani n-lo o nia ni bɛ di tooi ŋme o. (He put an arm amulet on so they couldn't strike him.)
Yuŋ ti zaŋla kania n-neeri ti duu. (At night we use a lantern to light our room.)
O kanjampini maa biɛya. (His calf is hurting.)

english: a rhinoceros (term used, though no rhinos in W. Africa)
example: Kantamparim nyɛla biŋkɔb' titali. (A rhinoceros is a large animal.)

english: 'maggi' (soup seasoning made from fermented kapok seeds)
example: Paɣa maa daa bɔri kantɔŋ n-niŋ ʒɛri maa ni. (The woman wanted kapok maggi to put in the soup.)
Kantɔŋ tia ŋɔ waɣa pam. (This kapok tree is very tall.)
O daa lo kante chinchina n-ti o ma. (He bought a kente cloth for his mother.)
Kantoo maa nyɛla din tɔɣu amaa ka di mali alaafee. (The bitter tomato is bitter but it's healthy.)

english: Kantoonsi (so-called Black Dagomba Muslim settlers in Dagbon)
example: Kantooŋa ŋɔ nyɛla ti ʒiʒiinikpee. (This Kantooŋa person is our neighbour.)

english: ruddy; dark red complexion; fair coloured (of persons)
example: O ningbuna nyɛla kanvaa ka di viɛla. (His complexion is fair and it's nice.)
O puu ni kaŋgbariŋga maa di pam. (The kaŋgbariŋga yams in his farm yielded a lot.)
O mali kaŋkpaɣila ka kom niŋ o ni. (He has a fever and feels hot.)
Bɛ daa zaŋ kaŋmɛri n-zahim kahu maa. (They used a grain-measure to measure the millet.)
Bɛ daa zaŋ kaŋwa n-niŋ taba maa ni. (They put saltpetre in the tobacco.)
Bɛ daa ʒi kapala na n-ti ti. (They brought the new grain for us.)
From: (English).  Kapinta maa mɛrila yili. (The carpenter is building a house.)
O kapirichi n-daa chɛ ka o lu. (His pride is what made him fall.)
Pl: kapirichilanima.  Kapirichilana ŋɔ je niriba. (This insolent person hates people.)

english: a type of soothsaying to find a wrongdoer; a bone cigarette holder
example: Bɛ daa niŋ kara n-ti nya ŋun zu binshɛɣu maa. (They performed soothsaying to find who stole the thing.)
From: (English).  Karachi maa sabirila gbana. (The clerk is writing letters.)
O daa nyu karakara maa zaa. (He drank all the light tomato soup.)
Karakoo maa yɛri la yɛltɔɣa kamani ninsala la. (The parrot is talking like a person.)
From: (English).  O zaŋla o karante n-che mɔri maa. (He used his cutlass to cut the grass.)
Kare! Di chaŋ nimaani. (Careful! Don't go there.)
O daa kari o puu maa gili ni bɛ di kpe. (He fenced his farm all round so they wouldn't enter.)

english: to chase; to drive away; to pursue; to dismiss; to divorce; to proscribe; to rebuke; to gallop
example: O daa kari baa maa bahi. (He chased the dog away.)
Noo maa karita dama o mali gala. (The hen is clucking because it has chicks.)
O daa lo kari n-ti o. (He put a curse on him.)
O daa zaŋ o bihi niŋ o mabia karifa. (He entrusted his children to his brother.)

english: a charm or spell to drive away ghosts
example: O daa bo kariga ni kpiimba di yi o nyaaŋa. (He got a charm so that ghosts wouldn't follow him.)

english: to be/get large; to fatten; to grow to required size (of fruit)
example: O bidibsi maa karigi pam. (His sons have grown big.)

english: to cut open (the stomach/belly); to tear (cloth)
example: Bɛ daa karigi o puli ni bɛ nya doro maa shee. (They cut open his stomach to find the site of the disease.)
O daa kɔhi karikaasi la o dakula ni. (She sold crackers in her shop.)
Binyɛra maa daa lu tiŋa ka di vuri mɔni karikari. (The things fell on the ground with a rattling crash.)

english: a large wooden needle for roofing; a harpoon
example: O zaŋla karikpandiga n-kpahi gbala maa. (He used the large needle to sew the mats.)

english: maize pounded and boiled and wrapped in leaves (like kenkey/banku)
example: Bɛ dira karili ni ʒɛri. (They eat the boiled maize meal with soup.)
Ninvuɣ' karili n-daa kana ti sani. (A big man came to us.)

english: to read; to study; to be at school/college
example: O karimdila kundi kam dali. (He reads a book every day.)
Pl: karimbanima.  Ti karimba wuhiri ti la baŋsim pam. (Our teacher teaches us a lot of knowledge.)
Pl: karimbaandaanima.  Karimbaandana ŋɔ je ni sokam ti o jilima. (This proud person wants everyone to respect him.)
O karimbaani n-daa saɣim o. (His pride ruined him.)
O karimbatali nyɛla din mali anfaani. (His work as a teacher is useful.)
Pl: karimbi'hi.  Karimbia maa bɔhimila baŋsim pam. (The student is learning a lot.)
Pl: karinjɛnima.  Karimbanima mini karinjɛnima n-daa be laɣiŋgu maa ni. (Teachers and important people were at the meeting.)

english: a big sore; serious sore (variant of kalinyaɣu)
example: O napɔŋ ni karinyaɣu maa bi tiri o suhudoo. (The big sore on his leg is not letting him rest.)
Pl: karinzɔna.  Bihi maa bela karinzɔŋ maa ni n-ti bɔhindi. (The children are in the classroom learning.)

english: reading; a reading/lesson/lecture; a chapter (in a book)
example: O kariŋ viɛla pam. (His reading is very good.)
Pl: kariŋbanima.  Kariŋba maa tiri ti la wuhibu pam. (The teacher gives us a lot of teaching.)
Kariŋbia maa nyɛla ŋun bɔhindi viɛnyɛla. (The student is one who learns well.)

english: a type of grass (used in a simile for a 'staff of reed')
example: Kariŋmaaŋa maa nyɛla din baligi pam. (That reed grass is very soft.)
O karitigu viɛla, o zooya n-gari sokam. (His running is good, he runs faster than everyone.)
Kariwampaɣu maa nyɛla zaɣ' ʒee. (The corn tassel is yellow.)
O zaŋla kariwandibili n-tu buɣim maa ni. (She used the corn-cob to add to the fire.)
Ti puu ni kariwani maa viɛla yuuni ŋɔ. (The maize in our farm is good this year.)
O chaŋla o kariwanlulle ni n-ti puri. (He went to his corn field to weed.)
Kariwannyia maa waɣa biɛla. (The dwarf maize is a bit short.)
Pl: kariwanpɔɣa.  Bɛ zaŋla kariwanpɔɣiri n-tu buɣim. (They used corn-husks to fuel the fire.)
Kariwanzum maa nyɛla din baligi pam. (The corn-silk is very soft.)

english: a type of capsicum/pepper (with upright branches)
example: O zaŋla kariwatia n-niŋ ʒɛri maa ni. (She put some of that pepper in the soup.)

english: a grass mat used to close up door openings
example: O daa zaŋ karo n-kpari dunoli maa. (He used a grass mat to close the doorway.)
Kasalli maa mɔɣiri kom pam. (The cactus stores a lot of water.)
O ŋɔhila kasana maa n-ti o. (She pulled out some millet stalks and gave them to him.)
Kasarili maa di pam yuuni ŋɔ. (The transplanted millet yielded a lot this year.)
O yili maa nyɛla din kasi pam. (His house is very clean.)
Bɛ daa zaŋ kasia n-niŋ tulaale maa ni. (They put cassia in the perfume.)
Kasiɣira maa daa gbi kabiri maa. (The gravedigger dug the grave.)
O di li kasikasi. (He ate it completely.)
From: (Arabic).  Kasitali nyɛla din simdi ti biɛhigu ni. (Cleanliness is important in our lives.)
Pl: kasitalilanima.  Kasitalilana ŋɔ jɛmdi la Naawuni dabisili kam. (This holy person worships God every day.)
O daa ti o katahili ni o di. (He gave him a bowl of millet to eat.)
From: (Hausa).  O daa nya katari n-ti o ma. (He got an opportunity to give to his mother.)

english: an umbrella; sun-shade; political party symbol (NDC)
example: Saa mi yɛn mi, ka o zaŋ katayua. (It's about to rain, so he took an umbrella.)

english: the third wife of the Yaan-Naa; her living quarters
example: Katini nyɛla ŋun mali jilima nayili ni. (The third wife is one who has respect in the palace.)
O yila katiŋa na. (He came from a far place.)
O puu ni katiŋguŋa maa di pam. (The cassava in his farm yielded a lot.)
Katu! N ku chaŋ. (Not at all! I won't go.)

english: a large wooden needle for roofing
example: O zaŋla katurigu n-kpahi gbala maa. (He used the large needle to sew the mats.)
O tum kavi tuma. (He did a shameless act.)
Kavini! Di lahi kana ti sani. (Go away! Don't come to us again.)
Kawaanpiɛlli maa nyɛla din baligi pam. (The white maize is very soft.)
O zaŋla kawaansana n-lo o kambɔŋ ni. (He stored the unshelled maize in his granary.)
Kawaanyia maa bi waɣa pam. (The dwarf maize is not very tall.)
Kawaanʒee maa nyɛla din mali alaafee pam. (Yellow maize is very healthy.)

english: Go away!; Be off! (variant of kavini)
example: Kawara! A bi yɛn ti ma laɣa. (Go away! You won't give me anything.)
From: (English).  O chaŋla kawunsila sani n-ti bɔhi o saawara. (He went to a counsellor to ask for advice.)
Kayeena maa yɛrila kahu maa. (The winnower is winnowing the millet.)
Kayeeni saha n-daa nyɛ li. (It was during winnowing time.)

english: the second growth of guinea corn
example: Kayɛlima maa yi na puu ni. (The second growth of guinea corn has come up in the farm.)
Paɣiba maa yihirila kayiha puu ni. (The women are gleaning in the farm.)

english: late millet crop; head of millet
example: Kayoli maa nyɛla din mali yaa. (The late millet crop is strong.)

english: self-sown cereal crop in an old field; scattered grain
example: Bɛ dira kayoo maa puu ni. (They are eating the self-sown grain in the farm.)

english: so-and-so much (a specific amount not mentioned)
example: O daa yo o liɣiri kaza. (He paid him so-and-so much money.)
Bɛ ʒinila kazala maa ni n-ti diri saɣim. (They are sitting on the veranda eating T.Z.)

english: the second wife of the Yaan-Naa
example: Kazia nyɛla ŋun sɔŋdi Katini pam. (The second wife is one who helps the third wife a lot.)

english: a head spike; ear of millet
example: Kazuɣu maa pali ni kabiɛlli. (The ear of millet is full of grains.)
Bɛ zaŋla kahu maa niŋ kaʒee ni. (They put the millet in the temporary grain-store.)
Bɛ zaŋla kaʒiɛɣu n-to burukutu. (They use sorghum to make pito.)
From: (English) "cupboard".  O zaŋla kɔba maa ni n-zaŋ o nɛma. (He used the cupboard to put his things.)
O gbaai kɔbaa kuliga ni. (He caught a 'water-dog' fish in the stream.)
O zaŋla kɔbahiga n-ʒi kom na. (She used a water-container to bring water.)

english: a decorated pot used for water for chiefs
example: Bɛ zaŋla kɔbaŋa n-ti Naa maa kom. (They used the special pot to give the chief water.)
O zaŋla kɔbɔbiga n-lo wahu maa. (He used a saddle-girth to fasten the horse's saddle.)
Bɛ nyaŋdi la kom kɔbɔɣili ni. (They get water from the waterhole.)

english: to take; to steal/capture; to catch a disease
example: O daa kɔbi doro maa. (He caught the sickness.)

english: hundred; ¢00 (00 'pounds'); a fine; a penny (00 cowries)
example: O daa ti o liɣiri kɔbiga. (He gave him one hundred cedis.)
O mali niɣi kɔbiga. (He has one hundred cows.)
Ti mali bihi kɔbiga ni anahi. (We have one hundred and four children.)
O mali piɛri kɔbiga ni anii. (He has one hundred and eight sheep.)
Bɛ daa yo liɣiri kɔbiga ni anu. (They paid one hundred and five cedis.)
O da la niɣi kɔbiga ni ata. (He bought one hundred and three cows.)
Bɛ daa kana ni niriba kɔbiga ni awɛi. (One hundred and nine people came.)
O mali yuun' kɔbiga ni ayɔbu. (He is one hundred and six years old.)
Bɛ daa laɣim kɔbiga ni ayɔpɔin. (They gathered one hundred and seven.)
Bɛ daa kana ni niriba kɔbiga ni ayi. (One hundred and two people came.)
O da la bindira kɔbiga ni pia. (He bought one hundred and ten food items.)
O yuun' nyɛla kɔbiga ni pia ni anahi. (He is one hundred and fourteen years old.)
O mali laɣiri kɔbiga ni pia ni anii. (He has one hundred and eighteen cedis.)
Bɛ daa laɣim kɔbiga ni pia ni anu. (They gathered one hundred and fifteen.)
Ti mali bihi kɔbiga ni pia ni ata. (We have one hundred and thirteen children.)
Bɛ daa kɔ puu kɔbiga ni pia ni awɛi. (They farmed one hundred and nineteen farms.)
O da la piɛri kɔbiga ni pia ni ayɔbu. (He bought one hundred and sixteen sheep.)
Bɛ daa kana ni niriba kɔbiga ni pia ni ayɔpɔin. (One hundred and seventeen people came.)
Bɛ daa yo o liɣiri kɔbiga ni pia ni ayi. (They paid him one hundred and twelve cedis.)
O mali niɣi kɔbiga ni pia ni yini. (He has one hundred and eleven cows.)
O mali bihi kɔbiga ni pishi. (She has one hundred and twenty children.)
Bɛ daa yo liɣiri kɔbiga ni pishi ni yini. (They paid one hundred and twenty-one cedis.)
O mali laɣiri kɔbiga ni yini. (He has one hundred and one cedis.)
Bɛ daa kana ni niriba kɔbigaayika. (Ninety-eight people came.)
O daa yo liɣiri kɔbigayinika. (He paid ninety-nine cedis.)
Bɛ daa kana ni niriba kɔbigayinika. (Ninety-nine people came.)
O kɔbigu maa waɣa pam. (His hair is very long.)

english: water mixed with porridge and sugar for drinking
example: O daa nyu kɔbihirili maa zaa. (He drank all the sweetened porridge water.)
Baa maa dira kɔbili maa. (The dog is eating the bone.)

english: (one's own) effort; strength; means (by means of...)

english: iron; metal; a flat gold pendant; time/o'clock (the hour)
example: Bɛ zaŋla kurugu n-mɛ li. (They used iron to build it.)
From: (English) via (Twi?).  Bɛ zaŋla kurupooti n-to buɣim. (They used a charcoal brazier to make fire.)

english: an enamel bowl; measuring-bowl (standard measure for grains)
example: O zaŋla kuruwa n-zahim kahu maa. (She used a measuring-bowl to measure the millet.)
O zaŋla kuruwabila n-ti bia maa bindirigu. (She used a small bowl to give the child food.)
Pl: kuruwɔɣa.  Bɛ zaŋla kuruwɔɣila n-kpari dunoya maa. (They used iron bars to lock the doors.)
O zaŋla o kushia n-ko puu. (He used his small hoe to farm.)
Kusumbuli maa ŋmɛ la gabiga. (The tarantula spun a web.)

english: a Muslim sermon (preaching by Imam at Friday prayers)
example: Bɛ wumila kutuba jiŋli ni. (They listened to the sermon at the mosque.)

english: a messenger sent to announce a death
example: Bɛ timla kutumo n-ti baŋsi ba kuli maa. (They sent a messenger to inform them of the death.)

english: measurement (a part of a span, e.g., half-span)
example: O zaŋla kutunyɔŋ n-zahim li. (He used a half-span to measure it.)
O yo la kutunyoo maa. (He paid the fee.)

english: to dry/get dry; to dry something; to be stingy/mean
example: Nɛma maa kuu ya wuntaŋa ni. (The clothes dried in the sun.)
Bɛ zaŋla kuuka n-niŋ ʒɛri ni. (They put dried baobab leaves in the soup.)
Tiŋgbani maa nyɛla kuuni. (The ground is dry.)

english: cooked yams (eaten with pepper, salt, and shea butter)
example: Bɛ dira kuyɔŋyɔŋ ni kpam. (They are eating cooked yams with shea butter.)

english: a 'funeral-house'; place where a funeral custom is being performed
example: Bɛ chaŋla kuyili maa ni. (They went to the funeral house.)
Bɛ yiinila kuyilli kuli maa ni. (They are singing a dirge at the funeral.)

english: a partly worn-out hoe (variant of kushia)
example: O kuʒia maa bi lahi ya. (His worn-out hoe is no longer sharp.)

english: a sickness that affects the waist
example: O mali kuʒiɛm ka chɛ ka o bi tooi yiɣisi viɛnyɛla. (He has a waist sickness which stops him getting up well.)

english: to laugh; to laugh at/deride; to break open (as a seed pod)
example: Bihi maa la la pam bɛ ni daa nya kpɛrigu maa. (The children laughed a lot when they saw the clown.)
O malila laɣifu pam. (He HAS a lot of money.) [emphasis on the verb 'mali']

english: the; that (definite article for new information); those in addition; particle indicating termination of dependent clause
example: N daa nya doo la ka o chanila n sani na. (I saw the man and he was coming towards me.)

english: last- (e.g., last year) (following a time expression)
example: Yuuni la, ti daa kɔ la kahu pam. (Last year, we planted a lot of millet.)
O zaŋ la laa maa n-ti o saɣim. (She used the bowl to give him T.Z.)

english: male- (suffix for certain animals; also for high pitch/large size)
example: O gbaai la kɔɣu laa. (He caught a male roan antelope.)

english: male (suffix used with certain animal names)
example: Bɛ da la Piɛdaa maa daa ni. (They bought the ram at the market.)

english: There is no God but Allah! (Islamic interjection, various forms)
example: O yɛli la "Laa ilaha illala" ka ti Naawuni. (He said 'There is no God but Allah' and submitted to God.)

english: a valued yam cultivar (variant of laribako)
example: Ti dira laabako Zuŋɔ. (We are eating laabako yams today.)

english: KaMara (residents of Larabanga, early Muslim settlers in Dagbon)
example: Laabaŋa maa nyɛla ban daŋ kana Dagbɔŋ ni. (The KaMara people were among the first to come to Dagbon.)
Laabira, o kana mi. (Truly, he came.)
From: (English).  Laafiri maa fe mi ka o bi nya li. (The referee was cheated and didn't see it.)
Laakali yini maa nyari binshɛɣu kam? (Does the one-eyed person see everything?)
Laakumbara maa ʒi laakum maa zuɣu. (The camel-rider is sitting on the camel.)
Bɛ zaŋ la laakumi n-ʒi nɛma timsa maa. (They used a camel to carry the heavy loads.)

english: Goodness me! (expression of surprise); don't want trouble
example: Laalaa! A niŋ la nyaɣisim pam. (Goodness me! You look very nice.)
O tuma maa nyɛla laalaata. (His action was shameful.)

english: to float/soar; to swing around/sway; to move aside
example: Fɔŋtɔɣu maa laali la zuɣusaa. (The kite is soaring up in the sky.)
Bɛ niŋ la laali titali zaŋ chaŋ piibu piibu. (They held a big rally for the elections.)

english: a plant species (Bengal bean, Mucuna aterrima); any substance that can cause irritation
example: Laam maa mali tim din tooi nyɔri. (The Bengal bean plant has medicine that can sting.)
Bihi laamba kana ti nya ba. (The children's parents came to see them.)
Pl: laandaanima.  Laandana maa doli la o dari n-chana. (The blind person is walking with his stick.)

english: a guinea fowl (possibly bush guinea fowl)
example: O gbaai la laanigu mɔɣu ni. (He caught a bush guinea fowl.)

english: a reward/prize; tribute received; rental money received; (in phrase 'Naawuni ni ti laara' = Thanks)
example: O nya la laara o tuma viɛnyɛla tumbu zuɣu. (He got a reward for doing his work well.)

english: a reckoning/accounting; a number/total of; a type/kind; reasoning; divination; useless/not included (in negative)
example: O mali la laasabu n-gbaari o liɣiri. (He does his accounts to check his money.)
Laasabuniŋda maa chaŋ ti buɣisi ti baɣa. (The soothsayer went to divine for us.)
From: (English).  O zaŋ la laati n-neei duu maa. (He used a torch to light the room.)
Bindirigu ŋɔ nyɛla labalaba, di nyɛla din di naai. (This food is soft, it is well-cooked.)

english: a half-piece of cloth; a half (of anything)
example: O zaŋ la labi maa n-she o chinchini maa. (He used the half-piece of cloth to patch his smock.)

english: to face; to turn in the direction of; to be opposite
example: Yili maa labiri la wulimpuhili polo. (The house faces east.)

english: to return/go back; to retreat; to do something again (auxiliary) (Western dialect)
example: O labi la o yiŋa zuŋɔ Asiba. (He returned home this morning.)

english: to move (someone/something) in the direction of...
example: Bɛ labisi la kuɣa maa n-ti naa maa. (They moved the stools for the chief.)

english: to return something/give back; to bring back; to retaliate/revenge; to reply/answer
example: O labisi ma la n liɣiri maa. (He gave me back my money.)

english: to court; to seduce/have intercourse with (for money); to make a cross mark to ward off evil
example: O chaŋ ti laɣi o paɣa maa. (He went to court his wife.)
O zaŋ la kpam maa n-laɣi o nuhi. (He smeared the oil on his hands.)
O daa ti o laɣibaligu pia ka o zaŋ da bindirigu. (He gave him ten coins to buy food.)
O laɣidaya maa zooi pam, ka o mali li kɔhiri nɛma. (His capital is large, and he uses it to trade goods.)
O ba tiri o la laɣidira dabsili kam. (His father gives him chop money every day.)

english: a cowry; a coin; money (plural liɣiri)
example: O zaŋ la laɣifu kɔbiga n-da kpaŋ maa. (He used one hundred cowries to buy the guinea fowl.)
Bɛ piigi o laɣigula n-ti ti kpamba. (They appointed him as treasurer for the elders.)
O lo la laɣikpahirili n-ti o bia. (He threaded a cowry and gave it to his child.)
O chinchini palli maa nyɛla laɣilaɣi. (His new smock is sparkling white.)

english: to gather together/assemble/meet; to call together/convene; to join in an activity/do together; to have (sexual) intercourse
example: Bɛ zaa laɣim la nayili dundɔŋ ni. (They all gathered in the palace courtyard.)
Bɛ laɣim niŋ saawara n-ti yɛli bɛ ni yɛn niŋ binshɛɣu n-ti kuli maa. (They held a meeting together to decide what to do for the funeral.)
O mali laɣima pam ka bɔri ni o kɔhi nɛma. (He has a lot of capital and wants to trade goods.)
Bɛ chaŋ ti nya laɣinamda maa ni o ti sɔŋ ba. (They went to see the money conjurer to help them.)
Bɛ boŋ la laɣiŋgu maa zaa yiŋa. (They called the whole group to the house.)
Bɛ gbaai laɣiŋmara maa dama o nama laɣifu. (They caught the counterfeiter because he was making fake money.)
O chaŋ laɣipaŋda maa sani n-ti paŋ liɣiri. (He went to the moneylender to borrow money.)
O zaŋ la laɣipaŋda maa n yo o paɣa sara. (He used the borrowed money to pay his wife's bride-price.)
O daa ti o laɣipiɛliga ka o chaŋ da bindirigu. (He gave him some cash to go and buy food.)
Laɣipiɛliga maa ʒi la naɣi maa zuɣu. (The cattle-egret is sitting on the cow.)
O laɣisɔŋbu viɛla, o mali laɣiri pam. (His saving is good, he has a lot of money.)
Bɛ piigi o laɣisɔŋda n-ti laɣiŋgu maa. (They appointed him as treasurer for the group.)
O laɣisi la o bihi maa zaa n-ti ba saawara. (He gathered all his children to advise them.)

english: to collect money (e.g., for a contribution)
example: Bɛ laɣisi liɣiri n-ti paɣa ŋun dɔɣi maa. (They collected money for the woman who gave birth.)
Laɣitaɣira maa taɣiri la laɣ' balibu daa ni. (The money-changer exchanges different currencies at the market.)
O laɣiwolinda maa zooi, ka o tooi kɔhi nɛma pam. (His trading capital is large, so he can sell many goods.)
Laɣiyubo n-daa chɛ ka o zu o kpee liɣiri. (The love of money is what made him steal his neighbour's money.)

english: a lover of money; an avaricious person
example: Pl: laɣiyuranima.  Laɣiyura ŋɔ bi yɛn niŋ nambɔɣu n-ti so. (This avaricious person won't show mercy to anyone.)
O laɣizuɣu maa daa saɣim o daa ni. (His capital got finished in the market.)
O ningbuna ka lahandi. (His body has no blemish.)
O lahi kana ti sani. (He also came to us.)
A wum la lahibali palli maa? (Have you heard the new story?)
A wum la lahibali zaŋ chaŋ Naa maa kuli maa? (Have you heard the news concerning the chief's funeral?)
O chaŋ jiŋli lahisari saha. (He went to the mosque at lahisari time.)
Bɛ zani la lai ni bɛ ti da bindirigu. (They stood in a queue to buy food.)
O zaŋ la lakɔrigu n-ti bia maa saɣim. (She used a clay bowl to give the child T.Z.)

english: so/thus/in that way; that/the aforementioned (as modifier); this situation (as subject)
example: O tum la lala ka di viɛla. (He did it like that and it was good.)
Lala zuɣu, ti zaa tu ni ti guli ti maŋa. (Because of this, we all must take care of ourselves.)

english: to glide/hover/float; to swing from/dangle; to die (euphemism)
example: Fɔŋtɔɣu maa lali la zuɣusaa. (The kite is gliding up in the sky.)
O nyɛla n laliga, ti lɛri la pam. (He is my joking-partner, we joke a lot.)

english: to distance oneself/get away; to escape from
example: O laligi ka chɛ biɛrisuŋ maa. (He distanced himself from the bad company.)
O nia laligi ya o ni daa lu maa. (His hand got grazed when he fell.)
O shihi la lam maa ka di mɔri o. (He touched the itching powder and it's making him itch.)

english: to eat the first-fruits of a crop; to sample/try/taste; to let someone sample
example: O daŋ lam bindirigu maa pɔi ka naan yi ti bihi maa. (He tasted the food first before giving it to the children.)

english: to taste the food (e.g., to check seasoning)
example: Paɣa maa lam bindirigu maa n-ti nya di ni tɔɣi bee di bi tɔɣi. (The woman tasted the food to see if it was tasty or not.)

english: a gap in a series/row (e.g., teeth or grains on a cob)
example: O nyina mali lama. (His teeth have gaps.)

english: a wattle of a cock; the gum (of the mouth) without the teeth
example: Nolɔɣu maa lamli nyɛla zaɣ' ʒee. (The rooster's wattle is red.)

english: a sample of food; a taster; a 'dash'
example: O ti o la lamni ka o lam bindirigu maa. (He gave him a taster to try the food.)
Bɛ yo la lampoo yuuni kam. (They pay tax every year.)
O lan bɔra ni o chaŋ. (He also wants to go.)

english: owner/'owner'; person of/with something; (plural) parents/guardians; master/mistress (of a slave)
example: Yili maa lana n-daa kana. (The owner of the house came.)
O nyɛla yurilimlana. (He is a loving person.)
Lana ni o bia chaŋ daa ni n-ti da nɛma. (The parent and child went to the market to buy things.)
Pl: landaanima.  Landana maa moli la jiŋli Asiba kam. (The muezzin calls to the mosque every morning.)

english: a bridle (metal/leather/rope for a horse's face)
example: O zaŋ la langami n-lɔ wahu maa. (He used a bridle to control the horse.)
Bɛ daa ja bua lana maa. (They castrated the goat.)
Lanjam tia ŋɔ mali tim pam. (This shrub has a lot of medicinal properties.)

english: a tree species (Burkea africana) used for charcoal
example: Bɛ zaŋ la lanjiŋa n-to buɣim. (They use lanjiŋa wood to make charcoal.)

english: an old man married to a young girl (lit. 'balls-won't-thrust')
example: Lankudaai maa kuli la bipuɣiŋga. (The old man married a young girl.)
Bɛ kɔrigi la lantabili maa n-ti sara. (They slaughtered the castrated animal for sacrifice.)

english: a casting net; a net used to wrap loads on the head
example: O labi la laŋa maa kuliga ni. (He cast the net into the stream.)
O nyɛla doo laŋalaŋa. (He is a lanky man.)

english: banding-metal; 'langalanga' (long strips of iron binding bales) (from Hausa)
example: Bɛ zaŋ la laŋalaŋa n-lo nɛma maa. (They used banding-metal to tie the goods.)

english: a disease with swellings; a large fruit of a certain tree used for medicine
example: O mali laŋgandili o niŋgbuŋ ni. (He has swellings on his body.)

english: a person with only one testicle; a man who is infertile (not impotent)
example: Pl: laŋgɔbihi.  Laŋgɔbili maa bi tooi dɔɣi bihi. (The infertile man cannot father children.)
Laŋgirindoo maa zooi mɔɣu ni. (That grass species grows in the bush.)
Kom maa laŋsira di ni daa zo n-yina maa. (The water was splashing as it flowed out.)
O lari maa wum ya zaa. (His laughter was heard by all.)
O puu ni laribako maa di pam. (The laribako yams in his farm yielded a lot.)
From: (Hausa) < (Arabic).  Laribu so n-daa kana tiŋa ŋɔ ni n-ti kɔhi nɛma. (An Arab came to this town to sell goods.)
O chaŋ ti ʒini Laribu. (He went to live in Arabia.)
O sɔɣi la lariga ni. (He hid in a nook.)
Chɛli ya larigim be yi mini o sunsuuni. (Leave a space between you and him.)
O larim la taŋkpaɣu o nɛma maa ni. (He besmeared his clothes with dust.)
Lariŋga maa ʒi tia zuɣu n-dira. (The squirrel is sitting on the tree eating.)
O nyɛla bipuɣiŋga lariŋga. (She is a slim but smart young woman.)
From: (English).  O chaŋ ti ʒini Laundani. (He went to live in London.)
O zaŋ la lɔba n-ʒi kom. (She used a plastic container to carry water.)

english: to throw; to throw/cast away (Western dialect, corresponds to 'labi' in Eastern)
example: O lɔbi la kuɣili maa. (He threw the stone.)

english: to move stealthily/go round stooping/creep about; to evade; (in phrase lɔɣi bɔɣili 'perform first stage of funeral')
example: O lɔɣi chaŋ n-ti sɔɣi. (He crept away and hid.)

english: a shield (plural form, possibly singular lɔɣibuli)
example: Tɔbbihi maa zaŋ la bɛ lɔɣibuya n-taɣi bɛ maŋa. (The warriors used their shields to protect themselves.)
O lɔɣikpia n-daa chɛ ka niriba zaa je o. (His cantankerousness made everyone hate him.)

english: the final observances of the Dagomba funeral custom
example: Bɛ niŋ la lɔɣiri n-ti o ba. (They performed the final funeral rites for his father.)

english: to remember (usually with negative 'bi lɔɣisi' = didn't remember)
example: M bi lɔɣisi n-ti o lahibali maa. (I didn't remember to tell him the news.)

english: to be eroded; to be rutted (as a road)
example: Saa maa lɔɣisi la soli maa. (The rain rutted the road.)
O zaŋ la lɔɣu n-ʒi kom na. (She used a large pot to bring water.)

english: male- (suffix for certain animals, variant of 'laa)
example: Bɛ kɔrigi la piɛɣu lɔɣu maa. (They slaughtered the male sheep.)
Bɛ zaŋ la lɔɣu n-niŋ o bindirigu ni. (They put poison in his food.)
O zaŋ la o piɛma niŋ lɔɣu ni. (He put his arrows in the quiver.)
O zaŋ la lɔha n-kɔɣi dam maa. (She used the fermenting-agent to brew the pito.)

english: to move from place to place; to spring from tree to tree (of disease, fire, animals)
example: Doro maa lɔhiri niriba pam. (The disease is spreading to many people.)

english: to soak in water; to submerge; to cook whole
example: O lɔhi la shinkaafa maa kom ni. (He soaked the rice in water.)

english: to tell a riddle/parable; to make a word-play
example: O lɔhi la salinli n-ti bihi maa. (He told a riddle to the children.)

english: a riddle; word-play; parable (in phrase saliŋ' lɔhili)
example: O baŋ la lɔhili pam. (He knows many riddles.)
O zaŋ la lɔhirili n-kɔɣi dam maa. (She used the fermenting-agent to brew the pito.)
O niŋ o noli lɔk. (He kept his mouth shut.)

english: a pillared area; courtyard; portico; a small yard within a large yard
example: Bɛ ʒinila lɔndo maa ni n-ti di saɣim. (They sat in the pillared courtyard to eat T.Z.)

english: the throat/inner throat/gullet; voice; a hollow place/enclosed area
example: O lɔŋ kuui ya konyuri zuɣu. (His throat is dry from thirst.)

english: a frog (in proverbs, contrasted with pololi 'toad')
example: Bihi maa gbahi la lɔna kuliga ni. (The children caught frogs in the stream.)

english: to filter/strain; to gag/choke/make a rattling sound in the throat during vomiting
example: O lɔŋ la kom maa ni salima. (He strained the water with a cloth.)
Kom maa nyɛla lɔŋalɔŋa. (The water is brackish.)
Bɛ zaŋ la lɔŋchi n-gari mɔɣili maa. (They used a ferry to cross the river.)
O lɔŋgbɔɣu maa nyɛla din yi shɛli. (His Adam's apple is prominent.)
O lɔŋkpilli maa biɛya. (His larynx hurts.)

english: in under/under/beneath something enclosing; at the foot of (a hill) (locative noun + ni)
example: Bɛ sɔɣi la tampima lɔŋni. (They hid under the rocks.)
Kom maa nyɛla lɔŋolɔŋɔ, di bi ni tooi nyu. (The water is bitter, you can't drink it.)

english: hoarseness; sore throat; loss of voice
example: O mali lɔŋshiliŋ ka o kukoli taɣi. (He has a sore throat and his voice has changed.)

english: the day of preparing quivers (part of funeral rites)
example: Lɔri bɔbu dali, bɛ laɣim mi n-ti yɛli kuli maa yɛla. (On the day of preparing the quivers, they gather to discuss the funeral matters.)
O sɔɣi la lɔriga ni. (He hid in a corner.)

english: to untie; (in phrases lɔrigi noli 'end a fast', lɔrigi alikauli 'annul a promise', lɔrigi amilia 'decree a divorce'); to be successful (in phrase lɔrigi daa 'trading is going well')
example: O lɔrigi o bɔbigu maa. (She untied her head-tie.)
O ni daa bi tum tuun' maa, di ŋmanila o lɔrigi la o alikauli. (Since he didn't do the work, it's like he broke his promise.)

english: to break one's fast/to eat after fasting
example: Wuntaŋa lu naai, ka bɛ lɔrigi noli. (After sunset, they broke their fast.)
Lɔsbidi, o bi mi shɛli. (Truly, he knows nothing.)

english: to feed a baby; to pour a liquid (slowly) into a vessel with a small opening
example: O le la bia maa bihim. (She fed the baby milk.)

english: pray...?; ...exactly...? (particle expressing impatience, concern, or asking for detailed answer)
example: Ŋuni lee n-nyɛ o? (Who exactly is he?)
O bi chaŋ, o lee kpalim ya. (He didn't go, he rather stayed.)
From: (English).  O wumdi la lahibaya leedio ni. (He listens to the news on the radio.)

english: a straw basket with a cover; 'bottom drawer' (basket containing dowry clothes)
example: O zaŋ la o nɛma niŋ leefe maa ni. (She put her things in the covered basket.)

english: to become/happen; to turn (something/someone) into; to install/enskin a chief/crown/inaugurate/appoint; to translate
example: O leei la Naa. (He became chief.)

english: an orange or other citrus fruit
example: From: (English) 'lemon'.  O dira leemu. (He is eating an orange.)
Leenkɔha maa kɔhiri la leemu daa ni. (The orange-seller sells oranges at the market.)

english: the orange-market (corner of the market where orange-sellers trade)
example: O chaŋ ti da leemu leenkɔhigɔni. (She went to buy oranges at the orange-market corner.)

english: a 'shade-porch'; a stall (erected shed); a small shelter
example: Bɛ ʒinila leeŋa maa gbini ka diri saɣim. (They are sitting under the shade-porch eating T.Z.)
From: (English).  O zaŋ la leeza n-pini o zabiri. (He used a razor to shave his hair.)

english: to become; to turn (self/something); to turn something into/make into; to turn something over/inside-out
example: O lɛbi ya doo titali. (He has become a big man.)
O ni daa nya o dim' maa, o lɛbi la o nyaaŋa n-zo. (When he saw his enemy, he turned his back and ran.)
O lɛbiɛlim maa bi lahi ya. (His adze blade is no longer sharp.)

english: to become/change into/turn into; to transform/disguise oneself; to change (someone/something) into; to translate/interpret/copy; to be smart/quick; to develop
example: O lɛbigi la o nia. (He changed his mind.)

english: to turn something upside down; to turn inside out; to take by surprise/upset/confuse; (in phrase X zuɣu lɛbigi = X is crazy)
example: O lɛbigi la o nɛma maa zaa. (He turned all his things upside down looking.)
O daa bɔra ni o chaŋ, amaa o lɛbigi o suhu. (He wanted to go, but he changed his mind.)
O lɛbigi yɛltɔɣa maa n-ti samba maa. (He interpreted the speech for the visitors.)
Tiŋa ŋɔ lɛbiginsim nyɛla din nyaɣisa. (The development of this town is good.)
O zaŋ la lɛbila n-ŋme o. (He hit him with a club.)
Bɛ zaŋ la lɛbira n-tum tuun' kpeeni maa. (They hired a labourer to do the hard work.)
Kapinta maa zaŋ la lɛbuli n-zahim di viɛnyɛla. (The carpenter used a level to measure it properly.)

english: the sacrifice offered at Chimsi (the festival sheep/animal)
example: Bɛ kɔrigi la lɛha piɛɣu maa Chimsi ni. (They slaughtered the sacrificial sheep at Chimsi.)

english: to replace; to fill in gaps where plants have failed to grow; to cut second teeth (of a child)

english: to squeeze; to hold someone/something tight
example: O mirisi o nuu. (He squeezed his hand.)
Di wɔɣilim nyɛla mita anu. (Its length is five metres.)
Bɛ zaŋ la mitil tihi n-daa. (They bought myrtle trees.)
Bɛ niŋ la mitiŋ n-ti yɛli yɛla. (They held a meeting to discuss matters.)

english: a striped red and white native cloth (lit. 'red thread')
example: O lo la miʒee chinchini. (He bought a red and white striped cloth.)
O bɔhi o, ka o yɛli "Mm". (He asked him, and he said 'Yes'.)

english: no; wrong; that is not so (exclamation, antonym of mm)
example: O bɔhi o, ka o yɛli "M'm". (He asked him, and he said 'No'.)

english: to wrestle; to contest/strive for/try to attain; (in phrases mo Naawuni 'blaspheme', saa mɔra 'the rain is threatening')
example: Bɛ mo la taba. (They wrestled each other.)

english: a puddle (in phrase saa mokɔro 'rainwater puddle')
example: Saa mokɔro maa ʒiya soli zuɣu. (The rainwater puddle is on the road.)

english: a white Fulani breed of cow (or cross with it)
example: O puu ni mɔlaa maa di pam. (His white Fulani cow produced a lot.)
O moli la dari maa n-ti yili maa. (He put the rafters on the house.)

english: to proclaim/be proclaimed; to beat drums in announcement; to broadcast; to preach; to give the adhdhan (Muslim call to prayer)
example: Landana maa moli la jiŋli. (The muezzin called to prayer.)
Bɛ niŋ la moli. (They had a wrestling match.)
O zaŋ la moli maa n-ti piɛm maa. (He used the shaft for the arrow.)
Molidibiga maa mali yaa pam. (The male kob is very strong.)

english: a type of felt hat worn by some Muslim men; a thick woollen hat (usually long)
example: O lo la molifee. (He put on a felt hat.)

english: an antelope species (kob, Kobus kob); Thompson's gazelle (Eudorcas thomsonii)
example: O gbaai la molifu mɔɣu ni. (He caught a kob in the bush.)
O mali moliga pam. (He has a lot of strength.)
O moliga maa piri ya. (His arrow-shaft is broken.)

english: a proclamation (usually with a drum); preaching (Church/Bible usage)
example: Bɛ moli la molo nayili ni. (They made a proclamation at the palace.)

english: a herald; evangelist; proclaimer; (in phrase lahibal' suŋ molomoona 'an evangelist')
example: Molomoona maa moli la lahibali suŋ. (The herald proclaimed the good news.)
O nyɛla ninvuɣ' mom. (He is a quiet person.)

english: a fresh grass-stalk; green pasture ('fresh grass')
example: Niɣi maa dira momahili. (The cows are eating fresh grass.)

english: to stalk; to walk quietly to avoid attention or notice
example: O momsi chaŋ n-ti sɔɣi. (He stalked away and hid.)
Moo so n-daa kana tiŋa ŋɔ ni. (A Mossi person came to this town.)

english: to carry/fetch fire (a glowing stick or coal to use to light a fire)
example: O mooi la buɣim na n-ti to buɣim. (He fetched fire to light the fire.)

english: to ripen; to be ripe (of fruit); to be red; to redden/make something red; (in phrases mooi nini/nini mooi 'be serious', mooi nini bahi X zuɣu 'to gloat over X')
example: Tiwala maa mooi ya. (The fruits have ripened.)
Bindira maa nyɛla mooni. (The food is ripe.)

english: reddening; red dye; redness; a crop used as a black or red dye
example: Bɛ zaŋ la moonsi n-niŋ gbaŋ maa ni. (They put red dye on the leather.)

english: pan for carrying fire (in biblical context, pl. moorisi)
example: Bɛ zaŋ la moorisi n-mooi buɣim. (They used a pan to carry fire.)
O moosim maa viɛla. (Her fair complexion is nice.)

english: a game type (tossing cowries into a distant hole; lit. 'I have reached')
example: Bihi maa diɛmdi la mpaaya. (The children are playing the cowry-tossing game.)
O daa kali la m-pɔi. (He counted seven.)

english: to be narrow; to be tight (as clothes)/make something tight; to throng/crowd; to be crowded/be pressed; (in phrase X mini Y muɣi 'X and Y are at loggerheads')
example: Soli maa muɣi pam. (The path is very narrow.)
O nyɔ' muɣili. (His breathing is painful.)
O siturili maa nyɛla muɣiliŋga. (His shirt is tight.)
O kalinli nyɛla muɣim. (His count is zero.)
Di kpalim muɣimuɣi. (It remained absolutely nothing.)

english: riding-boots (mainly used as part of parade and dancing costume)
example: O zaŋ la muɣiri n-pahi o kparigu zuɣu. (He put riding-boots on with his smock.)

english: to constrict/pressurise/compel/persuade; to oppress/persecute; to trouble/harm/cause pain; to punish
example: O muɣisi o ka o chaŋ. (He compelled him to go.)

english: a charm/spell/love potion; charm/good personality; gracefulness/good bearing (from Hausa < Arabic)
example: O bo la muhibba n-ti o. (He got a love potion for himself.)

english: a medicine type (herbs ground and rolled into balls/cones)
example: O zaŋ la muhili n-ti o. (He gave him herbal medicine.)

english: a skirt; (under-)skirt (a piece of cloth with strings attached, worn about the waist of women)
example: O lo la mukuru. (She put on a skirt.)
Mulichi maa daa laɣim ya. (The crowd gathered.)

english: a tall red hat (type of fez) (variant of molifee)
example: O lo la mulifeu. (He put on a tall red hat.)

english: red (felt) cloth; a tall red hat (type)
example: O lo la mulifu chinchini. (He bought a red felt cloth.)

english: to purse (e.g., lips)/be protruding; to form into cylindrical shape
example: O mulim o noli. (He pursed his lips.)

english: a 'roll-up'; tobacco rolled in paper for smoking; a cigar
example: O nyula mulimula. (He is smoking a cigar.)

english: round (as a log of wood)/cylindrical/tubular; complete/absolute (adjective)
example: Dari maa nyɛla mulinli. (The wood is cylindrical.)
Di nyɛla muliŋga. (It is cylindrical.)

english: a disease type (makes sores in the anus)
example: O mali muliŋme. (He has sores in his anus.)

english: to be square; to be angular
example: Di mulisi ya. (It has become square.)
O mulisi o maŋa. (He disguised himself.)
O mulitshi zaa be yiŋa. (All his dependants are at home.)

english: to shut something (particularly mouth)/shut up/stop crying; to bury/close over; (in serial verbs nyɛ m-mum 'to grow over', daai mum 'to cover up')
example: Mum a noli! (Shut your mouth!)

english: a tree species (white hog plum tree, Spondias mombin/purpurea); the fruit of this tree
example: Mumɔɣili tia ŋɔ wala pam. (This hog plum tree has a lot of fruit.)

english: slander; an informer/slanderer/secret accuser; a secret accusation (always detrimental)
example: O niŋ o munaafichi. (He slandered him.)
Pl: munaafichilanima.  Munaafichilana ŋɔ wurindi la niriba yuya. (This slanderer ruins people's names.)
Pl: munaafiɣisiranima.  Munaafiɣisira ŋɔ bi diri yɛlimaŋli. (This slanderer doesn't speak truth.)
O nya li la munaali. (He got it undeservedly.)
Di nyɛla munaalim n-ti o. (It is an undeserved recompense for him.)

english: a sunni Muslim; or follower of Afa Ajura
example: O nyɛla munchiri. (He is a sunni Muslim.)

english: stump/lower trunk of a tree/main body; piece of timber/lumber
example: Tia maa mundili kpalim ya. (The stump of the tree remains.)
O di la dabisi muni puuni. (He ate the whole day.)
O yo o la dabisi' munimuni. (He paid him a full day's wage.)

english: to leak (of grain from a container)
example: Kahu maa muri la kambɔŋ ni. (The millet is leaking from the granary.)

english: a dry reed/piece of straw/stalk; a rushlight (dried reed used as a torch); a tiniest bit/least bit of... (with negative)
example: O ka ya' muriga. (He hasn't the least bit of strength.)

english: a short piece of reed (used as a spill or taper)
example: O zaŋ la murijiŋa n-to buɣim. (He used a reed taper to light the fire.)
Mus maa ʒi tia zuɣu. (The cat is sitting on the tree.)
From: (Hausa) < (Arabic).  Mushiiba n-daa lu tiŋa ŋɔ ni. (Calamity fell on this town.)
O zaŋ la salima musimusi n-niŋ bindirigu ni. (She put a sprinkle of salt in the food.)
From: (Hausa) < (Arabic).  Musulimi maa chaŋ la jiŋli. (The Muslim went to the mosque.)

english: a type of clove used as a spice
example: O zaŋ la musulo n-niŋ ʒɛri maa ni. (She put cloves in the soup.)
From: (Hausa).  O doli la Musulunsi. (He follows Islam.)

english: moo (the cry of a cow, baby name for a cow; onomatopoeia)
example: Naɣi maa yɛli "muu". (The cow said 'moo'.)

english: a sincere, believing Muslim (from Hausa < Arabic)
example: O nyɛla muumini. (He is a sincere believer.)
From: (Hausa) < (Arabic).  O be la muzaaba ni. (He is in suffering.)
N chaŋ la daa ni. (I went to the market.)
M ba n-daa kana. (It was my father who came.)
O yiɣisi n-chaŋ. (He got up and went.)
N ti ya la liɣiri maa. (I gave you all the money.)

english: to conspire; to plot (in phrase na nari)
example: Bɛ na la nari n-ti o. (They are conspiring against him.)
Bɛ zaŋ la na maa n-sɔɣi. (They buried the placenta.)
Kam na! (Come here!)
O na be yiŋa. (He is still at home.)

english: a chief; a king; a headman/title-holder
example: Pl: nanima.  Naa maa ʒiya. (The chief is seated.)

english: (response to a call or to most greetings)
example: O bɔhi o, ka o yɛli "Naa!". (He called him, and he responded 'Naa!'.)

english: the eighteenth day of Damba, day for homage
example: Naa Damba dali n-daa nyɛ li. (It was the day of Naa Damba.)

english: an Elder of the Yaan-Naa who repeats loudly the speech of the Yaan-Naa
example: Naa Noli maa moli la Naa maa yɛltɔɣa. (Naa Noli proclaims the chief's words.)
Bɛ zaŋ la naabɔɣu n-ti niɣi maa. (They gave cattle immunisation to the cows.)
Bɛ zaŋ la naad n-niŋ tulaale maa ni. (They put nard in the perfume.)
From: (Hausa) < (Arabic).  O niŋ la naafiila jiŋli ni. (He performed supplementary prayers at the mosque.)

english: to agree/be reconciled; to reconcile people; (in phrase naai noli 'to agree/come to compromise')
example: Bɛ naai la noli. (They agreed.)

english: to finish (sth. does)/come to an end/run out; to finish (sth.) (transitive); to finish doing sth. (auxiliary)
example: Tuma maa naai ya. (The work has finished.)

english: an aide of the Yaan-Naa (lit. 'chief's supporting-stone')
example: Naakpɛliŋga maa be nayili ni. (The chief's aide is at the palace.)

english: a bird species (blue-coloured birds feeding on grasshoppers, possibly Abyssinian roller)
example: Naalɔŋ maa ʒi tia zuɣu. (The blue bird is sitting on the tree.)

english: an answer to a call (variant of naa)
example: O yɛli "Naam!". (He said 'Yes!' in response.)
Naamam maa ʒi tia zuɣu. (The cat is sitting on the tree.)
From: (Hausa) 'nama' + chɛmsi.  O daa la naamanchɛmsi. (He bought tinned corned-beef.)

english: would (marker for condition or hypothetical, apodosis); "should" (protasis)
example: N yi di mali liɣiri, n naan da li. (If I had the money, I would buy it.)

english: a plant species; a small sensitive plant
example: Naanaakpabigbandi maa vari ŋmaligiri. (The leaves of the sensitive plant fold up.)
O niŋ la o naani. (He trusted him.)
O naanigoo n-daa chɛ ka o niŋ suhusaɣiŋgu. (The betrayal of trust made him sad.)

english: an insect species; a silent cricket
example: Naaninye maa kumdi la yuŋ. (The cricket is chirping at night.)
O di naanyi kana. (He finally came.)

english: (a) pepper (fruit or as a condiment) (variant of nanzua)
example: O zaŋ la naanzua n-niŋ ʒɛri maa ni. (She put pepper in the soup.)

english: a pepper type (large or round) (variant of nanzubua)
example: O zaŋ la naanzubua n-niŋ ʒɛri maa ni. (She put large peppers in the soup.)

english: a pepper type (hot, smaller than naanzubua) (variant of nanzuchiriga)
example: O zaŋ la naanzuchiriga n-niŋ ʒɛri maa ni. (She put small hot peppers in the soup.)
O zaŋ la naanzutɔɣu n-niŋ ʒɛri maa ni. (She put hot peppers in the soup.)

english: a tree species (hard wood used for charcoal and rafters; used as chewing stick)
example: Bɛ zaŋ la naanʒili n-to buɣim. (They used naanʒili wood to make charcoal.)
Naasanjerigu maa be kom maa ni. (The crab is in the water.)

english: God (of Christian doctrine); the Almighty/High God (of Pagans); Allah (of Muslims)
example: Ti jɛmdi la Naawuni. (We worship God.)

english: a godly person, 'God's servant/slave'; hermit ant called chɛrili (epithet)
example: Naawuni dabili maa jɛmdi la Naawuni. (The godly person worships God.)

english: a dark circle around either the sun or the moon (lit. 'God's market')
example: Naawuni daa maa gili la wuntaŋa. (A dark circle is around the sun.)
O zaŋ la o Naawuni nubila n-ti o. (He used his forefinger to point at him.)
O Naawunitali n-daa nyɛ li. (It was his divinity.)

english: being god-fearing; absolute belief in God or Qur'an and Hadith (variant of wuntizɔbo)
example: O Naawuntizoo n-daa chɛ ka o doli Naawuni. (His god-fearing made him follow God.)
O mali la pihiwɛinaayi. (He has ninety-two.) Niriba kɔbisita ni pihiwɛinaayi n-kana. (Three hundred and ninety-two people came.)

english: the eighth day; a week after
example: O dɔɣim naba dali n-daa nyɛ li. (It was the eighth day after his birth.)

english: to fatten up/grow stout (of a child); to increase in weight; to increase in quantity
example: Bia maa nabi ya pam. (The child has grown very stout.)

english: a son of a chief; a 'prince'; a descendant of a chief
example: Pl: nabihi.  Nabia maa ʒiya nayili ni. (The prince is at the palace.)
Pl: nabigbula.  Nabigbulli ŋɔ bi mali jilima. (This princeling has no respect.)
O mali nabikɔɣu o nia ni. (He has ringworm on his hand.)

english: a princeling; petty/self-styled prince/princess (synonym of nabigbulli)
example: Pl: nabikuɣa.  Nabikuɣinli ŋɔ bi wumdi yɛltɔɣa. (This princeling doesn't listen.)
O naba biɛya. (His leg hurts.)
O arzichi nabili viɛla. (The increase in his wealth is good.)

english: a shrub species used as an herb to bathe thin children
example: Bɛ zaŋ la nabuli n-su bia maa. (They used the shrub to bathe the child.)

english: the same day in the following week; a week later
example: Di niŋ la o naba dali. (It happened a week later.)

english: pride of race (in good or bad sense)
example: O nabiligu n-daa chɛ ka o nyɛ jilima. (His pride of ancestry got him respect.)
Pl: nabinbiɛmlanima.  Nabinbiɛmlana ŋɔ bi zɔri niriba nambɔɣu. (This despot shows no mercy to people.)

english: a prince of rare qualities; a pretender prince
example: Pl: nabipariganima.  Nabipariga ŋɔ mali baŋsim pam. (This prince of rare qualities has much wisdom.)
Pl: nabipuɣinsi.  Nabipuɣiŋga maa ʒi nayili ni. (The princess is at the palace.)

english: pride of a princess (in good or derogatory sense)
example: O nabipuɣiŋli n-daa chɛ ka o je o tizo. (Her princessly pride made her hate her younger sister.)

english: a prince sine cura (member of chiefly family with no office)
example: Pl: nabiyɔri.  Nabiyɔŋa maa ʒi yɔɣu ni. (The prince without office lives in the village.)
Bihi maa diri la nachiinsi chuɣu maa ni. (The children are wearing fashionable clothes at the festival.)
Nachimba maa tumdi la tuma nayili ni. (The lads are working at the palace.)
Pl: nachimbihi.  Nachimbila maa chaŋ puu ni. (The young man went to the farm.)

english: one's youth; teen-age years; young manhood
example: O nachimbilim ni, o daa be yaɣa pam. (In his youth, he travelled a lot.)
Nachimpolli maa kumda. (The young boy is crying.)
Nachinkpamba maa laɣim ya nayili ni. (The court elders have gathered at the palace.)
O mini o nachintaba n-daa chaŋ daa ni. (He and his fellow young people went to the market.)
O lo la nachintɛri n-ti o paɣa. (He bought finery for his wife.)
O ʒini la naɣa. (He sat down heavily.)
Ti jɛmdi la Nagaŋa. (We worship the Only God.)
Pl: naɣgulanima.  Naɣgula maa guli la niɣi mɔɣu ni. (The cowherd is herding the cows in the bush.)

english: to bend concavely; to be sway-backed; (in phrase naɣi nyee niŋ X 'to despise X')
example: O naɣi o nyaaŋa. (He bent his back.)

english: to miss; to miss out; to fail to; to be absent
example: Ti naɣi la taba. (We missed each other.)
O naɣi n-daa kana. (Only he came.)

english: to despise someone (idiom, lit. 'to turn up the nose at')
example: O naɣi o nyee niŋ o. (He turned up his nose at him.)

english: except for; apart from (lit. 'fail to add')
example: So bi kana, naɣi pahila Abu. (No one came, except for Abu.)

english: to miss each other; to not see each other
example: Ti naɣi taba yuma ayi. (We haven't seen each other for two years.)

english: a bird species; black tick bird (possibly piapiac)
example: Naɣibariga maa ʒi naɣi maa zuɣu. (The tick bird is sitting on the cow.)

english: a dance type (a dance for men)
example: Bɛ diɛmdi la naɣibiɛɣu chuɣu maa ni. (They are dancing the men's dance at the festival.)
Pl: naɣibihi.  Naɣibila maa be o ma sani. (The calf is with its mother.)
Bɛ zaŋ la naɣibindi n-niŋ puu ni. (They used cow-dung on the farm.)
O lo la naɣichiinsi n-ti o bia. (She bought decorations for her child.)
Naɣichima maa tumdi la tuma. (The lad is working.)
Naɣidɔro gbaai niɣi maa. (Cattle-disease caught the cows.)
Bɛ zaŋ niɣi maa niŋ naɣigɔrigu ni. (They put the cows in the kraal.)
Naɣikɔhira maa kɔhiri la nimdi daa ni. (The butcher sells meat at the market.)
O zaŋ la naɣikɔrigu n-ko o puu. (He used a bullock to plough his farm.)

english: a peg driven in the ground to secure cattle; a place where cattle pegs are driven (cattleyard/kraal)
example: O zaŋ la naɣikpaa n-lo naɣi maa. (He used a cattle-peg to tether the cow.)
Bɛ zaŋ niɣi maa niŋ naɣikpahi ni. (They put the cows in the kraal.)

english: a cowherd; cattle-herder (usually a small boy or Fulani man)
example: Pl: naɣikparanima.  Naɣikpara maa guli la niɣi. (The cowherd is herding the cows.)
O zaŋ la naɣikuli n-ko o puu. (He used a plough to farm.)
Naɣikum gbaai niɣi maa zaa. (Cattle-plague caught all the cows.)
O ku kana naɣila saha ti paai. (He will not come unless the time arrives.)
Naɣila a niŋ yomyom, naɣi a ku paai saha. (Unless you hurry, otherwise you won't arrive on time.)
Pl: naɣilariba.  Naɣilaa maa mali yaa pam. (The bull is very strong.)
Bɛ zaŋ la naɣilaatabili n-ko puu. (They used a bullock to plough the farm.)
Bɛ kɔrigi la naɣilaatabili maa n-ti sara. (They slaughtered the bullock for sacrifice.)
Naɣilaatoli maa mali bihi pam. (The stud bull has many offspring.)
Naɣilabila maa nyɛla ŋun bala. (The bull-calf is young.)

english: to warp/twist; to form a hollow/to have a depression (of the ground)
example: Dari maa naɣilim ya. (The wood has warped.)

english: a small depression in the ground/hollow; a low valley
example: Kom laɣim ya naɣiliŋga maa ni. (Water has gathered in the hollow.)
Bɛ zaŋ niɣi maa niŋ naɣilua ni. (They put the cows in the kraal.)

english: as a windfall; unexpectedly received gratis (adverb)
example: O nya li la naɣinaɣi. (He got it as a windfall.)
Bɛ dira naɣinimdi. (They are eating beef.)

english: a moth species (possibly 'swooping round cows' eyes')
example: Naɣininpɔbirigu maa gili la firila maa. (The moth is circling the lamp.)
Pl: naɣinyansi.  Naɣinyaŋ maa dɔɣi la naɣibila. (The cow gave birth to a calf.)
Bɛ gbaai la naɣinyɛbira maa. (They caught the man who had sex with a cow.)

english: hyena (variant of nanyili, synonym of kunduŋ)
example: Naɣinyili maa kumdi la yuŋ. (The hyena howls at night.)

english: a tree species; locust tree (with spongy roots, long slender pods)
example: Naɣinyoontia tia ŋɔ wula pam. (This locust tree has many branches.)

english: a beetle species (black beetle played with and sometimes eaten by children)
example: Bihi maa gbahi la naɣinyooŋa. (The children are catching black beetles.)

english: a West African short-horn (type of cow)
example: O puu ni naɣiŋmaa maa mali yaa. (His short-horn cow is strong.)

english: a bird species; cattle-egret (Bubulcus ibis) (variant of naɣipiɛliga)
example: Naɣipiɛriga maa ʒi naɣi maa zuɣu. (The cattle-egret is sitting on the cow.)

english: a stirk (young bovine, not a small calf)
example: Naɣipolli maa mali yaa. (The stirk is strong.)

english: a stirk; young of cattle (not a small calf)
example: Naɣipolli maa tɔrila o ma. (The stirk is following its mother.)
Naɣipuri maa zooi mɔɣu ni. (That grass species grows in the bush.)
Bɛ puri la naɣiruŋ maa ni. (They are farming in the small valley.)

english: a heifer (female cow which has not produced young)
example: Pl: naɣisahi.  Naɣisaa maa na bi dɔɣi. (The heifer has not yet calved.)

english: to delay in doing something; to walk with difficulty due to a heavy load
example: O naɣisi o labbu maa. (He delayed his return.)
Bɛ zaŋ la naɣitɔroko n-ʒi kawana na. (They used an ox-cart to bring the maize.)
Naɣiyɛm maa diri la niɣi. (The cows are eating that grass.)
Wɔri maa dira naɣiyihim. (The horses are eating that grass.)
Naɣizino maa gindi la mɔɣu ni. (The stray cow is wandering in the bush.)

english: to cancel; to rub/wipe; to disappear for ever
example: O nahi la o yuviɛlli. (He wiped out his name.)

english: to make level; to be equal; to gather small quantities to make up what one wants
example: Bɛ nahi la tiŋgbani maa. (They levelled the ground.)

english: to worry someone; to vex/trouble someone; to try hard
example: Bihi maa nahinda la ba. (The children are troubling him.)
Yɛla maa chaŋ nahimmo. (Things went completely wrong.)
Bɛ sabi la ti nahingbaŋ. (They wrote our history.)
O be la nahiŋgu ni. (He is in distress.)
Pl: niɣi.  Nahu maa ʒe la soli zuɣu. (The cow is standing on the road.)

english: a plant species; a creeper vegetable used for soup
example: O zaŋ la nahu n-niŋ ʒɛri maa ni. (She put the creeper vegetable in the soup.)
Nakɔha maa kɔhiri la nimdi daa ni. (The butcher sells meat at the market.)

english: a division in a yam farm; an acre

example: O yihi o nuu ka chɛ tuma maa. (He withdrew from the work.)

english: he spoke out (lit. he opened his mouth)
example: O yooi o noli n-yɛli yɛlimaŋli. (He opened his mouth and told the truth.)

english: he is famous/renowned (lit. his name went up)
example: O yuli du tiŋgbani ŋɔ ni zaa. (He is renowned all over this land.)

english: he is disgraced/defamed (lit. his name spoiled)
example: O yuli saɣim ni o zuɣu. (He is disgraced because of theft.)

english: he is angry/upset (lit. his hair is burning)
example: O zabiri nyo, miri ka a yɛli o shɛli. (He is angry, don't say anything to him.)

english: he is interested/concerned (lit. his concern is there)
example: O zaɣa be ni tuma maa ni. (He is interested in the work.)

english: he is indifferent/not concerned (lit. his concern is not there)
example: O zaɣa ka ni ti yɛltɔɣa maa ni. (He is not concerned with our matter.)

english: his attention is focused (lit. his concern remains in one place)
example: O zaɣa kpalim luɣ'yini o tuma maa ni. (His attention is focused on his work.)

english: he discerned/understood (lit. he measured and knew)
example: O zahim baŋ ni ʒiri ka o yɛra. (He discerned that he was lying.)

english: he tested it and saw (i.e., he tried it out)
example: O zahim li n-nya ka di bi viɛla. (He tried it and saw that it wasn't good.)

english: he discerned him (i.e., he saw his true character)
example: O zahim o baŋ ka baŋ ni o nyɛla ʒirilana. (He discerned that he was a liar.)
O zahim zahimbu pam amaa o bi lu. (He was greatly tempted but did not fall.)

english: he had a dream (lit. he dreamt a dream)
example: O zahim zahinli zaawuni. (He had a dream last night.)

english: he stored/kept it (lit. he put it down and kept it)
example: O zali liɣiri maa mi n-sɔŋ. (He kept the money safely.)

english: she conceived (lit. she set down her pregnancy)
example: Paɣa maa zali o puu yuuni gari la. (The woman conceived last year.)

english: he imposed a fine on him
example: Kootu maa zali o la samli. (The court imposed a fine on him.)
Lala dabisili maa daa zali o zali. (That day was appointed for him.)
O zani n-tuhi o yɛlimaŋli ni. (He stood firm in his truth.)

english: he supports/backs him (lit. he stands behind him)
example: N zani a nyaaŋa tuma maa ni. (I support you in the work.)

english: he opposes him (lit. he stands on his head)
example: O zani n zuɣu, o bi bɔri ni n tum tuma maa. (He opposes me, he doesn't want me to do the work.)

english: he stagnated (lit. he stood on the road)
example: O tuma maa zani soli, bi lahi chani tooni. (His work stagnated, it's not progressing anymore.)
Tuma maa zani tariga saha shɛli. (The work came to an end at some point.)
O zaŋ o maŋa bahi, o bi lahi bo soya. (He gave up, he didn't look for ways anymore.)

english: he dedicated himself (lit. he took himself and gave)
example: O zaŋ o maŋa ti Naawuni tuma. (He dedicated himself to God's work.)

english: he attended (lit. he took his foot)
example: O zaŋ o naba n-chaŋ laɣingu maa ni. (He attended the meeting.)

english: he gave up/abdicated (lit. he took his foot away)
example: O zaŋ o naba bahi ka chɛ nam maa. (He abdicated the chieftaincy.)

english: he focused/concentrated (lit. he put his eye in)
example: O zaŋ o nini niŋ karim maa ni. (He focused on the reading.)

english: he concentrated on it (lit. he put his concern in it)
example: O zaŋ o zaɣa niŋ karim maa ni viɛnyɛla. (He concentrated well on the studies.)

english: he paid attention to/concentrated on (lit. he put his concern in)
example: O zaŋ o zaɣa niŋ karim maa ni. (He concentrated on the reading.)

english: he is afraid (lit. he fears fear)
example: O zɔri dabiɛm ni o chaŋ nimaani. (He is afraid to go there.)

english: his friendship refused him (i.e., his friend let him down)
example: Saha shɛli o ni daa bɔri sɔŋsim, o zɔri maa zaɣisi o. (When he needed help, his friend let him down.)
O zɔri mi n-gindi ka bi tumdi tuma. (He is running around aimlessly and not working.)
O zɔri nambɔɣu n-ti nandaamba. (He shows mercy to the poor.)

dagbani: o zɔri ni o ti nya
english: he is anxious/eager to get (lit. he is running to get)
example: O zɔri ni o ti nya liɣiri maa. (He is anxious to get the money.)

english: he is chasing after him (lit. he is running giving him his back)
example: O zɔri n-tiri o nyaaŋa ni o gbaagi o. (He is chasing after him to catch him.)

english: he is shy/bashful (lit. he fears shame)
example: O zɔri vi, o bi yɛri yɛltɔɣa zama ni. (He is shy, he doesn't speak in public.)
Bɛ ni daa ti o lahibali maa, o zo n-kuli. (When they gave him the news, he fled home.)

english: he outran him (lit. he ran and cut his head)
example: O zo n-ŋma o zo maa zuɣu n-daŋ na. (He outran his friend and arrived first.)
Bɛ ni daa ti o lahibali maa, o zo n-ti guui. (When they gave him the news, he ran away.)
O zo n-ti kana ti yɛli ti lahibali maa. (He ran here to tell us the news.)
Saa maa zuɣu, o zo n-ti kpe yili ni. (Because of the rain, he ran into the house.)

english: he came to his aid (lit. he ran and gave him support)
example: O zo n-ti o kuŋa di yi ti niŋ ka o be wahala ni. (He comes to his aid whenever he is in trouble.)
O zo n-ti sɔɣi di yi ti niŋ ka o nya o ba. (He runs and hides whenever he sees his father.)
O zo n-ti yi sarika maa ni. (He escaped from the prison.)

english: he escaped (lit. he ran and was safe)
example: O zo n-tiligi ka chɛ barina maa. (He escaped from the danger.)

english: it is many/much; he is abundant
example: Niriba zooi pam laɣingu maa ni. (There are many people at the meeting.)

english: he gossiped about/slandered him (lit. he stole his back)
example: O zu o nyaaŋa n-yɛli o yɛltɔɣa biɛɣu. (He gossiped about him.)

english: he went off without permission (lit. he stole the road)
example: O zu soli n-chaŋ daa ni. (He went off to the market without permission.)
O zuɣu biɛɣu zuɣu ka o bi di nasara. (Because of his bad luck, he didn't win.)

english: he is mad (lit. his head is shaking)
example: O zuɣu dam pam. (He is very mad.)

english: he is crazy (lit. his head changed)
example: O zuɣu lɛbigi, o bi lahi mi o ni yɛn niŋ shɛm. (He is crazy, he doesn't know what he is doing anymore.)
O zuɣu suŋ n-chɛ ka o nya liɣiri maa. (It was his good luck that made him find the money.)

english: he stands by his word/is faithful
example: O ʒɛya ni o yɛlimaŋli, o bi taɣira. (He stands by his word, he doesn't change.)

english: he doesn't know; he is ignorant
example: O ʒi binshɛɣu zaŋ chaŋ lala yɛlli maa. (He knows nothing about that matter.)
O ʒi la n-tiŋa n-gbihira. (He is sitting down sleeping.)
O ʒi la yiŋa ka bi tumdi tuma. (He lives at home and doesn't work.)

english: he is in the crowd/with the people
example: O ʒi la zama ni ka wumdi bɛ yɛltɔɣa. (He is in the crowd listening to their talk.)

english: he is trapped/caught in a snare
example: O ʒi la zana ni o dimba ni. (He is caught in a snare by his enemies.)
O ʒi la ʒii yuŋ maa ni. (He saw a vision in the night.)

english: he is sitting (in a seated position)
example: O ʒi la ʒiini n-karimda. (He is sitting reading.)
O ʒi la ʒiini n-suɣira o bihi zuɣu. (He is sitting worrying about his children.)
O ʒi la ʒiini n-tɛhira o biɛhigu yɛla. (He is sitting thinking about his life.)
Dam maa zuɣu, o ʒi la ʒiini n-ʒiɛra. (Because of the drink, he is sitting and staggering.)
O ʒi la o ʒiishee laɣingu maa ni. (He took his seat at the meeting.)
O ʒi la ʒili titali o zuɣu. (He is carrying a heavy load on his head.)
O ʒi la ʒili maa n-dimsi, ka di yi o zuɣu n-lu. (He carried the load carelessly and it fell off his head.)

english: he is carrying a heavy burden (literal and figurative)
example: O ʒi la ʒili timsili o suhu ni. (He is carrying a heavy burden in his heart.)

english: he has gone too far (in behavior)
example: O ʒiɛ n-gari, o lahi bi wumdi so. (He has gone too far, he doesn't listen to anyone anymore.)
Lala dabisili maa ʒiɛ n-ti o ni o chaŋ. (That day was appointed for him to go.)

english: he has gone astray (lit. he staggered out of the road)
example: O ʒiɛ n-yi soli, o bi lahi doli Naawuni. (He has gone astray, he no longer follows God.)
Dam maa zuɣu, o ʒiɛhi n-lu tiŋa. (Because of the drink, he staggered and fell.)
O ʒiɛm n-lihi o dama o nyɛla faralana. (He looks down on him because he is poor.)
O ʒiɛm o dama o nyɛla faralana. (He despises him because he is poor.)
O ʒiɛmbu paagi o ni o niŋ shɛli maa zuɣu. (Disgrace has befallen him because of what he did.)
O ʒiɛri gindi kaman piɛɣu ŋun bɔrigi. (He is wandering astray like a lost sheep.)
O ʒiɛri maa biɛya, o zaŋ la yalim pam. (The soup is bad, she put too much salt.)

english: the soup is tasty (lit. the soup stands)
example: O ʒiɛri maa ʒiɛya pam. (Her soup is very tasty.)

english: he blamed him (lit. he loaded him with blame)
example: Bɛ ʒili o la zambu zaŋ chaŋ taali maa polo. (They blamed him for the offence.)
O ʒilinsi galisi, o bi baŋ shɛli. (His ignorance is great, he knows nothing.)

english: he is anaemic/weak (lit. his blood is small)
example: O ʒim biɛla, o gbarigira. (He is anaemic, he is weak.)

english: he is energetic/strong (lit. his blood has strength)
example: O ʒim mali yaa, o tooi tumdi tuma kuŋ. (He is energetic, he can do hard work.)

english: he bled (lit. his blood came out on the ground)
example: O luhi tiŋa ka o ʒim yi tiŋa. (He fell down and bled.)

english: he is bleeding (lit. his blood is leaking)
example: O dɔro maa zuɣu, o ʒim yiya. (Because of his wound, he is bleeding.)
O ʒimi n-guli ka tuma maa na bi pili. (He is sitting idly waiting, and the work hasn't started.)
Bɛ ʒini o la gbandi zuɣu yuuni gari la. (He was enskinned last year.)

english: he sat in the holy place
example: Maligu maa ʒini la ʒiishee kasi. (The priest sat in the holy place.)
O ʒini n-guli ka so bi kana. (He sat and waited, and no one came.)
Oho! A kana. (Golly! You've come.)

english: I don't care!/come what may/be warned; Well... (exclamation, variant of ooho)
example: Ohoo, n na kana. (Well, I haven't come yet.)

english: So what? (exclamation, "That's your look-out, I don't care!")
example: Ohoronio! N bi yɛn chaŋ. (So what! I'm not going.)
Bɛ zaŋ la oki tia n-mɛ ŋariŋ. (They used oak wood to build the boat.)
From: (English).  O kɔ la olivi tihi. (He planted olive trees.)
O ku o maŋa. (He killed himself.)

english: himself/herself (emphatic, maŋmaŋa with rd pers. prefix)
example: O maŋmaŋa n-daa kana. (He himself came.)
Alfa ni Omɛga n-nyɛ piligu ni bahigu. (Alpha and Omega are the beginning and the end.)

english: a response to a call (by children)
example: Bia maa yɛli "oo!" ka o ma boli o. (The child said 'oo!' and his mother called him.)
Ooho! A yɛli yɛlimaŋli. (Really! You speak the truth.)

english: to be upon/on top of; to put on top of; to succeed/come next after/plant in succession; to nickname/give an extra name
example: Kuɣili pa la bɔɣili maa zuɣu. (The stone is on top of the hole.)

english: not be (verb, negative copula); (in various negative cleft constructions)
example: O pala doo. (He is not a man.)
O pa kana. (He has just come.)

english: next to each other; in succession; one after another
example: Bɛ ʒinila kuɣa pa taba. (They sat on stools next to each other.)

english: to be next in line; to follow after you
example: Ŋun pa yi zuɣu n-daa kana. (The one who came after you came.)

english: paid work (noun); (in phrase bu so paa 'to hire someone')
example: O tum la paa. (He did paid work.)
Bihi maa chaŋ paabi ni. (The children went on holiday.)
O zaŋ la liɣiri niŋ o paachi ni. (He put the money in his pocket.)
Bɛ chaŋ ti diɛm paachi ni. (They went to play in the park.)
O zaŋ la paadiri n-duhi ŋariŋ maa. (He used a paddle to propel the boat.)

english: to arrive/arrive at/reach; to attain/reach/become equal to (in size or status); (in phrase hal ti paai 'up as far as (time or place)')
example: O paai la yiŋa. (He arrived home.)

english: to lift off the top; to remove (bird from a nest)
example: O paai la kuɣili maa. (He lifted off the stone.)
Bɛ zaŋ la paain tihi n-mɛ ŋariŋ. (They used pine trees to build the boat.)

english: the senior wife (of a chief or title holder)
example: Paani maa ʒi naa maa zuɣuni. (The senior wife sits beside the chief.)

english: a needle for sewing sacks (plural paansi)
example: O zaŋ la paania n-she gbana. (She used a sack needle to sew the skins.)
O di la paanu. (He ate bread.)
Paaŋa maa kumdi la yuŋ. (The cricket is chirping at night.)
From: (English).  O paasi la bɔlɔŋ maa n-ti o zo. (He passed the ball to his friend.)

english: holidays for school children or students; vacation (from English 'pass')
example: Bɛ bahi la paasi n-ti bihi maa. (They gave the children holidays.)
Bɛ kpa la paati palli. (They formed a new political party.)

english: to faint; to be tired/weak/near the point of death
example: O pabigi ya konyuri zuɣu. (He fainted from thirst.)
Padira maa tumdi la tuma. (The hired labourer is working.)

english: a woman; a wife or brother's wife; grandmother (epithet); a female (especially in discussing sex of a baby)
example: Paɣa maa chaŋ puu ni. (The woman went to the farm.)

english: to marry (for a man lit. 'to take a wife')
example: O kpuɣi la paɣa. (He married.)

english: to marry (for a woman lit. 'to go home')
example: Paɣa maa kuli la doo. (The woman married.)

english: a male, esp. a boy, who is very much attached to women (paɣa + ba + lɛligu)
example: Paɣabalɛliga ŋɔ bi chɛri paɣaba. (This womanizer never leaves women alone.)

english: the relationship between paternal and maternal cousins; the son of a princess
example: Bɛ nyɛla paɣabia. (They are paternal/maternal cousins.)

english: husbands; married men (plural, lit. 'people with wives')
example: Paɣanima maa zaa kana laɣiŋgu maa ni. (All the married men came to the meeting.)

english: a lily species (Amaryllis belladonna) (lit. 'beautiful maiden won't touch water')
example: Paɣaviɛligakushihikom maa viɛla pam. (The lily is very beautiful.)

english: a promiscuous/adulterous woman; a prostitute; a concubine (plural pagɔra)
example: Pagɔrili maa chaŋ daa ni. (The prostitute went to the market.)
O tum la pagɔrilim. (She engaged in prostitution.)
Pagɔrisigu nyɛla taali ninsalinim' sani. (Adultery is a sin in people's eyes.)

english: to praise; to thank; to be pleased with; (in serial verb constructions) to prefer; (in phrase m paɣi ka... 'If only, would that...')
example: O paɣi o la. (He praised him.)

english: to set a fowl (put it to brood eggs); to shut someone up in a room
example: O paɣi la noo maa gala zuɣu. (She set the hen on the eggs.)

english: to wash anything (including body-part, but not 'bathe' whole body)
example: O paɣi la o nuhi. (He washed his hands.)

english: bride price (gifts given during courtship) (paɣa + bo + liɣiri)
example: O yo la paɣibɔriliɣiri maa zaa. (He paid all the bride price.)

english: a neonate; a woman who has just given birth (Eastern dialect?)
example: Paɣidɔɣisa maa nyɛla ŋun mali alaafee. (The new mother is healthy.)

english: a neonate; a woman who has just given birth (Western dialect?)
example: Paɣidɔɣiso maa vuhi la. (The new mother is resting.)
Paɣidɔɣisotɔɣinda maa sɔŋ o dɔɣibu. (The midwife helped her give birth.)
O mali paɣikɔhiŋgu. (He has tuberculosis.)
Paɣikpɛma maa ʒiya. (The old lady is seated.)

english: a drying-rack (bark of a tree hung above a fireplace to keep pepper, salt, etc.)
example: O zaŋ la paɣilari n-zaŋ o nanzua. (She used the drying-rack for her pepper.)
O daŋa maa ni paɣili yi na. (A scab has formed on his wound.)

english: fog/mist; black magic believed to make a person invisible
example: Paɣili limsi tiŋgbani maa. (Fog covered the land.)
Paɣinyaŋkurugu maa mali baŋsim pam. (The old woman has much wisdom.)
Pl: paɣinyulilanima.  Paɣinyulilana ŋɔ je o kpee. (This jealous woman hates her co-wife.)

english: a barren woman; childless woman (paɣa + ŋoo)
example: Paɣiŋoo maa suhiri Naawuni ni o ti o bia. (The barren woman prays to God to give her a child.)

english: a washerwoman; woman doing laundry (paɣa + paɣi + ra)
example: Pl: paɣipaɣiriba.  Paɣipaɣira maa paɣirila nɛma. (The washerwoman is washing clothes.)

english: a virgin; 'new woman' (possibly Christian invention) (paɣa + palli)
example: Paɣipali maa na bi kpuɣi doo. (The virgin has not married.)
O paɣipalitali n-daa nyɛ o nachiinsi. (Her virginity was her adornment.)

english: a virgin (lit. 'white woman', probably Christian invention) (paɣa + 'piɛliga)
example: Paɣipiɛliga maa mali jilima. (The virgin has honour.)
Bɛ daa guli o paɣipiɛlitali. (They guarded her virginity.)

english: a woman given in marriage; wife by arranged marriage (paɣa + pini)
example: Bɛ ti o la paɣipini. (They gave him a woman in marriage.)

english: customary thanks (a cock and kolanuts) provided by a person who is given a woman for marriage in appreciation (paɣa + puhigu)
example: O zaŋ la paɣipuhigu n-ti o. (He gave the customary thanks gift.)
O mali paɣipuli. (She is pregnant.)
Paɣipuulana maa chaŋ ashibiti. (The pregnant woman went to the hospital.)

english: a washing-implement; something to wash with (paɣi + riga)
example: O zaŋ la paɣiriga n-paɣi o nini. (She used a cloth to wash her face.)

english: a curved dagger (used by the Konkomba people)
example: O zaŋ la paɣiriga n-ku o. (He used a curved dagger to kill him.)
Paɣisaribila maa viɛla pam. (The young woman is very beautiful.)

english: birth of a woman's first child; woman's firstborn (paɣisarili + dɔɣim)
example: O dɔɣi la o paɣisaridɔɣim. (She gave birth to her first child.)

english: a maiden/damsel; a nubile girl (unmarried or not yet having had a child) (plural paɣisara)
example: Paɣisarili maa ʒi yiŋa. (The maiden is at home.)

english: maidenhood/virginity/young-womanhood; fashionable dress/young ladies' adornments (paɣisarili + lim)
example: O paɣisarilim ni, o daa viɛla pam. (In her maidenhood, she was very beautiful.)
O paɣisi o la. (He rivalled him.)

english: a man's first-wed wife (as opposed to 'senior wife') (paɣa + nintaa?)
example: O paɣitaa n-daa nyɛ o paɣa kurili. (His first-wed wife was his senior wife.)
O paɣitali n-daa nyɛ li. (It was her womanhood.)
Paɣiviɛligakushihikom maa viɛla pam. (The lily is very beautiful.)

english: a woman without any responsibility for a husband or a child (paɣa + wayini?)
example: Paɣiwayini maa be o ko. (The woman with no family ties is alone.)
Paɣiyihiŋ maa labi o yiŋa. (The divorced woman returned to her home.)
Paɣizɔritantulim maa zɔri yomyom. (The solifuge runs fast.)

english: a woman without a husband; a spinster or a divorced woman (paɣa + zilinli)
example: Paɣizilinli maa ʒiya. (The single woman is seated.)

english: to clear weeds with a hoe; to skim (take floating material on water or liquid)
example: O pahi la o puu. (He cleared the weeds on his farm.)

english: to add/give in addition; to increase in number/quantity; to be/do sth. in addition/again (auxiliary); (in phrase din pahi 'another thing/point/topic'); (with numerals) -th (ordinal)
example: O pahi la liɣiri maa. (He added the money.)
From: (English).  O zaŋ la pakati n-niŋ o liɣiri. (He put his money in a packet/pocket.)

english: a suitor of a widow (pakoli + bo + ra)
example: Pl: pakɔbɔriba.  Pakɔbɔra maa kana ti bɔri pakoli maa. (The widow's suitor came to court the widow.)

english: a man who performs the cleansing ceremony on widows (pakoli + pali + ga)
example: Pakɔpaliga maa niŋ la pakoli maa daɣiri yihibu. (The cleanser performed the widow's purification ceremony.)
Pakoli maa kumdi o yidan' kum. (The widow is mourning her husband's death.)
O be la pakolim ni. (She is in widowhood.)
O puu ni pakolzandee maa di pam. (The pakolzandee yams in his farm yielded a lot.)

english: first-born daughter (especially of a deceased chief or title holder)
example: O pakpɔŋ n-daa deei nam maa. (His first-born daughter inherited the title.)

english: an old woman (like 'witch', 'hag'); a witch (plural pakura)
example: Pakurigu maa ʒiya yiŋa. (The old woman is sitting at home.)

english: a shrub species (prickly shrub, bearing three-pronged thorns) (lit. 'witch's fang') (pakurigu + nyindaa)
example: Pakurigunyindaa tia ŋɔ mali gɔhi pam. (This shrub has many thorns.)
