english: to deride/pooh-pooh someone; to show discontent; to puff (make a sound in the mouth to indicate impatience or contempt)
example: O sabisi o la. (He derided him.)
Sabisiga maa be dukpini zuɣu. (The gecko is on the wall.)
From: (Hausa).  Sabita nyɛla din simdi ti biɛhigu ni. (Cleanliness is important in our lives.)
Sabitina maa kana tiŋa ŋɔ ni. (The superintendent came to this town.)

english: a field mouse species (having small eyes, usually found in swampy places) (sabili + tiŋgbuŋ?)
example: Sabitiŋgbuŋ maa be fadama ni. (That field mouse is in the swamp.)

english: money paid by a man to seal a marriage contract (from Hausa)
example: O yo la sadaachi n-ti paɣa maa. (He paid the marriage money.)

english: the direction from which a place gets its heavy rains (saa + daaŋa)
example: Sadaaŋga maa yi la wulimpuhili polo na. (The heavy rains come from the east.)
Sadibu maa nyaɣisi ya. (The lightning flashed.)
From: (English) < (Greek) < (Hebrew).  O alaama ni mali la safaya. (His ring has a sapphire.)
From: (English).  Safia maa zahim la tiŋgbani maa. (The surveyor measured the land.)

english: saffron (cosmetic, borrowed for Bible from English)
example: Bɛ zaŋ la safrɔn n-niŋ tulaale maa ni. (They put saffron in the perfume.)

english: a row of people for Islamic worship (from Hausa < Arabic)
example: Bɛ zani la safu ni n-ti puhi jiŋli. (They stood in rows to pray.)
O di la sagali. (He ate t.z. without soup.)
Sagani maa yi na. (The rainbow has appeared.)

english: a cloud; the sky (plural sagbana) (variant of sangbani)
example: Sagbani maa zibigi ya. (The cloud has darkened.)

english: a cloud; rain cloud (plural sagbana) (variant of sagbaŋ)
example: Sagbɔŋ maa kana na. (The rain cloud has come.)
O zaŋ la sagboo n-ʒi kom na. (She used a small calabash bottle to bring water.)

english: to answer/respond (to a call); to agree (to a proposal)/accept (a fact/opinion - not accept a gift); to obey/submit to; to be sated/have enough; to be enough; to fit/be the right or same size; to be adequate/good enough for; to be equal with (with taba); to be about to/on the point of... (auxiliary)
example: O saɣi la o boligu. (He answered his call.)

english: to be about to die; to be on the point of death (lit. 'to be enough to die')
example: O saɣi kpi. (He is about to die.)

english: to become a grown-up; to reach adulthood (lit. 'to be enough as a person')
example: O saɣi nira. (He has become an adult.)
O ti o la saɣibɔrili. (He gave him a pat of t.z.)

english: a person who does not eat t.z. (saɣim + chihi)
example: Pl: saɣichihanima.  Saɣichiha maa bi diri saɣim. (The person who doesn't eat t.z. doesn't eat it.)
O di la saɣidihili. (He ate supper.)

english: a t.z. bowl; porringer; bowl for serving/eating t.z. (saɣim + di + laa)
example: O zaŋ la saɣidirilaa n-ti o saɣim. (She used a t.z. bowl to give him t.z.)

english: a t.z. pot; pot for cooking t.z. (saɣim + duɣu)
example: O zaŋ la saɣiduɣu n-duɣi saɣim. (She used the t.z. pot to cook t.z.)
O di la saɣikurili. (He ate cold leftover t.z.)

english: food taken at night during the month of fasting (saɣim + la?)
example: Bɛ dira saɣila nolɔri ni. (They eat the night food during fasting.)
O kɔ la saɣiliŋga. (He farmed a narrow strip of land.)

english: t.z. (millet or other grain porridge - staple food); food in general (noun)
example: O di la saɣim. (He ate t.z.)

english: to spoil/break down/degenerate; to spoil/damage something; to destroy; to waste something/use up/spend freely
example: O saɣim la o nɛma. (He spoilt his things.)

english: a destroyer; Satan (has come to mean 'Satan' by Christian usage)
example: Saɣinda maa bɔri ni o saɣim bɛhigu. (The destroyer wants to destroy lives.)

english: lower part of the abdomen (plural saɣinsi)
example: O saɣiŋga biɛya. (His lower abdomen hurts.)

english: destruction; spoiling; breaking down (noun from saɣim)
example: Di tahi la saɣiŋgu na. (It brought destruction.)
O va la saɣiri. (He collected the rubbish.)

english: uncomfortable (ideophone, in phrase mali saɣisaɣi 'to cause discomfort')
example: Di mali o la saɣisaɣi. (It makes him uncomfortable.)

english: to agree/conform/be level; to equal/be equivalent/sufficient/correspond to/fit (as clothes); (in phrase saɣisi taba 'be equal/match/fit each other')
example: Bɛ saɣisi la taba. (They agreed with each other.)

english: to rebuke/'advise'/warn; to discipline/chastise; to straighten/set in order/prepare; (in phrase saɣisi miri 'come near')
example: O saɣisi o la. (He warned him.)

english: an extra wedge for a hoe (saɣisi + ga)
example: O zaŋ la saɣisiga n-kpahi kuli maa. (He used an extra wedge for the hoe.)
O ti o la saɣisigu. (He gave him advice.)
O saɣisim la tia maa. (He chopped up the tree.)
O bɔri la saɣisinli. (He wants a moderate amount.)
O di la saɣituliga. (He ate hot t.z.)

english: a stirring-paddle; stick used for stirring t.z. (saɣim + vuɣi)
example: O zaŋ la saɣivuɣili n-vuɣi saɣim. (She used a paddle to stir the t.z.)
Ninvuɣ' shɛb' bɛn nyɛ saguya ŋɔ bi mi shɛli. (Those stupid people don't know anything.)

english: time (specific or duration); (in phrases di saha/dinsaha 'then', lala saha 'at that time', bɔ saha 'when?', saha din ka bahigu 'forever', saha kpeya 'time is far spent', sahakam 'always')
example: Saha maa paai ya. (The time has arrived.)
From: (Hausa) < (Arabic).  O nyɛla n sahabe. (He is my confidant.)
O kana sahakam. (He always comes.)
Zahim maa mali sahi pam. (The fish has many bones.)

english: to whet/sharpen; to comb the hair; to collect fees; (in phrase sahi baa 'to 'sic' a dog')
example: O sahi la o sua. (He sharpened his knife.)

english: a small whirlwind; 'dust devil' (saa + hin? + ʒiɛpiliŋga)
example: Sahinʒiɛpiliŋga maa ʒiɛ taŋkpaɣu. (The dust devil is blowing dust.)

english: a wedge for a hoe (sahi + rigu)
example: O zaŋ la sahirigu n-kpahi kuli maa. (He used a wedge for the hoe.)

english: a costume for a dance called tuubankpuli or baamaya (sain + sain)
example: O lo la sainsain n-ti waa maa. (He wore a costume for the dance.)

english: a frog species (edible, with long legs)
example: Saka maa be kom maa ni. (The frog is in the water.)
Sakabiga maa ŋɛri buɣli maa. (The horsefly is biting the donkey.)
Sakarakara maa mi ya yuŋ maa. (A downpour fell last night.)

english: a beetle species (edible, small, grey, found mainly in reeds)
example: Bihi maa gbahi la sakaricheeŋa. (The children are catching those beetles.)
Sakɔɣu maa ʒi tia zuɣu. (The grasshopper is on the tree.)
Sakɔnooŋa maa be puu ni. (That grasshopper is in the farm.)
O to la sakɔro. (He pounded fufu.)
O nyu la sakom. (He drank rainwater.)
Sakpaleeŋa maa yiɣi la zuɣusaa. (The swallow flew up.)

english: thunderbolt; a hard, sharp stone (saa + kpani)
example: Sakpani maa lu tiŋa. (A thunderbolt struck the ground.)
O sakpuɣili biɛya. (His hip hurts.)
O sakpuɣu ŋmirigi. (The hollow of his thigh was dislocated.)
Sakuɣili maa luri na. (Hailstones are falling.)
Sakumsi maa tahi ya. (It thundered.)
Sakurigu maa mi ya. (Heavy rain fell.)
O zaŋ la sala n-to buɣim. (He used charcoal to make fire.)

english: a human being (plural salinima/salanima); humanity (variant of ninsala)
example: Sala nyɛla Naawuni ni nam shɛli. (Human beings are what God created.)
O kɔ la nyu' sala. (He planted big yams.)

english: to be slippery/sticky/slimy (verb, related to saligi, sarigi)
example: Tiŋgbani maa sala ya. (The ground is slippery.)

english: capital city/town (the seat of kingship of Dagboŋ, currently Yendi)
example: O chaŋ salaa ni. (He went to the capital.)

english: bride price (money paid by a man to the parent of a woman to seal a marriage contract) (from Hausa)
example: O yo la salaachi n-ti paɣa maa. (He paid the bride price.)

english: contemporaries/fellows/peers/fellow human beings (sala + lana + taba) (variant of saalalaantaba)
example: O mini o salalaantaba n-daa be ni. (He and his contemporaries were there.)
Bihi maa diɛmdi la salangbarigbari. (The children are playing tug-of-war.)

english: the late dry-season; 'hungry season'; beginning of farming (variant of salan')
example: Salaŋa saha, bindira bi zooi. (In the hungry season, food is scarce.)

english: first rain at the start of the farming season (salaŋa + saa)
example: Salaŋsaa maa mi ya. (The first rains fell.)
O kɔ la salaŋtuya. (He planted soybeans.)

english: a large piece of potsherd (plural saya)
example: O zaŋ la sali n-tu buɣim. (He used a potsherd to add to the fire.)

english: to be acquainted with; to resemble another person or thing; to linger/'frequent' (verb)
example: O sali la o zo. (He is acquainted with his friend.)

english: a sandal decorated with a tuft of wool
example: From: (English) 'Bata'?.  O piri la salibata. (He put on decorated sandals.)
O zaŋ la salichee n-kɔri o maŋa. (He used a potsherd to scrape himself.)

english: a weevil that destroys grain (plural salisi)
example: Saliga maa diri la kahu. (The weevil is eating the millet.)

english: a private pit latrine for the chief; private cesspit
example: O chaŋ saliga ni. (He went to the private latrine.)
O zaŋ la saligbabirisi n-gbabi sala. (He used tongs to hold the charcoal.)

english: to be acquainted with/used to; to be tame/tame something; to be slippery; to be worn out
example: O saligi la o tuma maa. (He is used to his work.)

english: to flatter/cajole; to tell fables/tales (Eastern dialect); to praise-sing/give a praise-name; to stop (Eastern dialect)
example: O salim o la. (He flattered him.)
Salim maa be kom maa ni. (There is slime in the water.)
O lo la salima. (He bought gold.)
Salimanja maa doli la sapashinnima. (The sergeant-major is following the officers.)
Bɛ ʒiri la salima salimbɔɣili maa ni. (They are mining gold in the gold mine.)
Pl: salindooriba.  Salindoora maa tɔ la salima. (The goldsmith is working gold.)

english: a riddle; riddle-story (salinli + lɔhili) (variant of saliŋlɔha)
example: O bɔhi o la salinlɔhili. (He asked him a riddle.)

english: a fable/parable/folktale (Eastern dialect); a praise-name/proverbial epithet or title (variant of salim)
example: O yɛli la salinli. (He told a folktale.)

english: gold (a piece of gold) (variant of saliŋ)
example: O mali salinli. (He has a piece of gold.)
Salinsaa maa be bindira zuɣu. (The sugar-ant is on the food.)
O kɔ la salintuya. (He planted soybeans.)

english: a herb species (frequently used in broth) (salim + vɔɣu)
example: O zaŋ la salinvɔɣu n-niŋ ʒɛri maa ni. (She put that herb in the soup.)
Pl: salinyɔriba.  Salinyɔra maa nyɔri la sala. (The charcoal-burner is burning charcoal.)
Pl: saliŋgbiriba.  Saliŋgbira maa gbiri la salima tiŋgbani ni. (The gold-miner is digging for gold in the ground.)
O yuuri la saliŋkom. (She likes orange.) Saliŋkom maa nyɛla din nyaɣisa. (The orange color is pleasing.)

english: a charcoal-burner (sala + pi + ra) (variant of salipiraba)
example: Pl: salipiriba.  Salipira maa pi la sala. (The charcoal-burner is burying charcoal.)
Bɛ kari la saliya. (They are judging the case.)
Tiŋgbani maa nyɛla salli. (The ground is level.)

english: a crowd of persons; people (en masse)
example: Salo pam laɣim ya. (A large crowd gathered.)
O sam la tua wala. (He mashed baobab fruit.)

english: a summons fee; summons (noun, from English 'summons')
example: O yo la sam. (He paid the summons fee.)

english: to issue a summons against/accuse at law/sue (verb)
example: O sam o la kootu ni. (He sued him in court.)

english: soft and sticky (ideophone, in phrase niŋ sam 'to stick firmly to')
example: Di niŋ sam. (It stuck firmly.)

english: aluminium; glaze; a fool (from Hausa, with folk-etymology)
example: Bɛ zaŋ la samafuu n-mɛ li. (They used aluminium to make it.)

english: a twin whose parent is also a twin (saman? + jaa)
example: Samanjaa maa nyɛla jaa. (The twin whose parent is a twin is a twin.)

english: a sanitary worker; health inspector (from samasama, originally pejorative)
example: Samasama maa lihiri la tiŋa maa sabita. (The health inspector checks the town's cleanliness.)

english: muddy; sticky (ideophone, in phrase niŋ samasama 'be muddy/sticky')
example: Kom maa niŋ samasama. (The water became muddy.)

english: a servant in a chief's house; a habitué of a house/trusted visitor (sambani + bia)
example: Pl: sambanbihi.  Sambanbia maa tumdi la nayili ni. (The palace servant is working.)

english: outside of a compound (area around outside, especially the 'front yard'); overseas; (in phrases samban paɣa/doo 'lover outside marriage') (plural sambana)
example: O ʒe la sambani. (He is standing in the front yard.)

english: the public chant of oral history of Dagbɔŋ and the Yaan-Nas (sambani + luŋa)
example: Bɛ ŋmɛ la sambanluŋa. (They performed the historical chant.)
Sambarigu maa be tiŋgbani ni. (The earthworm is in the ground.)
Sambarishee maa neeri la yuŋ. (The glow-worm lights up at night.)

english: a charm type (buried in the ground for harming persons) (in phrase sɔŋ sambu 'set a charm')
example: O sɔŋ la sambu. (He set a ground-charm.)

english: colostrum (the first fluid produced by the mother before normal milk comes in) (synonym of bihidɔsim)
example: Bia maa nyu la samla. (The baby drank colostrum.)
Pl: samlanima.  Samlana maa bɔri la o liɣiri. (The creditor wants his money.)
O mali samli. (He has a debt.)

english: a lean-to (gbala-mat shelter attached to a house); a dais/platform (saa? + mpaa?)
example: Bɛ ʒinila sampaa gbini. (They are sitting under the lean-to.)

english: a floor-beater (object used by women for beating down gravel on floors or crushing millet)
example: O zaŋ la sampani n-ŋmɛ ziɣa. (She used a floor-beater to beat the gravel.)

english: a tree species (hardy tree, roots used as medicine)
example: Bɛ zaŋ la sampeeŋa vari n-tibigi doro. (They use the leaves of that tree to treat sickness.)

english: threepence'; 'threepenny piece' (obsolete unit of currency: . pesewas) (plural sampɔhi) (variant of sampua)
example: O yo la sampoa. (He paid threepence.)
Sana maa kana ti sani. (The visitor came to us.)

english: a cloth type (high-quality cloth used for gowns and baggy trousers)
example: O lo la sanda chinchini. (He bought a sanda cloth.)

english: intercession or apology on behalf of another; a customary request of a man who elopes with a girl to her parents for their consent to marry her; (in phrases niŋ sandaani 'to beg for pardon', tim sandaani 'to send to beg a pardon/to ask for a girl's hand in marriage')
example: O tim la sandaani n-ti o. (He sent an apology to him.)

english: one who pleads or apologises for another; a go-between/intercessor/mediator (sandaani + timda)
example: Pl: sandaantimdanima.  Sandaantimda maa sɔŋ ba m-maani yɛltɔɣa. (The mediator helped them settle the matter.)
Pl: sandaanima.  Sandana maa bɔri la o liɣiri. (The creditor wants his money.)
Pl: sandihiranima.  Sandihira maa paŋ o la liɣiri. (The money-lender lent him money.)
Sandikuli maa mali gɔhi pam. (That grass has many thorns.)
Pl: sandiriba.  Sandira maa bi tooi yo o samli. (The debtor can't pay his debt.)

english: an important task; a pressing responsibility; an urgent problem/'intervening matter/unexpected event/mishap' (saa? + ngali?)
example: Di nyɛla sangali titali. (It is an important task.)

english: a yam cultivar (Western dialect) (variant of sangalnjo)
example: O puu ni sangalinjo maa di pam. (The sangalinjo yams in his farm yielded a lot.)
O sani la o nia. (He touched his hand.)

english: 'side'/location of (used with persons or animals, not usually made object of verb of motion)
example: O chaŋ o ba sani. (He went to his father.)

english: with someone; to/from someone; in/to/from the presence of someone; in the sight/opinion of someone (sani + ni)
example: O be o zo sani. (He is with his friend.)
O zahim li la saniya zuɣu. (He weighed it on the scales.)
Bia maa mali sanjaɣa. (The child has chickenpox.)
O mali sankaŋ. (He has an earache.)

english: a plant species (which bears round fruit with small balls which children use in a game)
example: Bihi maa diɛmdi la sankaŋli wala. (The children are playing with the fruits of that plant.)
Sankuriginli maa be mɔɣu ni. (That snake is in the bush.)

english: the atmospheric change brought by a rain (saa + noli?)
example: Sanoli maa kana na. (The rain's atmospheric change has come.)

english: a military camp (plural sansana) (variant of sanbani)
example: Bɛ ʒinila sansani ni. (They are in the military camp.)
From: (English).  O guli la santili. (He is on sentry duty.)
O ti o la sanyoo. (He gave him compensation.)
Sanzali maa gbaai tiŋa ŋɔ. (Drought has caught this land.)

english: a bean species (usually reddish-brown in colour)
example: O kɔ la sanʒi. (He planted that type of beans.)

english: a dust storm preceding a line-squall; storm (plural sanʒiɛri) (variant of saʒiɛɣu)
example: Sanʒiɛɣu maa ʒɛ taŋkpaɣu. (The dust storm is blowing dust.)

english: a plain swamp without trees (plural saŋa)
example: Saŋ maa be kom ni. (The swamp is in the water.)

english: an accidental mishap; a circumstance (variant of saŋgaya)
example: Sarati maa niŋ la saŋgali zuɣu. (The accident happened due to a mishap.)

english: a worm in kola nuts; an intruder
example: Saŋgarili maa be guli ni. (The worm is in the kola nut.)
Saŋgundili maa be mɔɣu ni. (The royal python is in the bush.)

english: an antelope species; bushbuck/harnessed antelope (Tragelaphus scriptus) (Eastern dialect)
example: Saŋgunli maa be mɔɣu ni. (The bushbuck is in the bush.)
Bihi maa diɛmdi la saŋkɔɣuchee. (The children are playing that game.)
Saŋkpalinnyaŋ maa dɔɣi la bia. (The female bushbuck gave birth to a calf.)

english: an antelope species; bushbuck (Tragelaphus scriptus) (variant of sankpaluŋ)
example: Saŋkpaliŋ maa be mɔɣu ni. (The bushbuck is in the bush.)
Saŋkpaliŋdibga maa mali yaa. (The young male bushbuck is strong.)
O mali saŋkpangoo o nia ni. (He has a whitlow on his hand.)

english: an anthrax boil or cow boil; craw-craw/itch/scabies (plural saŋkpana)
example: O mali saŋkpani. (He has an anthrax boil.)
O mali saŋkpani. (He has scabies.)

english: a tree species (the roots of which are used as a remedy for craw-craw or scabies) (saŋkpani + tɛɣu?)
example: Bɛ zaŋ la saŋkpantɛɣu n-tibigi saŋkpani. (They use that tree to treat craw-craw.)

english: asthma in a mild form; bronchitis (saŋkpaŋa + nyɔɣu?)
example: O mali saŋkpanyɔɣu. (He has mild asthma.)
O mali saŋkpaŋa. (He has neuralgia.)
Saŋkuriginli maa be mɔɣu ni. (The royal python is in the bush.)

english: a span (space between two points or objects) (plural saŋa)
example: Di waɣilim nyɛla saŋli. (Its length is a span.)

english: a nut (type); a children's game (played in a circle with nuts) (plural saŋa)
example: Bihi maa diɛmdi la saŋli. (The children are playing that nut game.)
Saŋmaribaŋda maa lihiri la saŋmarisi. (The astronomer looks at the stars.)
Saŋmariga maa neeri la yuŋ. (The star shines at night.)

english: a spitting-cobra; a frog with different colours (plural sapari) (variant of sapaɣa)
example: Sapaɣu maa be mɔɣu ni. (The spitting-cobra is in the bush.)
Sapasinbihi maa tumdi tuma. (The soldiers are working.)

english: an officer/sergeant/captain of the Dagomba Army (plural sapashin') (variant of sapashini, from English 'sergeant')
example: Sapasini maa doli la tɔbbihi. (The officer leads the soldiers.)
O mali sapibo. (He has piles.)
Sapili maa kumdi la yuŋ. (The hyena howls at night.)

english: the side of the body (plural sapira)
example: O sapirigu biɛya. (The side of his body hurts.)
O mali sapirigu. (He has pneumonia.)
O sapirikɔbili piri ya. (His rib is broken.)

english: a bird species (possibly quail) (in phrase Manna mini Sapiripiya 'Manna and Quail')
example: Bɛ di la sapiripili. (They ate that bird.)
O sapuɣu biɛya. (His lung hurts.)

english: tobacco (contracted form of nansara); snuff/powdered tobacco (synonym of taba)
example: O nyuri la sara. (He takes snuff.)

english: alms (given in the name of God, usually in Moslem worship); sacrifice (action or sacrificed animal and its meat); funeral offering/s/funeral
example: O ti la sara. (He gave alms.)

english: money offering without meat; funeral rites/gifts not including an animal (sara + 'kuuni) (variant of sarikuma)
example: Bɛ zaŋ la sarakuma n-ti kuli maa. (They gave a money offering for the funeral.)
Bɛ zaŋ la saramaha n-ti sara maa. (They gave a meat offering for the sacrifice.)

english: the last funeral rites of a deceased (sara + tari + bu)
example: Bɛ niŋ la sarataribu n-ti o. (They performed the last funeral rites for him.)
Sarati paagi o. (An accident happened to him.)

english: judgement; legal 'case' (variant of sariya); (in phrase sariasaria 'in an organised fashion') (from Hausa < Arabic)
example: Bɛ kari la saria. (They judged the case.)
O ʒini la o sariadibo ʒiishee. (He sat on the judgment seat.)

english: judgement; the act/process of judging a case (saria + kari + bo) (variant of sariakaribu)
example: Sariakaribo maa niŋ ya. (The judgement was given.)

english: a judge (saria + kari + ta) (variant of sariyakaria)
example: Sariakarita maa kari la saria. (The judge is judging the case.)

english: in an organised fashion; tidily/in order/arranged well (reduplication of saria)
example: Bɛ zaŋ li sɔŋ sariasaria. (They put it down in an orderly fashion.)
O zaŋ la sariga n-pili o ninni. (She used a veil to cover her face.)

english: to slide/slip; to be slippery; (in phrase nyɛ sarigibu 'be accidental')
example: O sarigi la n-lu. (He slipped and fell.)

english: a sliding/slipping; a slippery place (sarigi + nli)
example: Di nyɛla sariginli. (It is a slippery place.)

english: prison; an expensive gold necklace (from Hausa)
example: Bɛ zaŋ o niŋ sarika ni. (They put him in prison.)
Pl: sarikakpɛriba.  Sarikakpɛra maa zo n-yi. (The prisoner escaped.)
O mini o sarikakpɛritaba n-daa be sarika ni. (He and his fellow prisoners were in prison.)

english: to be better; to be preferable (verb); (in phrase di so ni... 'it is better than')
example: Di so ni a chaŋ. (It is better that you go.)

english: to rub; to grate (as nuts or yams)
example: O so la sima. (He grated the groundnuts.)

english: a/an/a certain/someone (adjective, mostly used in relative constructions) (plural 'shɛba/'shɛbi)
example: Do' so daa be ni. (A certain man was there.)

english: somebody/anybody (animate sg. indefinite); a person; the one who(m) (in relative constructions) (plural shɛba)
example: So kana. (Somebody came.)
From: (English).  O so la tia maa. (He sawed the tree.)
Sokam kana. (Everyone came.)

english: a road/highway/main path (plural solɔri) (variant of solari)
example: Bɛ mɛ la solɔɣu palli. (They built a new road.)

english: to put underwear on someone/'gird' with... (causative of so)
example: O soli la o bia. (He put underwear on his child.)

english: a road/path; way of doing something/way to do something; way of life; the right way/approved behaviour; opportunity/permission
example: O doli la soli. (He followed the path.)
Bihi maa diɛmdi la solo. (The children are playing on a swing.)
O som maa biɛya. (His gall bladder hurts.)
Somanji maa tumdi la tuma. (The servant is working.)
O be la somanji ni. (He is in servitude.)
O zaŋ la somaŋli n-kali duu. (She used a local broom to sweep the room.)

english: Somo (title of an elder responsible for the conduct of the first stage of the Damba festival)
example: Somo maa be nayili ni. (The Somo elder is at the palace.)

english: the first Damba dance (eleventh of the month) (Somo + Damba)
example: Bɛ diɛmdi la Somo Damba. (They are dancing the Somo Damba.)

english: painful menstruation/dysmenorrhoea; pains in the abdomen; a disease (of women) (som + puɣili?)
example: O mali sompuɣili. (She has dysmenorrhoea.)
Soo maa be kom maa ni. (The frog is in the water.)
From: (English).  O zaŋ la soobuli n-gbi tiŋa. (He used a shovel to dig the ground.)
O tum la sooche. (He trades in foodstuffs.)
Soochɛlana maa kɔhiri la bindira. (The trader sells foodstuffs.)

english: an improvement in anything; recovery (particularly in sickness); a reasonable price
example: From: (Hausa).  O doro maa nya soochi. (His sickness has improved.)

english: to ungird; to remove garment around the waist (cpart of so)
example: O sooi la o kpalaŋa. (He took off his trousers.)
O sooi la leemu maa. (He squeezed the orange.)
Sooje maa guli la tiŋa. (The soldier is guarding the town.)
Soojɛbila maa bɔhimdi la soojɛ tuma. (The cadet is learning soldier's work.)

english: a hare/'rabbit' (Lepus capensis zechi or Lepus togolensis)
example: Sooŋa maa zo n-kpe mɔɣu ni. (The hare ran into the bush.)
From: (English).  O ye la sooti. (He wore a shirt.)

english: a teacher of the way; instructor (in moral matters) (soli + wuhi + ra)
example: Sowuhira maa wuhiri ba la soli. (The instructor shows them the way.)
O chaŋ Speen. (He went to Spain.)
Bɛ zaŋ la stakti n-niŋ tulaale maa ni. (They put stacte in the incense.)

english: to bath/have/take a bath; to bathe (transitive, e.g. bathe a baby); (in phrases su noli 'to eat/take breakfast', su X kom (ko' suŋ) 'to baptise X')
example: O su la kom. (He bathed.)

english: to pocket something/put something in one's pocket; to put into a bag/sheath/enclosed area
example: O su la liɣiri maa o paachi ni. (He pocketed the money.)

english: to possess/get possession of; to own; to rule over/have authority over; to be responsible for; (in phrases su maŋa 'be free/independent', su fukumsi 'be in charge of/responsible for', su gbini 'be singleminded/concentrate')
example: O su la liɣiri pam. (He possesses a lot of money.)
O zaŋ la sua n-ŋmaai bindirigu. (He used a knife to cut the food.)
O zaŋ la subila n-ŋmaai bindirigu. (He used a small knife to cut the food.)
O deei la subusuŋ. (He was baptized.)
O zaŋ la suchɛriga n-che mɔri. (He used a cutlass to cut the grass.)

english: to dip (something in a liquid); (in phrase suɣi yaazi 'to feel happy/have a feeling of satisfaction')
example: O suɣi la bihi maa kom ni. (He dipped the stick in the water.)

english: patience; the having of patience (suɣilo + di + bo) (variant of suɣilodibo)
example: O suɣilɔdibo n-daa chɛ ka o di nasara. (His patience made him win.)

english: a temporary grass hut; a tent/canopy/booth (even if roofed with metal or grass); (in phrases biɛhigu shee suɣili 'Tabernacle', ti suɣili 'erect a tent/canopy/shelter', tanti suɣili 'a tent')
example: Bɛ ti la suɣili. (They erected a tent.)

english: to gargle/rinse out the mouth; to flush (with water) (variant of suɣiri); (in phrase suɣili noli 'to break one's fast')
example: O suɣili la o noli. (He rinsed his mouth.)

english: patience (from Arabic) (variant of suɣulo, suɣiro); (in phrases di / mali / niŋ suɣilo 'to have patience')
example: O mali suɣilo. (He has patience.)
Suɣilolana ŋɔ bi je suli. (This patient person doesn't get angry.)

english: to push/force through (push a stick in and out of a hole, walk with effort through grass)
example: O suɣiri la mɔɣu ni. (He is forcing his way through the grass.)

english: to beg/entreat; to ask for/request; to pray for/supplicate; (in phrases deei suhi 'intercede for', suhi soli 'ask for permission to leave/say goodbye', suhi noli 'ask for permission to speak', suhi alibarika (niŋ X) 'to bless (X)')
example: O suhi o la. (He begged him.)

english: to draw in breath through the teeth
example: O suhi la. (He drew in his breath through his teeth.)
O niŋ la suhigu. (He offered a prayer.)
O yum maa ni suhiri yirina. (Pus is coming from his sore.)
Suhpiɛllilana ŋɔ lara pam. (This happy person laughs a lot.)

english: heart (physical organ, but usually seat of mind and emotions) (plural suhiri) (variant of suhuri); (used in many idiomatic expressions)
example: O suhu biɛya. (His heart hurts.)

english: to become angry with someone (lit. 'the heart releases on someone')
example: O suhu bahi o zuɣu. (He became angry with him.)
O suhu biɛra n-ti o. (He feels sympathy for him.)

english: to be afraid; to be conscious of guilt (lit. 'the heart is disturbed')
example: O suhu chihi. (He is afraid.)

english: to be disturbed; to be frightened (lit. 'the heart shakes')
example: O suhu dam. (He is disturbed.)

english: to be at peace with the world (lit. 'the heart lies in one place')
example: O suhu dɔni luɣ' yini. (He is at peace.)
O suhu gari o. (He is sorry for him.)

english: to be courageous; to be fortified mentally (lit. 'the heart strengthens')
example: O suhu kpaŋ. (He is courageous.)

english: to be interested in; to be peaceful in mind (lit. 'the heart falls')
example: O suhu lu li ni. (He is interested in it.)

english: to be comforted; to be appeased (lit. 'the heart cools')
example: O suhu maai ya. (He is comforted.)

english: to be afraid; to be frightened (lit. 'the heart weakens')
example: O suhu ŋooi. (He is afraid.)

english: to be delighted; to be pleased (lit. 'the heart whitens')
example: O suhu paligi ya. (He is delighted.)

english: to be happy (lit. 'the heart is white')
example: O suhu piɛla. (He is happy.)
O suhu saɣim ya. (He is sad.)

english: to be kind; to be cheerful (lit. 'the heart is good')
example: O suhu viɛla. (He is kind.)
O suhu yiɣisi. (He became angry.)

english: to calm one's anger (lit. 'to kill the quick-temperedness')
example: O ku o suhu yiɣisili. (He calmed his anger.)

english: to be sincere; to be single-minded (lit. 'one heart')
example: O mali suhu yini. (He is sincere.)

english: to meditate (lit. 'to put the heart on the ground')
example: O zaŋ o suhu sɔŋ tiŋa. (He is meditating.)

english: to have doubts; to fear (lit. 'the heart runs')
example: O suhu zɔhi. (He has doubts.)
Suhubɔhibo gbaagi o. (He is heavy-hearted.)
Suhuchihibo gbaagi o. (Dread caught him.)

english: a disturbed mind; disturbance of the mind; disquiet (suhu + dam + li)
example: Suhudamli be o ni. (He has a disturbed mind.)

english: peace (suhu + do); (in phrase suhudoo sara maligu 'peace-offering')
example: Suhudoo be tiŋa ŋɔ ni. (There is peace in this town.)
Suhudoolana ŋɔ bi bɔri zabili. (This peaceful person doesn't seek trouble.)
Suhufabu gbaagi o. (Forgetfulness caught him.)
Suhufuŋbo be o ni. (He has mental confusion.)
Suhugarigu gbaagi o. (Sorrow caught him.)
Suhukabili gbaagi o. (Heartbreak caught him.)
O ti o la suhukpaŋsibo. (He gave him encouragement.)
Suhukpeendana maa tuhi la dimba. (The brave person fought the enemies.)
O mali suhukpeeni. (He has courage.)
Suhukpiŋ gbaagi o. (Forgetfulness caught him.)
O ti o la suhumahili. (He gave him comfort.)
Suhuŋooli gbaagi o. (Fear caught him.)
Suhupiɛlim be o ni. (Joy is in him.)
Suhupiɛlli be o ni. (Joy is in him.)
Suhusaɣiŋgu gbaagi o. (Sadness caught him.)
Suhutunanima maa bi tooi baŋ yɛla. (Slow-witted people can't understand things.)
O suhutuntali n-daa chɛ ka o bi baŋ. (His slow-wittedness made him not understand.)
Suhutuŋ gbaagi o. (Forgetfulness caught him.)
Suhutuŋlana ŋɔ bi teeri yɛla. (This slow-witted person doesn't remember things.)

english: hot-temperedness; anger (suhu + yiɣisi + li) (variant of suhuyaɣisili)
example: Suhuyiɣisili gbaagi o. (Anger caught him.)
Suhuyiɣisililana ŋɔ je suli yom-yom. (This hot-tempered person gets angry quickly.)
O mali suhuyini. (He is sincere.)
Suhuyoli be o ni. (He has ill-temper.)
O doli la o suhuyubu. (He follows his own will.)
O tum li la suhuyurilim ni. (He did it by whim.)

english: dread; terror (suhu + zɔhi + gu) (variant of suhuzɔhibo)
example: Suhuzɔhigu gbaagi o. (Terror caught him.)
Suhuʒia be o ni. (He is at peace.)
Wɔri maa dira suiin. (The horses are eating that grass.)
Sujee gbaagi o. (Anger caught him.)
O sua sukpali piri ya. (His knife's handle is broken.)
Suli gbaagi o. (Anger caught him.)
Suligbɔŋ maa ʒi tia zuɣu. (The crested hawk is on the tree.)

english: a hawk; raptor (any medium-sized bird of prey) (variant of sulugu, sulige, suliga)
example: Suligu maa pɔbi na n-ti to nɔbila. (The hawk swooped and struck the chick.)
Suligunyinyaɣu maa zooi puu ni. (That weed grows in the farm.)

english: ownership/rule/sphere of authority; a possession/sb.'s property; slave/descendant of a slave (su + linsi)
example: Di be la o sulinsi ni. (It is under his authority.)

english: a serval (Leptailurus serval) (sulinsi? + piɛlli) (variant of sulipiɛliga)
example: Sulipiɛlli maa be mɔɣu ni. (The serval is in the bush.)

english: shilling' (0 pesewas, obsolete unit of currency) (from English)
example: O yo la sulli. (He paid a shilling.)
O zaŋ la suluŋ n-sarisiri duɣu maa. (She used a sponge to polish the pot.)
Tia maa sum yirina. (The tree's sap is coming out.)
O bɔri la sumaŋa. (He wants independence.)
O zaŋ la sunɔdali n-tuɣi kuriti maa. (He used solder to join the metals.)
Di be la sunsuu. (It is in the middle.)
O be la bɛ sunsuuni. (He is among them.)
O zaŋ la sunyina n-so tia. (He used a saw to saw the tree.)
O nyɛla ninvuɣ' suŋ. (He is a good person.)
O suŋtali n-daa nyɛ li. (It was his goodness.)

english: to slip out of place/become entangled (as a skein of thread); to remove from a container with a small opening (cpart of su)
example: Mia maa surigi ya. (The rope has become entangled.)

english: to be dark (night time); to fell/cause to fall (possibly two separate lexemes)
example: Yuŋ surigi ya. (The night has fallen.)
O zaŋ la sua maa niŋ o surigu ni. (He put the knife in its sheath.)
O yɛli li surim. (He said it quietly.)

english: a credit union; tontine (contribution of money by a group of people which is given to them one at a time) (from Twi?)
example: Bɛ niŋ la susu. (They formed a susu.)
O zaŋ la sua maa niŋ sutɔɣu ni. (He put the sword in its scabbard.)

english: a charm giving the power making one impervious to knife cuts (sua + ti + li)
example: O bo la sutili. (He got a knife-proof charm.)
O zaŋ la sutitali n-che mɔri. (He used a cutlass to cut the grass.)
Suugariglana ŋɔ kumda. (This sorrowful person is crying.)

english: to rinse laundry; to bathe a dead body; (in phrase suui noli 'break one's fast/take breakfast')
example: O suui la o nɛma. (She rinsed her clothes.)

english: to unload someone; to lift down; to shed leaves/'die in large numbers' (of persons, animals or birds) (cpart ʒi)
example: O suui la nɛma maa. (He unloaded the goods.)

english: (Muslim) naming ceremony/'outdooring'; Muslim prayer which is optional/anything which is not compulsory (from Hausa < Arabic) (variant of sunna)
example: Bɛ niŋ la suuna n-ti bia maa. (They performed the naming ceremony for the child.)

english: a chapter (of a book, esp. Bible and Qur'an) (from Hausa < Arabic) (variant of suuri)
example: Bɛ karim la suura maa. (They read the chapter.)

english: deep blue; indigo (dye) (synonym of nuɣiso, shim) (from Hausa)
example: O zaŋ la suuri n-niŋ chinchini maa ni. (He used indigo dye on the cloth.)

english: disaster (in phrases nya suuta 'to have a disaster', mali suuta 'to be disastrous')
example: Suuta paagi o. (A disaster happened to him.)
O kana ta. He came exactly (on time).

english: to plaster; to put on (throw at) something not liquid but sticky
example: O ta la o duri. He plastered his rooms.

english: invitation to a child to stand up and walk
example: Taa! yiɣisim zani! Hey! Get up and walk!
O nyɛla n yidantaa. He is my first husband.
O nyɛla n taa. He is my peer.
Bɛ kɔhi la taabɔdari. (They sell timber.)
Bɛ zaŋ la taabo n-me yili. They used timber to build the house.

english: disturbance; lighting a fire (variant of taai)
example: Bɛ taabu maa wumya. The disturbance was heard.
O nyɛla taabushe. He is a rascal.
O tuma nyɛla taabushetali. His actions are rascally.

english: eulogising of commoners (by drummers); a dance for commoners
example: Lunsi maa yi la taachi. The drummers played the praise-rhythm.
Di nyɛla ti taada. It is our custom.
O taai o la naba ni. He trampled him with his foot.

english: to light a fire; to set fire to; to catch fire; to be ablaze; to burn; to be infuriated; to be sharp; to be good (of life)
example: O taai la buɣim. He lit a fire.

english: he is to blame (lit. guilt sits on his head)
example: Taali ʒi a zuɣu ni a niŋ shɛli maa. (You are to blame for what you did.)
Bihi maa zaŋ la taaya n-to binshɛɣu. The children used a slingshot to shoot something.
Ti mini taba m-bɛ ni. We are here together.
O nyuri la taba. He smokes tobacco.

english: well done!; jolly good show!; way to go!
example: Tabaarikalla! a tuma viɛla. Well done! Your work is good.
Bɛ pe la tababiri. They blew a fanfare.
O nyuri la tabaduɣu. He smokes a pipe.
O pe o la tabali. He provoked him.
Di niŋ la tabata. It is certain.
Bimbila ŋɔ nyɛla tabataba. This substance is sticky.

english: small kola nuts (with weevils); gummy; sticky
example: O kɔhi la tabataba. He sells small kola nuts.
O yɛltɔɣa nyɛla tabataba. His words are untrue.

english: to carry on or cover with the palm of the hand
example: O tabi la o noli. He covered his mouth with his hand.
Bɛ tabi la shiri maa. They harvested the honey.
Wɔhu maa tabi o. The horse kicked him.
O tabi la gooni maa. He leaned against the wall.
Noo maa tabi la tia zuɣu. The bird perched on the tree.
Tabibi maa dim o. The wasp stung him.
O malila tabibi. He has magic power.

english: to come unstuck; to unstick something
example: O tabigi la bintabindili maa. He unsealed the sticky thing.

english: to kick (esp. backwards, like a horse)
example: Wɔhu maa tabigi o. (The horse kicked him.)
Tabikɔhira maa kɔhiri la taba. The tobacco seller is selling tobacco.
Tabilakpia maa dim o. The wasp stung him.
Tabilalaa maa ʒi la o nini ni. A sweat-fly is on his eye.
Tabilangooŋa maa bela kɔbi ni. The beetle is in the grain.
Tabilankpia maa mɛ la o tɛɣu. The hornet built its nest.
Tabilɔɣu maa gbaai la naa. The tick caught the cow.

english: to stick to; to fasten to; to lean against; to be adjacent to; to extend as far as
example: Bintabindili maa tabi la litaafi maa. The seal stuck to the book.
O da la tabili. He bought tobacco.
Di nyɛla bin tabinli. It is a sticky thing.
O nyɛla n tabira. He is my supporter.

english: reading and interpretation of the Qur'an
example: O karim la tabishiiri. He recites the Qur'an.

english: to pilfer; to steal a little bit
example: O tabisiri la liɣiri. He is pilfering money.
Di tabisi li. Don't touch it.
O du la tabitabi. He climbed the stairs.
Di nyɛla tabizahim. It is a filthy act.
O nyɛla tabizahimlana. He is a filthy person.
Di malila tabo pam. It has many kinds.
O sabi la tadabo ni. He wrote with ink.
O zaŋ la tafarinom niŋ ʒɛri ni. She put garlic in the soup.
Di kpalim la tafirili. A tiny bit remains.

english: to change; to alter; to exchange
example: O taɣi la o nɛma. He changed his clothes.

english: to smell; to sniff; to snuff (animal scents)
example: Baa maa taɣi la ʒiɛɣu. (The dog caught a scent.)

english: to stop someone/something; to protect; to ward off; to drive herd; to prop up; to direct (liquid)
example: O taɣi la o biŋkɔbiri maa. He drove the animals back.
Di lu la taɣilataɣila. It fell in lumps.

english: to be attached (physically or in affection); to agree; to be consistent
example: Bɛ taɣi la taba. They are attached to each other.

english: to go round the edge; to fringe
example: O taɣi la o nɛma maa. He trimmed the edge of his clothes.
Naawuni taɣi o. God protected him.
Bɛ nɛma taɣilima niŋ la viɛlim. (The fringes of their clothes are beautiful.)
O taɣim la o nina. He smacked his lips.

english: grass bedding for a horse; litter
example: Bɛ zaŋ la taɣima n-ti wɔhu maa. They gave litter to the horse.
Di ka taɣinamsi. It has no shape.
O taɣinda maa wumiya. His lip-smacking was heard.
Taɣinda maa vɛla. The rubbing is good.
O taɣinda maa saɣiya. His trimming is finished.
O taɣinti la binmaɣili maa. She ground the millet in the basin.
Taɣibu maa niŋ ya. The changing happened.

english: to rub gently; to pulp something
example: O taɣiri la o nina. He is rubbing his eyes.

english: to arrange; to dab; to absorb
example: O taɣisi la o dari maa. He arranged the firewood.
Bɛ mali taɣitaɣi n-ti naɣi maa. (They have fodder for the cow.)

english: to be in worse circumstance; penniless
example: O nyɛla taɣitaɣi. He is penniless.
O kpuɣi la taɣizii chaŋ daa ni. He took a taxi to the market.
O kɔhi la taha. He sells brass bowls.

english: Muslim night prayers in late Ramadhan
example: O puhi la tahajud. He prayed the Tahajjud.
O zaŋ la tahali n-ʒi kom. He used a brass pan to carry water.

english: to lead; to take someone with you; to bring about; to cause
example: O tahi o bihi shikuru. He took his children to school.

english: to shout; to make a noise; to thunder; to burst; to crack
example: Bɛ tahi la vuri pam. They made a lot of noise.
O malila tahibi. He has a rosary.
Tahiga maa nyari o. The mouth sores are hurting him.

english: brass pan; enamel pan; iron sheet; tin; brass (the metal)
example: O da la tahili palli. She bought a new enamel pan.
O malila tahima. He has hope.
Bɛ zaŋ la tahiŋga n-fa o. They used a spell on him.
O zaŋ la tahipɔŋ n-ʒi bindirigu. He used a tray to carry the food.
O kɔhi la tahiʒiɛri. He sells copper pans.
O tuma nyɛla tajaɣiri. His work is clumsy.
O sabi la takada. He wrote a letter.
Takahi maa paagi o. Disappointment befell him.
Naɣi maa takarifeeni ku tooi chaŋ. The cow's hoof cannot walk.

english: decorative hood for a horse; caparison
example: Bɛ zaŋ la takarisabilo n-ti wɔhu maa. They put a caparison on the horse.
O to la takati maa. He shot the target.
Takatibinima maa buɣisi ba la. The diviners divined for them.
Takɔbchɛra maa tuhiri la tɔbu. The swordsman is fighting.
O malila takɔbi. He has a sword.
Takɔbinyaɣira maa guhi la o maŋa. The swordsman defended himself.
Di daa nyɛla takɔfa. It was sixpence.
O pɔb la takɔrɔbila maa. He closed the window-shutter.

english: window; title of a Dagomba army officer
example: O yooi la takɔro maa. He opened the window.
Bihi maa bɔri la takparikpaɣsi. The children want sweets.
O ti o la takubsi. He gave him a dash.
Di nyɛla talahi. It is necessary.

english: to be multiple; to be prosperous; to be abundant
example: O bindirigu tali o yiŋa. His food is abundant in his house.
O daadamtali viɛla. His humanity is good.

english: spur of fowls or game birds
example: Kpaɣi maa malila taligu. The guinea fowl has a spur.
O tuma nyɛla taligu. His work is ceaseless.

english: fontanelle; medicine for a soft spot on the head
example: Bia maa taligu bi na bi kpɛm. The baby's fontanelle is not yet hard.

english: to ask someone to carry a message; to 'message' someone; to order through another person
example: O talim o la yɛltɔɣa n-ti o ba. He sent a message to his father.
O talim ni o kpi. He is struggling in death.
Bɛ zaŋ o bia m-pa talima. They pledged their child.
O talisi tiŋa. He lay flat on the ground.
O chaŋ la talla ni. He went hawking.

english: message; errand (sent with someone going that way)
example: O tim o la taluŋ. He sent him on an errand.
Puuni maa tam viɛla. (The soil in the farm is good.)
O kana ni tam biɛla. He came after a short time.

english: to be upright on something; to perch; to set upright
example: Noo maa tam la tia zuɣu. The bird perched on the tree.
O tam la kom maa. She boiled the water.
O tam la o yuli. He forgot his name.
O malila tamaha ni o kana. He has hope that he will come.
O be la Tamale. He is in Tamale.

english: a species of ant; a species of hot pepper
example: Tamalichiriga maa dim o. The ant bit him.
Bɛ biri la tamarisik tia. (They planted a tamarisk tree.)
Tambaɣiŋsi maa yi la voli ni. The ants came out of the hole.
Bɛ gbaai la tambiɛɣikpani maa. (They broke the termite-heap.)
Tambiɛɣu maa diri la dari. The termites are eating the wood.

english: small brownish termite (slightly poisonous for chickens)
